covered by convenience store number 4 is 5.Now, suppose that 7-Eleven is considering the coordinates (1, 3) and (5, 3) as potential locations for a new store.If you set up a store at coordinates (1, 3), the number of blocks you can cover is 3 (Figure 4).On the other hand, if you set up a store at coordinates (5, 3), the number of blocks that can be covered is 4 (Figure 5).Therefore, the maximum number of blocks that can be covered is 4.Create a program that takes as input map information m × n, the number of existing convenience stores s and their coordinates (x, y), and the number of candidate locations t and their coordinates (p, q), and outputs the number of blocks that can cover the widest area among all candidate locations.

At Byakko Transport, we focused on the fact that trucks loaded with heavy loads require a lot of energy to start running.The idea was that gasoline could be saved by taking the shortest route a truck could take to reach its destination without stopping once after leaving the warehouse.Your job is to develop a car navigation system that can calculate these shortest routes.The specifications of the car navigation program to be created are as follows.Create a program that takes as input road information in the city, the time required for a truck to travel between intersections, intersections with traffic lights and the frequency of each traffic light, roads under construction, congested roads and congestion levels, and the locations of Byakko Transport's warehouse (starting point) and destination (destination point), and outputs the shortest time from the starting point to the ending point.As shown in the diagram, roads in the east-west direction are named with lowercase letters such as a, b, c, ..., and roads in the north-south direction are named with integers such as 1, 2, 3, ....Intersections within the city are designated by these lowercase letters and integer combinations H - V.For example, the northwestmost intersection in the city is designated by a-1.Each signal has period k and switches every k minutes.If east and west are blue, north and south are red, and if north and south are blue, east and west are red.There are no yellow signals.A truck takes D minutes to travel on the road between two intersections, and an additional d minutes if the road is congested.Trucks cannot move if the road is under construction.Also, if the traffic light is red when you arrive at the intersection, you will not be able to enter.Trucks can only turn east, west, south, or north at intersections, but not in the direction they came from (U-turn).The road is a two-way street, and the time it takes for trucks to come and go, construction status, and traffic congestion are the same for both directions.Initially, the truck is facing east, and all traffic lights turn green in the north-south direction the moment the truck leaves the warehouse.It is also assumed that the truck can reach the destination within 100 minutes.

In preparation for the next Tokaichi, the Tokaichi Executive Committee has decided to investigate which stalls will sell the most Okari Koboshi.The number of stores opening this year is 5 (A, B, C, D, E: half-width alphabets), and the number of sales is reported to the Tokaichi Executive Committee separately in the morning and afternoon.Create a program that takes information about each store as input and outputs the name and number of stores that sell the most items per day.

Japan wins the WBC national baseball competition for the second time in a row!! With baseball's popularity increasing, a baseball tournament was held at Aizu Gakuen High School.In this tournament, a round-robin league match was held, and the rankings were decided in the following manner.Please create a program that takes the results of each team as input and outputs the team names in order from the highest ranked team.If there are teams with the same ranking, please output them in the order in which they were input.However, the number of teams n is an integer between 2 and 10, the team name t is a single half-width alphabetic character, and the performance r for each match is represented by n - 1 numbers, with 0 for a win, 1 for a loss, and 2 for a draw.Also, it is assumed that there are no duplicate team names.

The greatest common divisor is an essential element in mathematics handled on computers.Using the greatest common divisor can make a big difference in the efficiency of calculations.One of the algorithms for finding the greatest common divisor is "Euclidean algorithm".The process flow is shown below.For example, in the case of 1071 and 1029, substitute 1071 to X and 1029 to Y, the remainder of 1071 ÷ 1029 is 42, substitute 42 to X and swap X and Y.(1 step) The remainder of 1029 ÷ 42 is 21, so substitute 21 for X and swap X and Y.(2 steps) The remainder of 42 ÷ 21 is 0, so substitute 0 for X and swap X and Y.(3 steps) Since Y has become 0, X at this time becomes the greatest common divisor.Therefore, the greatest common divisor is 21.In this way, we were able to find the greatest common divisor of 1071 and 1029 in just 3 steps.Euclid's mutual division method yields results much faster than the method of calculating divisors and comparing them.Write a program that takes two integers as input, finds the greatest common divisor using Euclidean's algorithm, and outputs the greatest common divisor and the number of steps required for the calculation.

Artist Shinagawa was asked to exhibit n works.Therefore, I decided to exhibit a cube with six sides colored with paint.The piece uses all six colors: Red, Yellow, Blue, Magenta, Green, and Cyan, with each side filled with one color.Shinagawa created n pieces of different works by changing the way the colors were arranged even though they were cube-shaped works.As a friend of his, you were allowed to view his work before it was exhibited, and you noticed something.Even though they appear to be colored differently, there are actually cubes with the same color combination.If this continues, we will not be able to exhibit n works.Create a program that takes as input the number of works you have created and the color information of each work, and outputs the number of pieces you need to exhibit.The colors of each side of the cube are represented by symbols c1 to c6, arranged as shown below.Also, each of c1 to c6 will be one of the following colors: Red, Yellow, Blue, Magenta, Green, or Cyan.

Eyes City, a neutral city located in the center of four countries, is the home of the transcontinental train Bandai.There is a row of chairs on the platform for passengers waiting for the Bandai, and those who enter the platform can use the chairs freely.The Bandai train is cheap, fast, and comfortable, so many people from the four surrounding countries use it.Today is the anniversary of the opening, so I'm thinking of doing something special for the people sitting on the platform.To do this, we need to know where the people who pass through the ticket gate are sitting.Create a program that simulates how the chairs are filled, taking into account the difficult personalities of the people in the four countries.People who pass through the ticket gate sit on chairs lined up in a row one after another.The people of the four countries have their own personalities and ways of sitting.The seating positions for each are as follows.Write a program that inputs the information of passengers about to board the Bandai train and outputs information about how they are sitting on their chairs.Outputs the nationalities of the people sitting in order from the left.However, if the seat is vacant, please print # (half-width sharp).

Taro is planning a long journey by train during his summer vacation.However, in order for Taro, a high school student, to travel as far as possible during his only one-month summer vacation, he must find the cheapest and quickest way possible to make a successful plan.Let's create a program that will help Taro plan so that he can enjoy his wonderful trip to the fullest.Create a program that takes track information and the number of stations as input, and outputs the minimum amount or shortest time according to the inquiry.

You have finally obtained the magic cauldron, the alchemy cauldron.You can create new items by putting multiple items into the alchemy cauldron.Newly created items can also be put into the Alchemy Cauldron to create other items.A list of items needed to create an item is called an alchemy recipe.The following three are examples of alchemy recipes.Items can be obtained using money, but it may be cheaper to craft them in an alchemy cauldron.For example, suppose the purchase price of each item is given as follows:You can purchase a racket for 5,000 yen, but you can get it for 3,800 yen by purchasing wood chips and thread and alchemizing it.Furthermore, by repeating alchemy, you can obtain items at a very reasonable price.Figure 1 combines the three alchemy recipe examples above.You can buy the guitar for 98,000 yen, but if you purchase and alchemize wood chips, string, rice, water, and a microphone, you can get it for only 13,636 yen.You want to get the items you want as cheaply as possible to make your adventure go smoothly.Create a program that takes as input a list of items, a list of alchemy recipes, and a specified item, and outputs the minimum amount of money required to make the specified item.There is no limit to the quantity of each item.An item may be used in multiple recipes, but there is at most one recipe for making an item.Also, when you follow a recipe starting from a certain item, that item will not appear in the recipe again.

At Taro Aizu's company, there is a boss who hates things that cannot be divided.When Taro goes out to eat with his boss, he splits the bill, but whenever the amount to be paid is not divisible by the number of participants, his boss always pays for it.One day, Taro became the organizer of a dinner party.Taro, who was short on money, wondered if he could somehow invite his boss to treat him.I now have to order from the restaurant, but since I don't know how many people will be attending yet, I would like to order something so that no matter how many people attend, they will be able to get something.You, who are Taro's classmates and are also planning to attend the dinner party, decide to work with Taro to calculate an amount that is less than the budget and is not evenly divisible by any number of people.Create a program that takes as input the type of dish, the price of each dish, and the budget amount, and outputs the total amount that is not divisible by any number that is less than or equal to the budget amount (excluding 1 and the total amount).Please note that you can order multiple dishes of each type, but it is not necessary to order all types of dishes.However, if there is no such total amount, print NA.

Mr. Yukiya, the manager of Aizuyama Ski Resort, has prepared a course with obstacles and jumping hills for advanced skiers.There are various ways to ski on the course, and users who can ski all of the patterns during the season will receive a gift.Let's create a program for Mr. Yukiya that will output the number of skiing patterns based on the course sketch.The course is represented by a grid consisting of X × Y squares as shown in the diagram above.Assume that the origin is at the top left, the x coordinate increases toward the right, and the y coordinate increases toward the bottom.Each sliding pattern starts at the highest point ( y = 1 , but without obstacles) and progresses toward the goal ( y = Y ).A runner in grid square ( x, y ) can move to ( x − 1, y + 1 ), ( x, y + 1 ), or ( x + 1, y + 1 ).There are obstacles and jumping platforms in the squares, and you cannot enter a square with an obstacle, but if you enter a square with a jumping platform, you will move to (x, y + 2).However, there is no jump platform in the highest square (the square with y = 1), and you can only enter a square with a jump platform from a square with the same x coordinate.If you start from the top of the course ( y = 1) and cross the bottom without deviating from the course ( y ≥ Y ), it is considered one way of sliding and the slide ends.Please create a program that takes course information as input and outputs the total number of ways to ski.

In the year 40XX, the Earth is being invaded by aliens! Most of the Earth has already been taken over by the aliens, and the only remaining defense base is Tsuruga Castle Fortress.Suppression forces are approaching the Tsuruga Castle fortress one after another.But hope remains.The Defense Force's ultimate weapon, the ultra-long-range penetrating laser cannon, has been completed.The downside is that it requires a certain distance before it becomes effective, and enemies that are too close will simply pass through.Enemies that have invaded an area where no power can be produced have no choice but to deal with them using other forces.Defense Force staff members need to know the number of invading UFOs in order to deal with them, but there are so many enemies that it seems difficult to count them properly.So the staff officer orders you to prepare a program that will output the number of UFOs that were not shot down by the laser.Create a program before the battle begins and use it to protect Tsuruga Castle Fortress.Enemy UFOs will simply charge straight at the base with the laser cannon (the UFOs are designed to pass through each other, so they won't collide).The laser cannon starts firing a laser at the center of the nearest UFO after 1 minute from its initial state, and continues firing the laser under the same conditions every minute thereafter.The laser can penetrate and shoot down UFOs that are just grazed by the laser.However, this laser has a range within which it is not powerful, and there is no point in aiming at UFOs that fall within that range, so it is designed not to aim at them.When you have shot down as many UFOs as possible, how many UFOs have entered the range where the laser's power is not available?Create a program that takes as input the radius R of the range where the laser's power does not come out and information on incoming UFOs, and outputs the number of invading UFOs that were not shot down.Information about the attacking UFOs consists of the number N of UFOs, the initial coordinates (x0, y0) of each UFO, the radius r of each UFO, and the minute speed v of each UFO.Let the origin of the coordinates (0, 0) be the position of the laser cannon.The distance between the laser cannon and the UFO is given by the distance from the origin to the center of the UFO, and if this distance is less than R, the UFO is considered to be within the range where the laser's power is not produced.It is assumed that there are multiple targets to aim at at the same time.All calculations are considered on a plane, and all inputs are given as integers.

A group of five friends decided to play rock, paper, scissors.Rock, paper, scissors has three moves: Goo, Choki, and Par. If it's a match between Goo and Choki, Goo "wins" and Choki is "lose." If it's a paper and par, then Choki "wins" and Par means "loss." If it's Par and Goo, Par "wins" and Goo "loses."If everyone rolls the same move, or if all of the moves are "goo", "choki", and "pa", it will be called "Aiko".Write a program that takes the input of 5 people's rock-paper-scissors moves and outputs the winning and losing results for each person.Rock-paper-scissors moves are represented by the numbers 1 for go, 2 for scissors, and 3 for par.Wins and losses are expressed as 1 for "win", 2 for "loss", and 3 for "adjust", and are output according to the input order.

You want to go on a trip with a friend.However, my friend who is a spendthrift has a hard time saving up for travel expenses.If my friend continues with her current lifestyle, I don't know when she will be able to go on a trip.So you want to go on a trip soon, so you decide to create a program to help your friends save systematically.If your friend's allowance for a given month is M yen and the money you spend that month is N yen, you will save (M - N) yen for that month.Write a program that inputs monthly income and expenditure information M and N and outputs the number of months it takes for the savings to reach the travel cost L.However, if the savings have not reached the travel cost after 12 months, please print NA.

Mr. B, a relative, came to Mr. A's house.He is 3 years old and loves blocks.The block he has is shaped like Figure 1.Mr. B is laying blocks on the board.When I asked him, "What are you making?", he cheerfully answered, "Maze!!"The maze he refers to is an arrangement of blocks that can be traced from the start to the goal using only blocks of the same color that touch on the sides.Figure 2 shows a maze created by yellow blocks from the top left (start) to the bottom right (goal).As you, the programmer, look over at Mr. B who is innocently playing, you decide to see if the blocks are arranged in a maze.Create a program that inputs block information, start and goal coordinates, and outputs OK if the blocks are in a maze, and NG if they are not.The board has dimensions w in the horizontal direction and h in the vertical direction, and the top left coordinates are (1, 1) and the bottom right coordinates are (w, h).The blocks are all 2 x 4 rectangles of the same size.The block color c can be 1 (white), 2 (yellow), 3 (green), 4 (blue), or 5 (red).The orientation of the block on the board, d, is 0 if it is long in the horizontal direction, and 1 if it is long in the vertical direction.The position of the block is represented by the top left coordinates ( x, y ) of the block.In addition, the position of the block will not overlap with other blocks and will not protrude from the board.

Mr. Devunky, an architect living in Waterdeven, receives a request to renovate an old large hospital.In some countries, there are people who do not want to use numbers that are disliked as room numbers (4 and 9 are famous in Japan).However, the room numbers at this hospital were numbered sequentially starting from 1, regardless of the number of abominations.Mr. Devunky was concerned about this, and before he finished replacing all the equipment and beds, he renumbered the room with numbers excluding Water Devun's hated numbers ``4'' and ``6''.However, since the replacement work was planned using the old room number, it is necessary to convert the old room number to the new room number to ensure that the remaining work can be completed.Mr. Devunki, who is not good at calculations, is shocked when he realizes this.For Mr. Devunky, please create a program that inputs the old room number and outputs the corresponding new room number.The correspondence table for room numbers up to the 15th is as follows.

A came to Aizu for sightseeing.You can see the Aizu Basin from the window of the hotel where you stayed.As I was looking at the scenery, I noticed a piece of a photo on the floor.It looks like he took a picture of the scenery outside the window.``I wonder where the picture was taken?'' Mr. A asked, holding up the photo so that the view outside the window was the same size as the photo, and began searching for a place where the photo and the view outside the window matched.Now, let's try doing what Mr. A is doing on a computer.Divide the view outside the window into n × n squares (n is called the size of the view).Each square contains an integer greater than or equal to 0 that represents information about the image in that square.The position of the square is expressed by coordinates ( x, y ).The coordinates of each square are as follows.A photo scrap is represented by an m × m square (m is called the size of the photo scrap).In this square, image information is written in the squares that are part of the scrap, and -1 is written in the squares that are not included in the scrap.For example, in the image below, the colored area represents the shape of the actual photo scrap.There may be holes in the pieces, but they are always connected together.(If cells with a value of 0 or more touch only at their vertices, they are considered not connected.) Also, cells with a value of -1 cannot be lined up vertically or horizontally from end to end.Find an area in the window view that matches the photo slice rotated 0, 90, 180, or 270 degrees.For example, if the landscape is represented as shown in the image below, rotating the slice in the image above 90 degrees counterclockwise will match the colored area.Write a program that takes as input the scenery seen from the window and the information about the edge of the photo, and outputs the coordinates of the square closest to the left edge of the area closest to the top of the area that matches the edge rotated by 0, 90, 180, or 270 degrees.If there are multiple matching areas, output the coordinates of the square closest to the left edge among the squares in those areas that are closest to the top.If there is no matching area, output NA.

A new gigantic maze, The Squares, has been completed at the famous theme park.Evacuation drills must be conducted under the guidance of the fire department, but since it is a huge maze, it is impossible to predict how long the drill will take.Therefore, you have decided to develop an evacuation training simulator based on the following specifications.As shown in Figure 1, a giant maze is represented by W × H squares with W horizontally and H vertically.Each square is either a passage (white square), a wall (brown square), or an emergency exit (green square).The ○ in the diagram represents a person, and the lowercase letters (E, W, S, N) inside represent the direction the person is facing (east, west, north, south).The diagram is drawn with north at the top.People in the giant maze initially stand facing either north, south, east, or west.Each person attempts to move simultaneously in 1-second increments using the following steps:Those who arrive at the emergency exit after moving will safely evacuate and disappear from the maze.Create a program that takes the given giant maze and the location information of people as input and outputs the time until all the people have evacuated.If it takes longer than 180 seconds to escape, please output NA.The location information of the maze and the person is given by the letters in row H and column W.The meaning of each character is as follows.#: Wall .: Floor X: Emergency exit E: Person facing east N: Person facing north W: Person facing west S: Person facing south The boundary between the maze and the outside is either wall # or emergency exit X.Also, there is always at least one person in a giant maze.

Akabeko Elementary School will be holding a slightly different jog in which all students will participate.Each student runs their own circuit at their own pace.After each child completes one lap around their own course, they return to the elementary school.After everyone starts elementary school at the same time, how many laps have each student completed at the same time before they all meet each other at the same time? Write a program that takes as input the number of students n, the distance of one lap of each student's course d (km), and the running speed of each student v (km/hour), and outputs the number of laps each student has completed since they all started elementary school at the same time and will be at the elementary school at the same time next time.Please note that each student will not run more than 2 31 -1 laps.

Mr. A is planning to take a solo trip by express bus (hereinafter referred to as the ``bus'') during his high school vacation.First, Mr. A chose the town he most wanted to visit and made that his destination.Next, you need to decide on a route to take the bus from your departure point to your destination.When transferring, you will need to get off the bus and then transfer to another bus, so you will need a ticket for each bus.A received some discount bus tickets from an uncle of his relative.By using one of these tickets, you can purchase one ticket at half price.For example, when going from departure point 5 to destination 1 in Figure 1, there are two possible routes: 5→4→6→2→1 and 5→3→1.Assuming you have two discount tickets, if you take the route 5→4→6→2→1 to get the lowest transportation cost, you will use the discount on routes 4→6 and 6→2, and the total fare will be 4,600 yen.On the other hand, if you take the route 5→3→1, you will receive discounts on routes 5→3 and 3→1, and the total fare will be 3750 yen.A: You want to spend your money on sightseeing, so you are trying to keep transportation costs as low as possible.So Mr. A decided to write a program to find the cheapest transportation cost from a departure point to a destination.Create a program that takes as input the number of discount tickets, the number of towns connected by the bus, the number of bus routes, and route information for each bus, and outputs the cheapest transportation cost from the departure point to the destination.Each bus operates at the same fare in both directions.Also, let the number of towns be n, and each town is assigned a different number from 1 to n.It is assumed that there is always a route from the origin to the destination.

Housing manufacturer Yamada House has launched a new feature: Midori Home Town subdivisions, which are well-equipped with schools, hospitals, and other facilities.This subdivision is divided into multiple lots, and you can purchase as many as you like, but the combined land shape of the lots you purchase must be rectangular (including squares).In order to manage the subdivisions where all the lots have been sold, Yamada House has drawn boundaries for each buyer and placed a signboard with the purchaser's number on one of the lots.The boundary line was simply a line drawn on the ground with a tree branch, but it disappeared due to heavy rain a few days later, leaving only the signboard.Figure 1 shows the lot where the sign was located and the number of the purchaser who bought that lot.This does not explain how the subdivision land was purchased.The saving grace was that I found a memo (Figure 2) with the purchaser number b and the number of plots purchased k in a drawer in the office.As a programmer, you have decided to help Yamada House by writing a program.Create a program that takes as input the size of the lot X × Y, the number of buyers n, the memo information b, k, and the position information s of the signboard, and outputs the buyers of each lot as shown in Figure 3.For the given information, output NA in the following cases:

In Shikakucho, where many people who love squares live, a festival is held where the entire town is decorated with illuminations made up of square electric boards.This electric light board emits light when electricity is passed through it, and the boards that are in contact with the light-emitting board also emit light.Therefore, no matter how many electric boards you use, as long as they are all in contact with each other, you only need one power source.The festival executive committee will collect designs from the townspeople that combine rectangles and create a large number of illuminations.The number of power supplies required for each illumination varies depending on the arrangement of the rectangles that make up the design, so it takes a lot of effort to figure out the number of power supplies required.So, you decide to help the executive committee by creating a program that examines the layout of the rectangle and calculates the number of power supplies needed.Create a program that takes as input the number of illuminations and information about the rectangles that make up each illumination, and outputs the number of power supplies required for each illumination.Consider overlapping or touching rectangles as one group, and count one power supply per group.

Pachimon Creature, a very popular game in a certain country, has been remade and released in Japan.You love games, and after playing this game so many times, you start thinking about how to complete it in the fastest way possible.However, no matter how much you think about it, you still can't figure out the fastest way to beat the game, so you decide to create a program to find out how quickly you can complete the game.The details of the game are as follows.The game is set in a world where there are many creatures called Pachimon Creatures (Pachikuri).Each pachikuri has one of five attributes: fire, ice, wood, earth, and water.At the beginning of the game, the main character selects one Pachikuri of his or her favorite attributes as his adventure partner.The goal of the game is to aim for the goal with the pachikuri, defeat the rivals at the goal, and become the pachikuri master.However, in order to defeat your rival, you cannot win unless you have Pachikuri of all attributes, so you must catch Pachikuri of all attributes along the way.Attributes are the key to catching Pachikuri.Fire attribute Pachikuri can catch ice attribute Pachikuri, similarly, ice attribute can catch wood attribute, wood attribute can catch earth attribute, earth attribute can catch water attribute, and water attribute can catch fire attribute.The relationship between attributes is shown in the figure below.The diagram below represents an example of the map on which the game will take place.The main character starts from the starting point "S" with one Pachikuri and aims for the goal point "G" by moving one space at a time.Along the way, if you catch a Pachikuri of four attributes other than the first Pachikuri you have and move it to the square that is the goal point, the game will end.The protagonist can move from the square they are currently on to the next square that shares an edge, and this is counted as one move.When the main character moves to a square with a pachikuri, if he has a pachikuri with an attribute that can catch that pachikuri, he will have caught that pachikuri.You can move to all squares as many times as you like, regardless of whether or not you can catch the Pachikuri in that square.Create a program that inputs the size of the map (number of horizontal columns, number of vertical rows) and the initial state of the map, and outputs the minimum number of moves from the start point to the goal point required to catch the initially selected pachikuri attribute and the other four pachikuri attributes.

Mr. Matsudaira is always conscious of the environment in his daily life.Last month's water bill was 4,280 yen, which exceeded my usual goal of 4,000 yen, so I've been trying to save water this month.How much did you save on your water bill compared to last month?Create a program that takes this month's water usage w [m 3 ] as input, compares it with last month's water bill of 4280 yen, and outputs how much you saved on your water bill.In addition, water charges are calculated as follows.(Water charge) = (Basic charge) + (Water amount charge) Water charge is calculated according to the amount used as shown in the table below.For example, if the amount of water used is 40 [m 3 ], the basic fee will be 1150 yen (1st stage) + 10 [m 3 ] × 125 yen (2nd stage) + 10 [m 3 ] × 140 yen (3rd stage) + 10 [m 3 ] × 160 yen (4th stage) = 5400 yen.

At Aizu Riverside Hospital, inpatients walk twice a day for rehabilitation and health promotion.In order to leave the hospital in good health, the number of people who are trying to recover their physical strength through walking is increasing day by day, so the director launched a project to ``Give a present to the person who walked the longest distance in one day!''Create a program that takes as input the number of patients n (1 ≤ n ≤ 10000), each patient's number p i (1 ≤ p i ≤ 10000), the first distance walked d1 i , and the second distance walked d2 i (0 ≤ d1 i , d2 i ≤ 5000), and outputs the number of the patient with the longest total distance walked and its distance.However, it is assumed that no two patients walk the same distance in a day.

At Aizu Shingjuku, when students enter the cram school, they take an ability test and are assigned to a class.Tests are conducted in three subjects: mathematics, English, and Japanese, and students are divided into classes A, B, and C.The level of A class is the highest and goes down in order.Classification decisions are based on the table below.If multiple conditions are met, you will be placed in a higher level class.Input the number of students n (1 ≤ n ≤ 10000), each student's math score pm i (0 ≤ pm i ≤ 100), English score pe i (0 ≤ pe i ≤ 100), Japanese language score pj i (0 ≤ pj i ≤ 100), and each student's class A, B, C (half-width alphabet).Please create a program that outputs.

There is an ice cream shop named Ten Ice Cream.The shop always has 10 different types of ice cream on the shelf.The store manager creates a graph showing ice cream sales every day to use as a reference for product development.For the store manager, you have decided to create a program that displays the number of sales of each ice cream in a graph.Create a program that takes as input the total number of ice creams sold in a day and the number of ice creams sold, and outputs the number * (half-width asterisk) of each type of ice cream sold.However, the type of ice cream is represented by an integer from 0 to 9.Also, if the number of ice creams sold is zero, one - (half-width hyphen) will be output.

``What size are your shoes?'' he suddenly asked when we met for the first time.``It's 23.5'' ``Wow, that's a really neat number. It's 2 to the 4th power plus 2 to the power 2 plus 2 to the 1st power plus 2 to the 0th power and 2 to the -1th power.'' The doctor then asked.``How tall are you?'' ``Yes, 158.1.'' The doctor folded his arms and closed his eyes.After a moment of silence, he spoke."Nah~" During the time we spent together after that, I gradually came to understand the doctor's actions.First, I will give you a real number as requested by the doctor.When a real number is expressed as a binary number with an integer part of up to 8 digits and a decimal part of up to 4 digits, the doctor talks with satisfaction about the result of conversion to binary number.If not, it will meow sadly.This repeats until I say a negative real number.Now, as the doctor ages, it becomes increasingly difficult to do long calculations.Therefore, in place of the doctor, please create a program that inputs real numbers, converts them to binary numbers, and outputs them.However, if the binary expression does not fit within the limited number of digits (up to 8 digits for the integer part + 4 digits for the decimal part), output NA (half-width alphabetic characters).The input real number must be within 8 digits for the integer part and 4 digits for the decimal part, and the binary representation to be output should be 8 digits for the integer part and 4 digits for the decimal part.

There is a game using numbers called "Fizz Buzz".In this game, multiple players count up the numbers one by one starting from 1, and each player says the next number that the previous player said.At that time, you must say "Fizz" if the number is divisible by 3, "Buzz" if it is divisible by 5, and "FizzBuzz" if it is divisible by both.For example, the first 16 utterances are as follows:1, 2, Fizz, 4, Buzz, Fizz, 7, 8, Fizz, Buzz, 11, Fizz, 13, 14, FizzBuzz, 16, ... Taro decided to play "Fizz Buzz" with his friends.Taro and his friends decided on the following rules.``The person who makes a mistake is eliminated.The next person starts with the number after the number they got wrong.In other words, if you say 1, 2, 3, you made a mistake with 3, so you will start with 4 next time.''The game is played according to this rule, but because you are not used to the game, you may not realize that you have made a mistake, and you will not be able to make fair decisions.So, in order for Taro and his friends to enjoy this game, you decided to create a program that outputs the number of people who remained after a set number of statements have been made.Create a program that takes as input the number of players, the number of times they said something during the game, and each comment, and outputs the numbers of the remaining players in ascending order of the number when input is complete.However, the players are assigned numbers starting from 1, and the speaking order will start from the first player, and when the first player has finished speaking, the first player will speak again.If the player who took his turn has already been eliminated, the next player will speak.Also, the program must ignore subsequent comments once the player is alone.

A set of four prime numbers arranged like ( a, a+2, a+6, a+8 ) is called a quadruplet prime.Among the four prime numbers that make up a quadruplet prime, the largest number is called the size of the quadruplet prime.For example, the smallest quadruplet prime is the pair (5, 7, 11, 13), whose magnitude is 13.The next largest quadruplet prime is the pair (11, 13, 17, 19), which has magnitude 19.Write a program that takes an integer n (13 ≤ n ≤ 10,000,000) as input and outputs the size of the largest quadruplet prime whose size is less than or equal to n.

Takayuki and Kazuyuki are twins who are best friends, but their behavior is completely opposite.For example, if Takayuki goes west, Kazuyuki goes east, and if Kazuyuki goes north, Takayuki goes south.The two of them are now at the department store and are in separate locations.How can two people who are moving in opposite directions meet as quickly as possible? A department store is represented by a grid consisting of W squares x H squares, and two people can move one square north, south, east, and west in a unit time.However, you cannot move to a square that is outside the grid or has an obstacle.As shown, the positions of grid squares are represented by coordinates ( x , y ).Write a program that takes grid information and the initial positions of two people as input, and outputs the shortest time until two people meet.If you cannot meet one or it will take more than 100 hours, print NA.The grid information is given by numbers in H rows and W columns, and the location information of the two people is given by coordinates.If either Takayuki-kun or Kazuyuki-kun falls outside the range of an obstacle or grid after moving, they will not be able to move, so the one who is located outside the range of the obstacle or grid will return to its original location, but the other players will be able to move without returning to their original location.When two people meet, it means that they stop on the same square after moving.Even if two people pass each other, it does not mean they have met.

Mr. A loves sweets, but recently his wife has been telling him to go on a diet.One day, when Mr. A was going from home to the city hall, his wife suggested that he go by bicycle.So Mr. A reluctantly got on his bicycle and went out, but Mr. A, who has a sweet tooth, decided to stop at a cake shop along the way and eat some cakes.If you cycle, you will burn calories depending on the distance traveled, but if you eat cake, you will ingest the same amount of calories.Net calories burned is calculated by subtracting the calories consumed by eating cake from the calories burned by cycling.Therefore, your net calorie expenditure may be less than 0.When Mr. A buys a cake at a cake shop, he eats it all on the spot.Mr. A may not necessarily stop at every cake shop, but whenever he passes through a point where a cake shop exists, he will definitely stop by, buy a cake, and eat it.However, it can be intimidating to pass by the same cake shop over and over again, so you can only visit each cake shop once.In addition, you may stop at a cake shop after passing the city hall, which is your destination, and then return to the city hall to finish your business, and you may visit as many times as you like except for the cake shop.Create a program that takes as input the map information from Mr. A's home to the city hall, a list of the calories in the cakes he can eat at the cake shops along the way, and the calories burned per unit distance, and outputs the minimum value of the net calories burned from the time he leaves his home until he enters the city hall.The map shows Mr. A's home, the city hall, a cake shop, and landmark buildings.The input data representing the map includes a line consisting of symbols representing two points and the distance between them, when there is a road connecting each landmark point with Mr. A's home, city hall, and cake shop.For example, if the distance between the fifth cake shop and the third landmark is 10, the input data would contain rows like this:In this way, cake shops are represented by C, and landmarks are represented by L in front of the number.Also, Mr. A's home is represented by H and the city hall is represented by D.If the input data is given two points and the distance between them, we can move between the two points in either direction.For example, in the example above, you can go from the cake shop to the landmark and vice versa.Also, you must be able to get to City Hall from your home.Other input data given are the number of cake shops m, the number of landmarks n, the calories consumed per unit distance k, m pieces of data representing the calories of cakes that can be bought at each of the cake shops from the first cake shop to the mth cake shop, and the total number of distance data d.

A relative, Mr. B, came to Mr. A's house.He is 3 years old and loves singing.He is trying his best to sing a song he learned in kindergarten called ``Kobutanu Kitsuneko'' (lyrics and music by Naozumi Yamamoto).In this song, the four words ``little pig,'' ``tanuki,'' ``fox,'' and ``neko'' are written in order, and the last and first sounds are the same.Boy B asked Girl A to tell her if she could make a similar shiritori from the words Boy B said.So, in order to help Ms. A, let's create a program that sequentially creates a Shiritori using all the words given, and then determines whether it is possible to make the first letter of the first word the same as the last letter of the last word.Write a program that takes n words as input, determines whether a shiritori can be created from a set of words, and outputs OK if possible, and NG if not.

Taro and Hanako decided to play hit and blow.The rules for hit and blow are as follows:Taro-kun and Hanako-san seem to find it a bit troublesome to judge the number of hits and blows each time.For these two people, let's create a program that will instantly tell you the number of hits and blows.Write a program that takes the correct answer r and answer a as input, and outputs the number of hits and blows.r and a are each a sequence of four numbers from 0 to 9.

As the weather continues to be bad and vegetable prices are soaring, Seven Mart is holding a sale for customers to buy vegetables in bulk.The store is very busy, as vegetables that are hard to find in stores are available at reasonable prices.One day, a trio of close friends living in the Matsunaga housing complex were having a lively conversation with a Seven Mart advertisement in hand.As this sale is called ``Customer Appreciation Day'', the highlight is that the cheapest vegetables in the bag will be free.When I read the advertisement, it seems like the sale is as follows.The three of us immediately went shopping at Seven Mart.After they finished shopping, the three of them met outside the store and talked with each other, feeling satisfied that they had bought so much at such a low price.It turned out that they had all bought the same vegetables.One person said, ``That's really cheap. That's XXX yen!'' The other person was surprised, ``Huh? Mine cost ** yen more than that! Why?'' The other person looked at the receipt and realized that he had bought it at the cheapest price.Now, how can you pack it in bags to get the lowest purchase price?Create a program that takes as input the number of vegetables to purchase, the number of vegetables in a bag, and the price of each vegetable, and outputs the minimum purchase price.

The screen that displays digital numbers, often seen on calculators, etc., is called a 7-segment display because the digital numbers are made up of seven segments.A new product that Wakamatsu is planning to sell will incorporate a 7-segment display, and you, as an employee, will be tasked with creating a program to display a given number on the 7-segment display.This 7-segment display does not change display status until the next switching instruction is sent.By sending a 7-bit signal, you can switch the display information of each corresponding segment.A bit is something that has a value of 1 or 0, where 1 means "toggle" and 0 means "as is."The relationship between each bit and segment is shown in the figure below.The signal sends 7 bits in the order "gfedcba".For example, to display "0" from a hidden state, "0111111" must be sent to the display as a signal.To change from "0" to "5", send "1010010".If you want to continue changing "5" to "1", send "1101011".Write a program that takes n (1 ≤ n ≤ 100) numbers of digits as input and outputs the signal sequence necessary to display those digits d i (0 ≤ d i ≤ 9) in order correctly on a 7-segment display.The initial state of the 7-segment display is assumed to be all hidden.

Ataru Ohashi, a student at Aizu Gakuen University High School, decided to play slot machines.When you insert a medal into this machine, the three reels start spinning and each reel stops automatically.In a normal game (normal game), you insert 3 medals, and when the symbols line up, you will get the following medals depending on the symbol.Depending on how the patterns line up, a special service will begin.When you get 3 7 symbols, the big bonus starts and you can play 5 bonus games.Also, when you get 3 symbols on the BAR, the regular bonus starts and you can play 3 bonus games.When you match three star symbols, a free game will start, and you will not receive any medals, but you will be able to start the next game without inserting any medals.During the bonus game, if you insert 2 medals per game, you will automatically get 3 grape patterns and get 15 medals.Mr. Ohashi took 100 medals and started playing with the machine.I played for a while and ended up with the normal game.How many medals did you have left?Please create a program that takes play information as input and outputs the number of medals remaining in hand.The play information is given as the number of big bonuses b, the number of regular bonuses r, the number of times grapes are aligned during the normal game g, the number of cherries aligned c, the number of stars aligned s, and the total number of games t.Note that t includes the number of bonus games.Also, medals will not run out during the game.

Ninja Atsushi guards the town from the roof of the Ninja Building every day from early morning until late at night.The Ninja Building is two adjacent buildings with the same number of floors, and Atsushi's daily routine is to jump between buildings to get to the roof for security purposes.These two buildings undergo frequent cleaning, so there are ladders to help climb the buildings and slippery areas to get in the way.Moreover, the position of ladders and slippery surfaces changes daily.Therefore, Atsushi has to think about how to get to the rooftop every day.Atsushi jumps between the walls of two buildings with the same number of floors, aiming for the roof of the building.Jumps can be started from the first floor of either building.When jumping to the opposite building, you can jump to the same floor, one floor above, or two floors above.There are three types of walls as shown below, and the movement after jumping onto each wall is determined.Additionally, the walls run from the ground floor to the top floor just below the rooftop, and the rooftop can only be accessed from the top floor of the building.Also, the walls on the lowest floor of the building will not be sliding walls.Write a program that takes as input the number of floors n of two buildings and the types of walls of two buildings, and outputs the minimum number of jumps required to reach the top floor and reach the roof.Please note that you can reach the roof of either building.However, if Atsushi cannot reach the roof of either building, please output "NA".

In Aizu Furumatsu Village, located far north of Aizuwakamatsu City, a bridge called ``Yabashi'' is the only means of transportation to surrounding villages.Although the bridge is used by many people, it is so old that it looks like it will fall apart at any moment.Ya Bridge has the strength to withstand up to 150 kg.For example, a person weighing 80 [kg] and a person weighing 50 [kg] can cross at the same time, but if a person weighing 90 [kg] starts crossing while a person weighing 80 [kg] is crossing the bridge, the bridge will collapse.If Ya Bridge were to be destroyed, the people of Aizu Furumatsu Village would have no means of traveling to surrounding villages.So, in order to protect the lives of the villagers, you, the only programmer in the village, decide to create a program that can determine whether or not a bridge will break based on how it is crossed.Write a program that takes as input the number of passersby crossing the bridge n (1 ≤ n ≤ 100), the weight of each passerby m i (1 ≤ m i ≤ 100), the time a i to start crossing the bridge, and the time b i (0 ≤ a i , b i < 2 31) to finish crossing the bridge, and outputs "OK" if the bridge does not break, and "NG" if it breaks.Furthermore, if the total weight of passersby on the bridge exceeds 150 kg, the bridge will collapse.Also, the passerby is on the bridge at time a i , but not at time b i .

Taro went to a toy store to buy the Game of Life made by Aizu Hobby.The Game of Life is played using a grid board and a roulette wheel.As shown in the diagram, there is one start point and one goal point on the board, and they are connected by a single connected square.First, the pieces are placed on the starting square, and the pieces advance according to the number that comes out when spinning the roulette wheel.Depending on the square, there is an event square where you can earn money or change the position of the pieces by stopping or passing there.The final winner or loser is determined by the amount of money the pieces have when they reach the goal.What's interesting about this company's game of life is that the size of the roulette wheel, the number of squares to reach the goal, and the arrangement of event squares are different for each package.They are written on the case and can be confirmed by reading it.Taro, who wants to choose the life game that will give him the most money, wants to buy the game with the highest expected value of money.So you decide to help Taro choose a game.Assume that the circumference of a roulette wheel is divided into X equal parts, and the values ​​V 1 , V 2 , ..., V X are written in each part.The board has squares numbered 0, 1, ..., Y, and they are connected in order.Among the squares, there are Z special squares called event squares, which perform a special action when you reach them.The grid number of the event grid is given by N i.There are 1 to 3 types of event masses (E i ), each of which performs the following actions.You start with 0 yen, start from the 0th square, and reach the goal when you reach the Yth square.If you cross the goal, it is also considered a goal.There are no events at the start and goal, and multiple events cannot overlap in one square.Ignores the event of the square you advanced to due to the event.If the amount of money you have is less than 0 yen, it will be set to 0 yen.For example, the expected value of money earned in a certain game of life can be calculated as follows.This example shows the game of life, which consists of three squares: start, event square (get 100 yen), goal, and a roulette wheel that rolls 1 or 2.First, when you spin the roulette wheel for the first time, if you roll a 1, you will reach the event square and have 100 yen in your possession.On the other hand, if you roll a 2, you have reached the goal and your money remains at 0 yen.Both of these things happen with a 1 in 2 chance.Furthermore, if you reach the event mass on the first spin, you will spin the roulette wheel a second time, but no matter what value you get, you will reach the goal, and you will have 100 yen in all cases.In this way, there are a total of three ways to reach the goal.Focusing on the amount of money you have at the time you reach the goal, there is one chance that you will end up with 0 yen and the probability is 1/2, and there are two ways that you will end up with 100 yen and the probability is 1/4.In this case, the expected value of the money you have at the goal is the sum of (money you have x the probability) for each method of goal, and the expected value of this game of life is 50 yen.Create a program that takes the roulette information and board information as input and outputs the expected value of the money you have at the goal.

There are many different ways to represent numbers, and the decimal system we usually use is only one representative one.Binary and hexadecimal numbers are very familiar to people with computer-related knowledge.Mr. N took advantage of his qualifications as a librarian to get a job as a librarian.The first task is to organize the books.In this library, each book is given a consistent number and the books are organized accordingly.In this library, book numbers are expressed in negative decimal numbers.Negative decimal numbers are written as a n a n−1 a n−2 ... a 2 a 1 a 0 (each a i is a number from 0 to 9), and this number represents the following number.a n ×(−10) n + a n−1 ×(−10) n−1 + ... + a 2 ×(−10) 2 + a 1 ×(−10) 1 + a 0 ×(−10) 0 For example, the negative decimal number 2156 corresponds to the decimal number -1944 as shown below.2×(-10) 3 + 1×(-10) 2 + 5×(-10) 1 + 6×(-10) 0 = 2×(-1000) + 1×(100) + 5×(-10) + 6×(1) = -2000 + 100 - 50 + 6 = -1944Mr. N feels very inconvenienced because it is difficult to convert decimal numbers to negative decimal numbers.Write a program that takes a book number as input and outputs the minus decimal representation of this number.

Aizu has an ancient legend of buried treasure.You have finally located the place where the buried treasure is buried.Since we know the depth to which the buried treasure is buried and the condition of the strata to be dug, if we make a careful plan, we can reach the buried treasure at the lowest possible cost.So you decide to write a program that reads the conditions of the geological formations and calculates the route that will take you to the depth of the buried treasure with the least cost.The state of the strata is represented by cells arranged in a two-dimensional grid, and the position of each cell is represented by coordinates (x,y).Assume that the top left is (1,1), the x coordinate increases as you go to the right, and the y coordinate increases as you go deeper down.You pick one of the cells with the lowest y coordinate and start digging there, and work your way down to one of the cells with the highest y coordinate.There are two types of cells in a stratum: You can only dig from a cell to the left, right, and down.Once a cell has been dug, it can be moved left or right, but it cannot be moved up.When excavating, you must carry an oxygen cylinder with you.The moment your oxygen tank reaches zero, you will no longer be able to move, excavate, or replenish oxygen.The remaining amount decreases by 1 each time you move a cell.Even if you reach the depth of the buried treasure and the oxygen tank has 0 remaining, it is not considered that you have reached it.In addition, oxygen can be replenished in cells that have accumulated oxygen, but any oxygen that exceeds the capacity will be discarded.Create a program that takes as input the size of the stratum, excavation cost, oxygen cylinder capacity, amount of oxygen in the initial state, and stratum information, and outputs the minimum cost to reach the deepest cell.However, if the minimum cost exceeds the excavation cost, or if you cannot reach the buried treasure no matter how much you dig, please output "NA".

Under the command to ``save Sergeant Ryan,'' the Aizu army's rescue team engages in fierce battles with enemy forces on the floating city of Germany.They were safely reunited with the sergeant, but there were so many enemy tanks that they were unable to call for a rescue helicopter.So they decide to blow up all the bridges in the city to confuse the enemy tanks.The plan was immediately communicated to headquarters, and preparations were made for a rescue helicopter.In order to send a rescue helicopter, you have to predict when all the bridges will be blown up.Your task as a military programmer is to calculate the time required for the rescue team to blow up all the bridges.The floating city consists of N islands, with bridges between them.All islands are connected in a tree shape (see image below).There is only one route from one island to another.It takes a set amount of time to cross each bridge, and you can cross the bridge in either direction in that amount of time.The rescue team does not have boats or other means of water transportation, so the only way to travel between the islands is via bridges.The rescue team can instantly blow up any bridges adjacent to the island they are currently on.What is the minimum time required for the rescue team to blow up all the bridges?However, we do not consider the travel time within the island.Create a program that takes as input the number of islands and information on each bridge, and outputs the minimum time required to blow up all the bridges.Each island is represented by a number from 1 to N.There are N -1 bridges.Bridge information consists of the numbers (a, b) of the two islands adjacent to the bridge and the time t required to cross the bridge.The rescue team shall start from island number 1.

The alien Mr.X left a message for Earthlings to commemorate his visit to Earth.The place Mr.This was a mysterious place with strange stone statues placed casually in the squares of a Go board of various sizes.As a message, Mr.Mr.However, depending on the placement of the stone statues, there were some Go boards where it was impossible to draw a closed curve, and in that case no message was left.The closed curve drawn on the Go board in Figure 1 passes through all empty squares only once.Mr.X left a closed curve like this as a message.Mr.X did not draw a closed curve like the one drawn on the Go board in Figure 2.Later, Mr.However, due to weathering over the years, the message disappeared and only the legend remained.Create a program that takes the Go board information as input and outputs Yes if Mr.X left a message on the board, and No if Mr.X did not.However, if stone statues are placed in all squares, No will be output.

Unexplored land "Nalamanda region" Explorers Bob and Mike faced various difficulties in search of the golden legend hidden in this deep cave.The last door I finally reached.The gold of your dreams awaits you.There were many triangles drawn on the door.When Mike touched one, the triangle lit up.The light extends vertically from the base of the triangle toward the apex while drawing a rectangle, and touches another triangle beyond that.Then, the triangles that touched the light also emitted light in the same way, and the process was repeated.All the triangles on the door are isosceles triangles (not including equilateral triangles), all of them are the same size, none overlap (and do not touch), and the light extends a certain distance.When the last triangle finished glowing, silence returned as if nothing had happened.Bob finds a strange message at his feet.``Do not touch this door indiscriminately. If you touch this door correctly, this door will not be released.'' Bob and Mike thought about the meaning of this message and tried touching the triangle many times, but the door would not open.However, as they repeated this process over and over again, they gradually began to understand the meaning of the message.``Don't touch the door unnecessarily'' means that you should touch the door as little as possible. If you touch it as few times as possible and make all the triangles glow, the door will surely open!'' For example, a door has multiple isosceles triangles drawn on it, as shown in Figure 1.When you first touch the triangle on the lower left, as shown in Figure 2, light is emitted from the base of the triangle perpendicularly toward the apex, and the triangles that the light touches light up one after another.Then touch the leftmost triangle and all the triangles will light up, as shown in Figure 3.This procedure is the minimum number of moves (2 moves) to make all triangles shine.Now help Bob and Mike open the final door.Create a program that takes as input the number of triangles drawn on the door, the length of the light, and the coordinates of the vertices of each triangle, and outputs the minimum number of times the door must be touched to make all the triangles glow.Assume that the length of light is the length from the base.Also, the light is long enough to include the vertices of the triangle from which it is emitted.When solving this problem, if the distance between two points is 0.01 or less, they are treated as the same point.Also, if the distance between a point and a straight line is 0.01 or less, this point is treated as being on this straight line.

Food contains three macronutrients: protein, fat, and carbohydrate.Protein and carbohydrates have 4 kcal (kilocalories) per gram (gram), and fat has 9 kcal.For example, according to the table below, cake number 1 contains 7 g of protein, 14 g of fat, and 47 g of carbohydrates.Calculating the calories contained based on this is 4 × 7 + 9 × 14 + 4 × 47 = 342 kcal.Others are calculated in the same way.Create a program that takes as input the number n of sweets to be classified, information about each sweet, and information about restrictions, and outputs a list of sweets that do not exceed the limit (and can be eaten) if only that one sweet is used.Information about a candy consists of the number s of the candy, the weight of protein contained in the candy p, the weight of lipid q, and the weight of carbohydrate r.The restriction information consists of the maximum protein weight P that can be contained, the maximum fat weight Q, the carbohydrate weight R, and the maximum calorie C that can be ingested.If any one of the protein, fat, carbohydrate, or calorie content is exceeded, it will violate the restriction and will be judged as a ``candy that cannot be eaten.''For a list of sweets you can eat, please output the numbers of the sweets you can eat in the order you input them.If there are no sweets you can eat, please output "NA".For the four sweets in the table above, if the restrictions are P = 10, Q = 15, R = 50, C = 400, cake and pudding are classified as sweets that can be eaten because their respective nutrients and calories are below the limit values, but potato chips are classified as sweets that cannot be eaten because the amount of carbohydrates and calories exceeds the limit values, and dorayaki is classified as a sweet that cannot be eaten because the amount of carbohydrates exceeds the limit values.A sequence of multiple datasets is given.The end of the input is marked by a line with a single zero.Each dataset is given in the following format:The number of sweets n (1 ≤ n ≤ 1000) is given in the first line.In the following n lines, the number of the i-th candy s i (1 ≤ s i ≤ 1000) and the integers p i , q i , r i (0 ≤ p i , q i , r i ≤ 100) representing the weight of each nutrient are given.In the following rows, integers P , Q , R (0 ≤ P , Q , R ≤ 100), C (0 ≤ C ≤ 1700) representing the limit values ​​for each nutrient and calorie are given.The number of datasets does not exceed 100.For each data set, output the number or “NA” of the candy that is allowed to be eaten.

Interest is attached to money deposited in banks, and the calculation method and interest rate vary depending on the bank.The combination of interest and principal is called principal and interest, and there are two ways to calculate principal and interest: simple interest, which calculates interest without incorporating it into the principal, and compound interest, which calculates interest by incorporating it into the principal.In order to get more principal and interest, you must understand these differences.The principal and interest calculation method is as follows.Create a program that inputs the number of banks, the number of years you want to deposit the money, and information about each bank (bank number, interest rate type, annual interest rate (percentage)) and outputs the bank number that gives you the highest principal and interest rate.However, there is only one bank that offers the highest principal and interest rate.Given multiple datasets.The end of the input is indicated by a single zero.Each dataset is given in the following format:The first line gives the number of banks n (1 ≤ n ≤ 50), and the second line gives the number of years y (1 ≤ y ≤ 30) to keep the money.In the following n lines, the bank number b i of the i-th bank, an integer r i (1 ≤ r i ≤ 100) representing the annual interest rate, and the type of interest rate t i (1 or 2) are given.The type of interest rate t i is given as 1 for simple interest and 2 for compound interest.The number of datasets does not exceed 100.For each data set, the bank number with the highest principal and interest rate will be output on one line.

There are things called quaternions that are an extension of complex numbers.This is a convenient number that can be used to control the arms of robots, as it is useful for expressing things like the rotation of objects.A quaternion is expressed as $x + yi + zj + wk$ using four real numbers $x$, $y$, $z$, $w$ and special numbers (extended imaginary numbers) $i$, $j$, $k$.The sum of such quaternions is defined as:$(x_1 + y_1 i + z_1 j + w_1 k) + (x_2 + y_2 i + z_2 j + w_2 k) = (x_1 + x_2) + (y_1 + y_2)i + (z_1 + z_2)j + (w_1 + w_2)k$ while 1, $i$, $j$, $k$The product between is given as:This table represents the product $AB$ of two special numbers $A$ and $B$.For example, the product $ij$ of $i$ and $j$ is $k$, and the product $ji$ of $j$ and $i$ is $-k$.The product of general quaternions is computed to satisfy this relationship.For example, the product of two quaternions, $1+2i+3j+4k$ and $7+6i+7j+8k$, is calculated as follows.$(1+2i+3j+4k) \times (7+6i+7j+8k)=$ $7+6i+7j+8k$ $+14i+12i^2+14ij+16ik$ $+21j+18ji+21j^2+24jk$ $+28k+24ki+28kj+32k^2$ By applying the above table $=-58+16i+36j+32k$.Input two quaternions ($x_1+y_1 i+z_1 j+w_1 k$) and ($x_2+y_2 i+z_2 j+w_2 k$) where the four coefficients $x$, $y$, $z$, $w$ are integers and are not all zero, and their product is ($x_3+y_3 i+z_3 j+w_3Write a program that outputs $x_3$, $y_3$, $z_3$, and $w_3$ when k$).Given multiple datasets.The end of the input is marked by a line with a single zero.Each dataset is given in the following format:$n$ $data_1$ $data_2$ : $data_n$ The number of quaternion pairs to be processed $n$ ($ n \leq 10$) is given in the first line.In the following $n$ lines, information $data_i$ for the $i$-th quaternion pair is given in the following format.$x_1$ $y_1$ $z_1$ $w_1$ $x_2$ $y_2$ $z_2$ $w_2$ All given coefficients must be between -1000 and 1000.The number of datasets does not exceed 50.Prints the product of the given quaternion pairs for each dataset.

Mobile phones are equipped with a function that displays input candidates in order to efficiently enter texts such as emails.This records frequently used words and presents words with the first letter of the input letter as input candidates.For example, if you often type the word "computer", just type "c" and "computer" will be suggested as a suggestion.Let's create the basics of such functionality.Write a program that takes a sentence or character as input and outputs words with that character as the first letter in order of number of occurrences.However, if there are multiple words with the same number of occurrences, they will be output in lexicographical order.Up to 5 words can be output.If the corresponding word does not exist, please output "NA".Given multiple datasets.The end of the input is represented by a single zero.Each dataset is given in the following format:The first line gives the number of lines of text n (1 ≤ n ≤ 10).The next n lines contain sentences.Text should consist of half-width lowercase letters and half-width spaces, and the number of characters per line should be between 1 and 1024 characters.Words separated by spaces can be at least 1 and no more than 20 characters.The initial letter k is given as a half-width alphabetic character on the following line.The number of datasets does not exceed 100.Outputs words starting with the specified letter or “NA” for each dataset.

Tablet interface technology, which allows users to operate the screen by touching it with their fingers, is also being applied in the field of games, creating various types of games with a new sense of operation.The game currently being developed by AZ is one of them.The requirements for this software (game) are as follows.It is a game where you fill in the grid in this way, and eventually make the entire grid one color.If you solve the problem in fewer steps, you will receive a higher score.As a programmer at AZ Corporation, you are tasked with developing part of the logic for this game.First, in order to calculate this maximum score, I decided to create a program that calculates the minimum number of times the selection button must be pressed to make the grid a solid color if the game is solved in the shortest possible time.Given multiple datasets.The end of the input is indicated by two zeros.Each dataset is given in the following format:The first row gives the grid column and row sizes X , Y (2 ≤ X , Y ≤ 10).In the following Y rows, the characters c i,j representing the color of the cell in the i-th column and j-th row are given, separated by spaces.Outputs the minimum number of button presses in one line for each dataset.

Takeshi, who loves hot springs, is planning a trip to a certain hot spring area during his next long vacation.When traveling, I would like to take a long-distance bus and get to my destination as cheaply as possible.Even though he has savings, Takeshi doesn't have much money to spare, so he decides to consult his grandfather.After hearing the plan, the old man was impressed and gave Takeshi a special ticket.The ticket was for one free ride on two consecutive long-distance bus sections.Depending on how you use it, you can expect a considerable reduction in travel costs, but in order to make the most of it, you need to have a solid plan.A total of n departure points, destinations, and relay points are given, and m routes connecting the two points are given.Each point is assigned a number from 1 to n.The starting point is 1 and the destination is n.Route information is represented by the two points a and b that the route connects, and the fare c.The special ticket allows you to cross two consecutive lines once from any point for free.However, passing through a destination on the way does not mean that you have reached your destination.Create a program that inputs the starting point, destination, total number of transit points n, number of routes m, and information about each route, and outputs the minimum fare.However, it is assumed that there is always a route from the departure point to the destination.Given multiple datasets.The end of the input is indicated by a line of two zeros.Each dataset is given in the following format:The first line gives the total number of departure points, destinations, and transit points n (2 ≤ n ≤ 100) and the number of routes m (1 ≤ m ≤ 300).The following m lines give information about each route a i , b i , c i (1 ≤ c i ≤ 1000).The number of datasets does not exceed 40.For each input dataset, output the minimum value of the charges in one line.

Better products, cheaper.Today, there is a fierce battle going on during the time sale held at some supermarket.LL-do here in Aizu is one such supermarket, and in order to compete with other chain stores, they run a slightly unusual time sale.In a typical time sale, multiple products become cheaper at the same time, but at LL-do, the start time of the time sale is set separately depending on the target product.The Sakai family is one of the families that often uses LL-do.Under the leadership of his wife, the Sakai family held a strategy meeting for the next Sunday's time sale, and decided to analyze the order in which they should shop to maximize discounts.The Sakai family, who are familiar with the store, were able to draw a floor plan of the sales floor, and were able to figure out where the items they wanted were located, how much they were discounted, and how long it would take for them to sell out.The Sakai family had been perfect up to this point, but they didn't have anyone who could do an analysis.So you, who were friends with the Sakai family, decided to write a program.We perform a simulation assuming that one person is moving.There are 10 types of products, and they are distinguished by the one-digit product number g.Time sale information includes product number g, discount amount d, time sale start time s, and sell-out time e.The inside of the store is represented by a two-dimensional grid consisting of squares of X width and Y length, and each square is assigned either an aisle or a shelf.One product shelf has one type of product, and it is distinguished by the product number g.You can buy any product, but please do not buy multiple products with the same product number.You can pick up products from the shelf in any direction from the adjacent aisles, vertically, horizontally, or horizontally.You can pick up products from the time the time sale starts, but you can't pick up the products from the time they sell out.Also, the time starts from 0 when you enter the store.You can move from the square you are currently in to the adjacent aisle squares up, down, left, or right, but you cannot move to the square of the product shelf.You also cannot go outside the store represented by the grid.One unit of time passes each time you move.Also, we do not consider the time it takes to pick up the product.Create a program that takes as input a floor plan of the store, the initial location of the shopper, and time sale information on products, and outputs the maximum value of the total discount amount of the products that could be purchased.Given multiple datasets.The end of the input is indicated by a line of two zeros.Each dataset is given in the following format:The store size X , Y (3 ≤ X , Y ≤ 20) is given in the first line.In the following Y row, the i-th column and j-th in-store information m i,j is given as follows.(period): Aisle square Number: Product number P: Shopper's initial position square The number of time sale information n (1 ≤ n ≤ 8) is given in the following row.The i-th time sale information g i d i s i e i is given in the following n lines (0 ≤ g i ≤ 9, 1 ≤ d i ≤ 10000, 0 ≤ s i , e i ≤ 100).The number of datasets does not exceed 50.Outputs the maximum total discount amount of products that can be taken in one line for each data set.

The manager of Yukurido, a Japanese sweets shop in Aizuwakamatsu City, is a very skilled craftsman, but I'm disappointed in him for being a little moody.The manju made by the manager is very delicious, but the size varies depending on your mood at the time.The store manager's wife, who couldn't bear to see it, came up with the idea of ​​packing manju of varying sizes and weights into bags and selling them.If you pack the manju into bags to a certain weight and sell them, you can sell them at a fixed price even if they are all different sizes, and the surprise of not knowing what kind of manju is inside until you open it may also be a good selling point.When they started selling the product under the name ``Barabara Manju,'' the new idea became a hot topic, and Yukurido quickly became popular.However, there was a problem: the number of ``separate manjus'' that could be made changed depending on how the manjus were put together in the bag, resulting in waste.You came to Yukurido for a part-time job and decided to create a program to fill bags without wasting anything.There are 9 different weights of manju from 1 to 9.When packing the buns, pack them so that the total weight of the buns is exactly 10.The combination of manju is 1+1+2+4+2=10 or 1+1+1+1+1+5=10, and you can use any number of manju of the same weight.Create a program that takes the information about the number of manju to be made and the weight of the manju as input, and outputs the maximum number of "separate manju" that can be made.Given multiple datasets.The end of the input is indicated by a single zero.Each dataset is given in the following format:The number of buns n (2 ≤ n ≤ 100) is given in the first line.The second line gives the weight m i (1 ≤ m i ≤ 9) of each bun.The number of datasets does not exceed 100.Outputs the maximum number of "separate buns" that could be packed into one line for each dataset.

There is a rectangular maze with square cells lined up horizontally and vertically.In this maze, move to adjacent squares in the north, south, east, and west, starting from the starting square S and aiming for the goal square G.There are three types of trout: plains, mountains, and ice.S and G are placed on the plain squares.You can move to plains squares, but you cannot move to mountain squares.Ice squares can be moved, but depending on the conditions, the ice may crack and become immobile.For example, Figure 1 shows that the number of steps in the shortest path of a given maze is 11.However, if you try to take a shortcut through the ice cubes as shown in Figure 2, you will not be able to reach G because you will be stuck after the fourth movement to the ice block of size 6.Create a program that takes this maze information as input and calculates the number of steps in the shortest path from S to G.Each type of square is represented by the following letters: The given maze must be solvable.A maze is given as the number of squares in the east-west direction, X, the number of squares in the north-south direction, Y, and X × Y letters.Given multiple datasets.The end of the input is indicated by a line of two zeros.Each dataset is given in the following format:The first line gives integers X , Y (2 ≤ X, Y ≤ 12) that represent the size of the maze.The information for the i-th line of the maze, line i (a half-width alphabetic string of length X), is given in the following Y line.The number of datasets does not exceed 40.Outputs the minimum number of steps in one line for each dataset.

Deep in a secluded forest, there lived a witch named Marie.Since she is a witch, she uses magic to provide for the necessities of life, such as food, water, and fuel.Her magic is activated by drawing a magic circle using several magical stones and strings.This magic circle is created by setting up stones and connecting several pairs of stones with a string.However, the string should not be loose.Furthermore, none of the strings (other than the ends) should touch any other strings or stones.Of course, you cannot place multiple stones in the same place.As long as this constraint is
