In order to make sure that the servant eventually find the brave man, the king demon decided to eliminate all cycles by building walls between cells. At the same time, he must be careful so that there is at least one path between any two cells not occupied by obstacles. Your task is to write a program that computes in how many ways the kind demon can build walls.

Mr. KM, the mayor of KM city, decided to build a new elementary school. The site for the school has an awkward polygonal shape, which caused several problems. The most serious problem was that there was not enough space for a short distance racetrack. Your task is to help Mr. KM to calculate the maximum possible length for the racetrack that can be built in the site. The track can be considered as a straight line segment whose width can be ignored. The boundary of the site has a simple polygonal shape without self-intersection, and the track can touch the boundary. Note that the boundary might not be convex.

One sunny day, Dick T. Mustang found an ancient personal computer in the closet. He brought it back to his room and turned it on with a sense of nostalgia, seeing a message coming on the screen: Yes. BASIC. BASIC is a programming language designed for beginners, and was widely used from 1980's to 1990's. There have been quite a few BASIC dialects. Some of them provide the PLAY statement, which plays music when called with a string of a musical score written in Music Macro Language (MML). Dick found this statement is available on his computer and accepts the following commands in MML: As an amateur composer, Dick decided to play his pieces of music by the PLAY statement. He managed to write a program with a long MML sequence, and attempted to run the program to play his music -- but unfortunately he encountered an unexpected error: the MML sequence was too long to be handled in his computer's small memory! Since he didn't want to give up all the efforts he had made to use the PLAY statement, he decided immediately to make the MML sequence shorter so that it fits in the small memory. It was too hard for him, though. So he asked you to write a program that, for each given MML sequence, prints the shortest MML sequence (i.e. MML sequence containing minimum number of characters) that expresses the same music as the given one. Note that the final values of octave, volume, and default duration can be differrent from the original MML sequence.

Somewhere in the sky, KM kingdom built n floating islands by their highly developed technology. The islands are numbered from 1 to n . The king of the country, Kita_masa, can choose any non-negative real number as the altitude for each island, as long as the sum of the altitudes is greater than or equals to H . For floating the island i to the altitude h_i , it costs b_i h_i . Besides, it costs |h_i - h_j|c_{i,j} for each pair of islands i and j since there are communications between these islands. Recently, energy prices are rising, so the king, Kita_masa, wants to minimize the sum of the costs. The king ordered you, a court programmer, to write a program that finds the altitudes of the floating islands that minimize the cost.

There is a grid that consists of W \times H cells. The upper-left-most cell is (1, 1) . You are standing on the cell of (1,1) and you are going to move to cell of (W, H) . You can only move to adjacent lower-left, lower or lower-right cells. There are obstructions on several cells. You can not move to it. You cannot move out the grid, either. Write a program that outputs the number of ways to reach (W,H) modulo 1,000,000,009. You can assume that there is no obstruction at (1,1) .

A tree is one of the most popular data structures in computer science. Tree itself is very elegant, but it is incompatible with current computer architecture. In the current architecture, memory can be regarded as a one-dimensional array. Since a tree is not a one-dimensional structure, cache efficiency may come into question when we allocate a tree on memory. We can access the data faster if it is contained in cache. Let us consider the allocation of nodes of a tree which achieves high cache performance. We define the cost of allocation for a tree as follows. For simplicity, we regard memory to be separated to blocks each can store at most B nodes of a tree. When we access data in a block, all data in the block are stored in cache and access request to data in the block will be faster. The cost of an access to node u after node v is 0 if u and v are in same block, and 1 otherwise. Initially, cache has no data and the cost of an access to a node is always 1. The cost of the path v_1 , v_2 ,..., v_n is sum of the cost when we access the nodes v_1 , v_2 ,..., v_n in this order. For a tree, we define the cost of allocation as a maximum cost of the paths from root node to each terminal node. The figures below show examples of allocation when B = 4 and node 1 is the root node (it corresponds to the tree described in the third sample input). Each frame represents a block. The left figure is an example of allocation whose cost is 3. It is not optimal, because the right example achieves the optimal allocation cost 2. Given a tree, for each node i in the tree, calculate the minimum cost of allocation when the node i is the root node.

I bought spaghetti for the first time at a local supermarket.According to the information on the package, this spaghetti will be boiled to the most ideal state if you boil it for T seconds.By the way, I am a person who hates electricity, and I don't even have a television, computer, or even a clock at home.Therefore, even when I want to boil spaghetti, I cannot measure the time.I don't like battery-powered watches, so I headed to the hourglass section of the supermarket to buy an hourglass instead.There were N hourglasses in the store.The i-th hourglass seemed to be able to measure exactly x i seconds.There are a lot of things that bother me, but it seems foolish to buy multiple hourglasses for spaghetti, so I think I'll just buy one of these.However, as you all know, once you start measuring with an hourglass, you have no idea what time it is until you finish measuring.Therefore, if I buy and use the i-th hourglass, I can measure x i seconds, 2x i seconds, 3x i seconds, etc. by using the clock consecutively, but conversely, I cannot measure any other times.It seems that the ideal time required for boiling is T seconds, but here we will compromise and allow for an error of within E (< T) seconds.In other words, we would like to use any one hourglass to measure any of the following times: T−E seconds, T−E+1 seconds, T−E+2 seconds, ..., T+E−2 seconds, T+E−1 seconds, T+E seconds.What number hourglass should I buy and use? The input is given in the following format.N is the number of hourglasses.T is the time (seconds) required to ideally boil spaghetti, and E is the allowable error time (seconds) for boiling.x i is the time (in seconds) that the i-th hourglass can measure at one time.Print out which hourglass should be used.If there are multiple such hourglasses, output one of them.If you cannot measure the time using any hourglass, output -1.It is sufficient if you can measure any one of 8 seconds, 9 seconds, 10 seconds, 11 seconds, or 12 seconds.The times that can be measured with the first hourglass are 3 seconds, 6 seconds, 9 seconds, 12 seconds, etc., and the times that can be measured with the second hourglass are 4 seconds, 8 seconds, 12 seconds, 16 seconds, etc., so you can use either hourglass to measure the desired time.In output example 1, the answer is 2, but it is also correct to use 1 as the answer.No matter what hourglass you use, you cannot measure the desired time.

Mr. B loves Othello (for the rules of Othello, please refer to the Othello page on Wikipedia. Regarding "pass", as is written on Wikipedia, it is possible to pass only if a piece cannot be hit, and there is no limit to the number of passes.) Mr. B is interested in how the outcome of the game changes depending on the initial arrangement of the pieces.However, it is difficult to think suddenly in terms of a two-dimensional board, so I decided to think in terms of a one-dimensional board for now.In other words, in this problem, the board is assumed to be a square with a vertical width of 1 and a width of infinite width.In addition, the Othello pieces will be represented by the lowercase letters o and x.Mr. B decided to consider the initial arrangement of Othello on this board by arranging N pieces consecutively and setting the i-th piece from the left as c i ∈ { o , x }.If the first piece is o, find out who will be the winner when both players do their best.The input is given in the following format.c i represents the i-th piece from the left in the initial arrangement.Output the winning piece.Note that this game is guaranteed not to end in a draw.At first, it is o's turn, but there are no squares to hit, so he passes.Next, it is x's turn, and he can hit either the left or right end.However, no matter which way x plays, he cannot beat o.

There are N rabbits who are trying to cross a river using a boat.There are K boats, and we randomly decide who gets on which boat.By the way, rabbits are sensitive to how well they get along with each other, and if they are confined in a small space with other rabbits they don't get along with, they will feel bad.It is known that some pairs of rabbits do not get along well with these N rabbits, and the current assignment may make some of them feel bad.It would be a good idea to think of a better allocation, but first I would like you to find out how many rabbits will feel bad with the current allocation.The input is given in the following format.N is the number of rabbits on the boat.K is the number of boats.m i is the number of rabbits on boat i.bunny i,1 , ..., bunny i,m i represents the number (1 to N) of the rabbit on boat i.R represents the number of pairs of rabbits that are not on good terms.p j , q j represent that rabbit p j and rabbit q j are not on good terms.Print the number of rabbits that make you feel sick.No rabbit is feeling sick.The assignment is the same as in Example 1, but the pairs representing bad relationships are different.In this example, rabbits 2, 3, and 4 feel sick.

Building 10 is a building in a university where members of the J Graduate School work day and night on research.Building 10 was famous for being an old building, but it was finally renovated this year.One day, when everyone was hoping that they would be able to conduct their research in a clean environment after the renovations were completed, news arrived that some members of the K Graduate School would be invading the rooms in Building 10 after the renovations were completed.If the room in Building 10 is invaded by the K Graduate School, the members of the J Graduate School who were working in that room will have to move to Building 2, which is also known to be an older building.In order to stop it somehow, the professors at the J Graduate School decided to pool their powers to protect each room in Building No. 10 from the K Graduate School. Building No. 10 has N rooms lined up in a straight line, numbered 1, 2,...,N in order.Graduate School K will try to invade all of these N rooms.There are M professors in Graduate School J, and each professor can exercise power over the rooms included in a certain section.Rooms where one or more professors exert power can be protected from being invaded by K Graduate School, but rooms where no professor exerts power will be invaded.The professor is busy with research, so I would like to protect all rooms with as few people as possible.Find the minimum number of professors who should exercise their authority to protect all rooms.Please note that even if all professors exercise their authority, it may not be possible to protect all rooms.The input is given in the following format.N is the number of rooms in Building 10, and M is the number of professors in Graduate School J.a i , b i means that professor i can exert power over rooms a i , a i +1, ..., b i .If all the rooms in Building 10 can be protected, print the minimum number of professors who should exercise power in one line.Otherwise, print Impossible on one line.By exerting their power, the second and fourth professors can protect all rooms in Building 10.

E You love rock-paper-scissors (by the way, for the rules of rock-paper-scissors, see the Wikipedia page for rock-paper-scissors).One day, Mr. E learned that there was a rock-paper-scissors tournament in his town, and because he was confident in his rock-paper-scissors skills, he decided to participate right away.However, on the day of the tournament, what awaited E-kun was not a normal rock-paper-scissors game consisting only of the usual ``goo, chop, and pa,'' but a generalized game of rock-paper-scissors consisting of many more moves.Generalized Rock, Paper, Scissors is a game played by two players.First, the two players are told the number N of moves that can be used in this rock-paper-scissors game, and a table showing the winning/losing relationship of the N moves.Then, just like in normal rock-paper-scissors, players put their hands out at the same time, and the winner is determined based on the winning/losing relationship of the hands that are communicated.Repeat this 1,000 times.Points are earned depending on the outcome of a single game of rock-paper-scissors.If you win against your opponent, you get 3 points, and if you are in a relationship with your opponent, you get 1 point.If you lose to your opponent, you will not receive points.Mr. E had completely lost his will to fight due to the rules that he didn't understand, but apparently, even if he couldn't beat his opponent in terms of points in the end, if he got 350 points or more, he could get a prize.It was a pain, so I decided to get 350 points and leave the tournament quickly.The purpose of this problem is to create an AI that plays a generalized game of rock, paper, scissors, and have it compete against an AI prepared by a judge to score 350 points or more.First, input is given in the following format.N is the number of moves in rock-paper-scissors.Each a i,j represents the winning/losing relationship of N hands.a i,j can be -, o, or x, where - indicates that move i draws against move j, o indicates that move i wins against move j, and x indicates that move loses.Play rock-paper-scissors with the AI ​​1,000 times under these conditions.When the program outputs the moves it uses, it can receive the moves used by the judge's AI as input.For example, to use the third move in C/C++, use .Next, if you enter , the variable judge_ai_hand will contain the move used by the judge's AI.Note that the moves in rock, paper, scissors are 1-indexed.Immediately exit the program after completing 1,000 rock-paper-scissors.After that, if the total score obtained is 350 points or more, the answer is determined to be correct.First, the program receives the table of Rock, Paper, Scissors moves.After that, play rock-paper-scissors 1,000 times.Note that in the first rock-paper-scissors game, the contestant's AI beats the judge's AI, but in the 1,000th rock-paper-scissors game, the contestant's AI loses to the judge's AI.

The vast ocean known as the Internet was gradually being painted black.Bot rebellions, a storm of DDOS attacks, the spread of viruses, and repeated wars with crackers have left people and the Internet in tatters.The Internet cannot be controlled by humans.That's why people spent a tremendous amount of time creating a ``girl'' who could restore the Internet.The girl started using the internet.I tried my best to do something about the endlessly wide world of the Internet.Although the girl can only weave twisted pairs of wires by herself, she hopes that once the internet is restored after a long period of time, she will be able to do her best together with everyone.The girl is working hard every day to restore N services that once existed on the Internet.Let the current day be day 0.The extent to which the Internet has been restored is expressed as an index called the degree of recovery, and the current degree of recovery is assumed to be 0.The girl works every day, increasing the recovery level by 1 each day.Any service i that has not yet been restored will be automatically restored when its restoration degree is greater than or equal to w i .When service i is restored, everyone's help will speed up the work for x i days from the day after it is restored, accelerating the increase in the degree of restoration.There are three types of services, and the extent to which the degree of recovery increases depends on the type.Let us represent the types of services with numbers 0, 1, and 2.If the type of service i is t i (∈ {0, 1, 2}), from day d-1 to day d after service i is restored (1 ≤ d ≤ x i ), the degree of recovery increases by 1 if t i =0, d if t i =1, and d 2 if t i =2, independent of the girl's work.Furthermore, if multiple services are being restored at the same time, each service will increase its recovery degree independently and in parallel.The girl thought it would take a ridiculous amount of time for the service to be restored, so she decided to find out what day from now the service would be restored.I was also curious about what the recovery level would be on one day y j, so I decided to check it for Q days as well.The input is given in the following format.First output N lines, and on the i-th line output the date on which service i will be restored.Next, output line Q, and on line j, output what the recovery level was on day y j.If the date when service will be restored exceeds 3,652,425, output "Many years later."To judge this problem, a group of test cases worth 15 points is set.Test cases included in this group satisfy the following constraints in addition to the above constraints.The second service outputs "Many years later" because the recovery date is more than 3,652,425 days.

Shieru the fox is visiting a small village on vacation.What surprised her was the nature of the village's nameplate.There are N houses in the village.For simplicity, we assume that the village is a two-dimensional plane, and that the houses are points on this plane.Each house had a nameplate, which displayed the family name of the house.As Shieru visited the village, he noticed that the nameplate of this village had the following characteristics.Here, the distance between houses is measured using Euclidean distance on a plane.Shieru was curious about how many types of nameplates there were in this village, but realized that this village was surprisingly large, so he decided to use a calculator to find the answer.The input is given in the following format.N is the number of houses.R is a value related to constraints on the placement of houses.(x i , y i ) represents the coordinates of house i.Find the number of types of nameplates that exist in the village.To judge this problem, a group of test cases worth 15 points is set.Test cases included in this group satisfy the following constraints in addition to the above constraints.Houses 1, 2, and 3, house 4, and house 5 have different nameplates.There are three nameplates in total.

K, a student in the magic department of a certain university, lives in a vast mansion.There was a forest behind the mansion, but there were some places where no trees were growing.Mr. K felt that it would not look good from the mansion as it was, so he decided to plant a tree where there was no tree.K, who is an aspiring wizard, can summon familiars, so he decided to have them do the work of planting trees.A forest can be regarded as a rectangular area with H × W cells.Each cell has either one tree growing on it or no trees growing on it.Mr. K specifies one of these H × W cells and summons his familiar there.However, since K is still an inexperienced person, he is unable to adjust his magical power, and whenever he summons familiars, he always summons five familiars.To make matters worse, the familiar that K-kun summons is perverse: if there is no tree growing in the cell he visits, he will plant a tree there, but if there is already a tree growing in the cell he visits, he will erase the tree in that cell.Four of the summoned familiars start from the designated cell and are scattered north, south, east, and west in a straight line, visiting each cell one by one.These familiars disappear when you leave the forest.The remaining familiars only visit the specified cells and then immediately disappear.To be more precise, when Mr. K summons a familiar to cell (i, j), an operation is performed for H+W-1 cells in the i-th row or j-th column such that if no tree is growing, a tree is planted, and if a tree is growing, the tree is deleted.Summoning requires a lot of magical power, so I want to cover the forest with trees in as few summons as possible.Which cell should K summon his familiar to so that the forest can be completely covered with trees with the minimum number of summons?The input is given in the following format.If a i,j is 1, it means that a tree is growing in cell (i,j), and if it is 0, it means that there is no tree.If it is impossible to completely cover the forest with trees, print Impossible on one line.If not, output the summoning procedure that minimizes the number of summons in the following format on line H.b i,j must be 0 or 1; 1 means to summon the familiar to cell (i,j), and 0 means not to summon it.Note that there is no point in summoning a familiar to the same location twice.If there are multiple summoning procedures that minimize the number of summons, you can output any one.To judge this problem, a group of test cases worth 3 points is set.Test cases included in this group satisfy the following constraints in addition to the above constraints.By summoning familiars to cell (1,1) and cell (4,4), you can completely cover the forest with trees.

I received a request to go on a treasure hunt.Apparently, there is a treasure buried underground within a 2W x 2H area near here.If you use dowsing, which is my specialty, you can find out in which direction the treasure is sleeping from your current location.However, my ability is greatly affected by my physical condition that day, and today when I dowsed, it seemed like I would have an error of up to E degrees.Also, there are limits to my physical strength.You can only dowsing up to 200 times a day.I'm not good at thinking.I would like you to give me instructions on where to dowse to find treasure.The initial input is given in the following format.All inputs are given as real numbers with up to 12 decimal places.This input indicates that the coordinates (x,y) where the treasure is buried satisfy and that the error of the dowsing result is less than E degrees.From now on, you can specify where you want to dowse and get the results, or you can say you have determined the location of the treasure.Perform dowsing at the point (x,y) and use to obtain the result.Next, you can obtain the dowsing results by setting .The resulting angle degree is generated by the uniform distribution of [θ-E,θ+E], where θ is the direction of the treasure from (x,y).If dowsing is performed at a location where there is treasure, θ=0.The angle is 0 degrees in the positive direction of the x-axis and +90 degrees in the positive direction of the y-axis.If -180 ≤ degree ≤ 180 is not satisfied, degree is modified to fit within this range.To tell that there is a treasure at point (x,y), use .At this time, the absolute error from the actual treasure position must be less than 0.5 in L ∞ norm.Note that if you output anything other than dowsing or specifying the location of the treasure, it will be judged as a wrong answer.To judge this problem, a group of test cases worth 3 points is set.Test cases included in this group satisfy the following constraints in addition to the above constraints.First, the program receives the treasure area W,H and the dowsing accuracy E.After that, the program performs the first dowsing at (-97.3, -55.0) and obtains information that the direction of the treasure is 45.8 degrees.Next, the program dowses a second time at (44.5, -54.5) and learns that the direction of the treasure is 110.9 degrees.After that, it dowsed several times and finally outputted that the location of the treasure was (4.5, 50.0).

Shieru the fox was fishing in the river.I was able to obtain a long, black fish, so I decided to chop it up on the spot and eat it as sashimi.A fish has a body length of N centimeters.Strangely, this fish had different densities in different parts of its body.When dividing the fish's body into sections 1 cm from the head and numbering them as 1, 2, ..., N, the weight of the i-th part of the body was w_i.Let the i-th to j-th parts of the fish be expressed as [i..j].Initially, Shieru can be considered to have only one fish body [0..N-1].Shieru wants to cut this and finally get the fish body divided into N parts [0..0], [1..1], ..., [N-1..N-1].Since Shieru is out in the field and doesn't have a cutting board, he decides to use the following acrobatic method to cut the fish's body: First, take one piece of the fish's body.Let this be [i..j].The body of this fish must be at least 2 centimeters long.That is, j-i ≥ 1.Then throw it into the air.He then holds a Japanese sword in his hand and cuts the floating fish in half.At this time, Shieru can cut the fish at any position using his strong motor nerves, but he must always cut at 1cm intervals.As a result, the original fish body [i..j] is divided into two parts, [i..k] and [k+1..j], at an arbitrary cutting position k (i ≤ k < j).When cutting a fish's body in two using this acrobatic method, the cost is equal to the weight of the original body (= w i +w i+1 +...+w j ).Shieru wants to minimize the total cost of cutting all parts of the fish into 1 cm pieces.The input is given in the following format.N is the body length of the fish.w i is the weight of the i-th part of the fish.Output the minimum total cost to cut the fish into N pieces.To judge this problem, a group of test cases worth 3 points is set.Test cases included in this group satisfy the following constraints in addition to the above constraints.First, cut the whole fish between the second and third parts.This costs 1+2+3 = 6.Next, cut between the first and second parts.This costs 1+2=3.This costs a total of 6+3=9 and is the best option.

A unique rally game is popular at KU University.The circuit that is the stage of this game has N rest stops and M roads, and the i-th road is between the f i-th rest stop and the t i-th rest stop.There is one checkpoint on every road, and when you pass the checkpoint on road i, the score of p i is added by XOR no matter which direction you go.Added using XOR.I said it twice because it's important.In this game, you can pass the same rest stop or road as many times as you want, and even if you reach the goal you can continue the rally, but you are prohibited from bypassing checkpoints along the way to go to another rest stop.Therefore, the reason why it is so popular is that you have to think hard about what route to take to get a high score.This time, there are Q participants, and the jth participant seems to start from rest area a j and go to rest area b j, which is the goal.As someone on the management side, I decided to research the highest scores of each participant in advance.The input is given in the following format.The rest area number is 0-indexed.Output Q lines, and in the jth line output the maximum score obtained on the route from the a jth rest stop to the b jth rest stop.A group of test cases worth 60 points is set to judge this problem.Test cases included in this group satisfy the following constraints in addition to the above constraints.

A college student who loves 2D, commonly known as 2D-kun, has replaced the Galapagos mobile phone he had been using for many years with a smartphone.When Mr. 2D operated a smartphone for the first time, he noticed that the method of inputting characters was slightly different from that of older mobile phones.Smartphones take advantage of the ability to touch and flick the screen and have adopted a method called flick input (flick means ``slipping your finger on the screen'').Flick input is a character input method as shown below.In flick input, input is performed using 10 buttons 0-9 (# and * are omitted this time), as shown in Figure A(a).Each button is assigned one A-W row as shown in the figure.A single button can be operated using the method shown in Figure A(b).In other words, .Figure A(c) shows how to perform flick input for all buttons.Please note that to output "n", flick button 0 upwards.Your job, given a string indicating a flick input operation, is to find the string that is output as a result of that operation.

2D enthusiasts from R University (2DRespecters) will participate in a programming practice camp held at Atsu University.At this practice camp, participants bring their own programming problems and use them for practice.This year, the members of 2DRespecters ran to be in charge of the B questions, which are easy to write, so we decided to compete for the position of B question solver.The stage of the battle is a certain vending machine.This vending machine sells only one type of juice for 90 yen, and is famous for its affordable price.However, only two coins, 10 yen coins and 100 yen coins, can be inserted into the vending machine, and change can only be paid in these coins.Inside the vending machine, there is a 10 yen coin storage for storing 10 yen coins and a 100 yen coin storage for storing 100 yen coins, each of which initially stores T 10 yen coins and H 100 yen coins.Only one coin can be inserted into the vending machine at a time, and the inserted coin is stored in the internal storage corresponding to the coin.Initially, the balance displayed in the vending machine is 0 yen, and when you insert a coin, the balance in the vending machine increases by the amount of the inserted coin.As a result of inserting coins, if the balance in the vending machine becomes 90 yen or more, one can of juice will come out of the juice outlet, and at the same time, coins with exactly the same amount as the remaining amount in the vending machine minus 90 yen will come out of the change outlet as change.Change at this time is paid using as many 10 yen coins as possible within the range that can be paid with the coins in the internal storage.After the change is paid, the balance in the vending machine returns to 0 yen.However, this vending machine will break if you perform the following operations.Now that we have finished introducing the vending machine, we will explain the game that N 2DRespecters members participated in.At the beginning of the game, each member i of 2DRespecters has t i 10-yen coins and h i 100-yen coins.In this game, all N people take turns inserting one coin into a vending machine.At this time, insert member 1, member 2,...., member N in this order.When all N people have finished inserting one coin at a time, they insert coins one at a time in the same order as last time, and the same goes for the third week onwards.When inserting coins, if you have 10 yen coins, be sure to insert 10 yen coins, and if you do not have 10 yen coins, insert 100 yen coins.When member i inserts a coin, if change comes out at the change outlet, member i receives all the change, leaves the outlet empty, and then moves on to the next member's turn.In this game, the first member to meet the following conditions will be the winner and will be in charge of problem B.Additionally, the vending machine can store an unlimited amount of juice, and the 100 yen coin storage can store an unlimited number of 100 yen coins.Your job is to find out who is in charge of problem B.A fierce battle for Problem B, at the cost of a meager coin, begins now.By the way, the person in charge of problem B will make sure to enjoy the juice you purchase later.

Yayoi Takasugi is a super popular idol.There is one thing she is not good at.It's a high place...She has an extreme fear of heights.This time, due to the producer's clumsiness, she was forced to take on the following challenge on a variety show.This time's location shoot will take place in a certain room of a certain ninja mansion.The floor of this room is covered with square tiles, and some tiles have fallen out, creating holes.If you fall through this hole, you will find yourself upside down in a pond several meters below.Yayoi's challenge is to start from a designated location in this room, collect all the scrolls placed in the room, and bring them to the goal point.Yayoi can move around the room according to the following rules.Yayoi wants to finish this challenge as soon as possible.Your job is to help Yayoi by writing a program to find the shortest time to complete this challenge.

Anipero Summer Live, commonly known as Anipero, is the largest anime song live event in Japan, where various anime song artists gather.2D, who loves anime songs, decided to go to Anipero again this year, just like last year.He has already purchased m bottles of psyllium to enjoy Anipero.A psyllium is a stick that glows due to a chemical reaction when broken.By shaking the psyllium in time with the rhythm, you can liven up the live performance and increase your level of satisfaction.All the psylliums he purchased this time had the following properties.2D decided to think about the following problem to see how much satisfaction he could get from this year's Anipero by using the limited psyllium wisely depending on the song.He predicts n songs that will be played during the live performance.Each song is 5 minutes long.n songs continue to play continuously, and the time between songs can be considered to be 0 minutes.Each song is given the following three parameters.Just because you shake the psyllium doesn't necessarily mean you'll feel more satisfied.If you shake it, it may actually disrupt the atmosphere of the venue and reduce satisfaction.The same thing can be said if you don't shake it.Psyllium must be used according to the following rules.2D was able to predict the songs for the live show, but he was so tired from just doing that that he didn't feel like solving the problems.Your job is to write a program on his behalf to find the maximum satisfaction possible from this year's live performances.

It has been several centuries since we, the human race, have been in gradual decline.The Earth already belongs to a "Programmer".Programmers with an average height of 170 cm, 7 heads, high intelligence, and a love of coding.I returned to my hometown of Nibunki to take on the role of "Esui", an international civil servant who plays an important role between programmers and people.I chose this job because I thought it would be easy since it was a job that even my grandfather's age could still do.One day, the programmers handed me what looked like two design documents.According to the programmer, various information can be easily exchanged using text and scripts.The first design document was an explanation of the file that represents the sentence structure.The file name of this file ends in .dml and is called a DML file.Inside a DML file, the structure of the text is expressed by sandwiching the text between strings called tags.Tags include a start tag and an end tag, and are represented by elements.In this case, the start tag name and end tag name are represented by the same character string.Tags can be nested, such as .Also, structures like are not allowed.Tag names in DML files can be arbitrary character strings, except for some special ones.There are five special tags:Tag names are expressed using only uppercase and lowercase letters of the alphabet.Spaces will never appear in the tag name.The character strings that appear in the DML file are uppercase and lowercase letters of the alphabet, spaces, '<', '>', and '/'.It is also possible that tags with the same name exist.Strings other than tags enclosed in script tags are output left-aligned from the top left of the screen (0, 0), and are not line-broken until the edge of the screen or the br tag appears.The second design document was an explanation of the DS file that represents the behavior when a button in the DML file is pressed.A DS file is a list of subroutines.A semicolon marks the end of an expression.For example, suppose there is a sentence surrounded by ? in a DML file.The possible expressions in this case are the following four assignment expressions.Assigning a boolean value to visible changes whether the contents of that tag are displayed or not.If it is no longer displayed, subsequent sentences will be shifted to the left.When the currently displayed DML file is rewritten for the first time or by clicking a link tag, the initial values ​​are all set to true.'!=' represents a negative assignment.In the above example, the 1st and 4th lines, and the 2nd and 3rd lines are equivalent.An expression can also change multiple values ​​at the same time, as shown below.At this time, processing is performed in order from the right.In other words, it is equivalent to two lines of .However, this representation is for convenience, and the tag is not specified at the rightmost position of the sentence in the script.Also, is equivalent to .Tags are specified by narrowing down with '.'.For example, refers to a title tag surrounded by dml tags and body tags.However, script tags and br tags are not used for filtering or specified.At this time, please note that the elements to be narrowed down are not necessarily enclosed directly.For example, when you say , you can refer to c using either a.b.c or a.c.Also, if there are tags with the same name, there may not be only one specified tag.If the specified tag does not exist, it will not affect the display, but if it appears when changing multiple values ​​at the same time, it will be evaluated as if it existed.The BNF looks like this:It looks like it's going to give me a headache.Why not?is that so.After the first DML file is displayed, the user clicks on the coordinates ( x , y ) on the screen.Then, the screen will change depending on the link or button at that location.What if I click somewhere else?Nothing will happen.As expected.So, what can you do?I decided to watch over him while giving him his favorite drink, Enajidrink.

You are set to play mahjong against Mr. Takasu, a gambler who is rumored to be a natural disaster that descended into the darkness.Since Mr. Takasu was rumored to be a natural disaster, he proposed a game using strange mahjong tiles called Takasu tiles.Mahjong is a game in which players each hold a number of tiles called hand tiles, and victory is secured by moving the tiles in your hand faster than your opponent's in the form of an agari.The front side of a mahjong tile has something written on it, and the back side of the tile has nothing written on it.Therefore, when you play mahjong, it is a good idea to face the front side of the tiles in your hand toward you and the back side toward the player.This way, only you will know what the tiles are in your hand.The real thrill of mahjong is that players read each other's arrangements and have fun doing so.However, Takasu tiles are transparent mahjong tiles.Since the Takasu tiles are transparent, each player can see the tiles in their opponent's hand.This makes it impossible to enjoy reading arrangements with each other.``This is no longer mahjong.'' You are not satisfied with the idea of ​​using Takasu tiles, but after many twists and turns with Mr. Takasu, you decide to play a game using a mix of Takasu tiles and regular mahjong tiles.To begin with, you decide to predict what the Agari tile in Mr. Takasu's hand will be.Mr. Takasu's hand consists of Takasu tiles and regular tiles.To simplify the problem, assume that Takasu tiles and ordinary tiles consist of 4 tiles each of 12 types numbered 1 to 12.For a hand consisting of 3 n +2 tiles, if the following three conditions are met, the hand tile is Agari.For example, the 14 tiles like are all made up of Takasu tiles.In this case, you can make one combination of two tiles and four groups as shown below, which is Agari.Next, I will give an example of a hand tile that is a mixture of ordinary tiles and Takasu tiles.The 14 tiles like this are made up of 12 Takasu tiles and 2 regular tiles (*).In this case, two normal tiles may be 8 and 9 as shown below, so one combination of two tiles and a group of four can be made, which may be Agari.For the same example, the two regular tiles could be 8 and 8, as shown below, so it could be an agari of the form:However, since only up to four of the same 7 tiles can be used, the following form of agari is not possible.When Mr. Takasu's 3 n +1 tiles are given as input, consider adding 1 tile to Mr. Takasu's hand to create 3 n +2 tiles.At this time, find all the additional tiles (Agari tiles) that can make 3 n + 2 tiles into Agari.

Code Art Online (CAO) is an online RPG in which you advance your adventure by solving various problems through programming.Because CAO was the world's first game to create a virtual reality space, it was extremely popular and sold out, and users who purchased the game were sure to enjoy this world.However, the players who dive into the game hear a terrifying oracle from the game master."Unless one of you players solves the last problem of the last dungeon, you will not be able to escape from this virtual space. And in this world, WA, TLE, and RE mean death in the real world. Please approach the problem with caution." You are one of the CAO players, and you are doing your best to conquer the game in order to clear this game and return to the real world.One day, you are faced with the following problem.This time, a party was formed with n members to conquer a new dungeon.You are also one of this party.In order to enter the dungeon we are about to conquer, we must pass through a hole visible from the cliff.If you can pass through the hole safely, you can enter the dungeon, but if you are unable to enter the hole and touch other parts, death awaits you.Furthermore, if someone passes through one hole, that hole closes, so each person has to go into a separate hole.Let's consider holes and people on a two-dimensional plane.The shapes of the holes are circles of various sizes, as shown in Figure G-1.Input is given the length of the radius.The human shape is given by a polygonal cross-section (not necessarily convex), as shown in Figure G-2.In the input, vertices are given counterclockwise to represent the shape of the polygon.You can assume that any three points of a polygon do not line up on a straight line.Furthermore, it is assumed that polygonal line segments do not intersect with each other for any purpose other than forming vertices.You can freely rotate it to pass it through the hole (however, rotation in the z-axis direction is prohibited).Assume that even if the vertices of the polygon representing a person are on the circumference of the hole, it can pass through the hole.You, who have the highest algorithm skills among the party, decide to solve a problem to determine whether everyone can pass through the hole safely.If it is possible to pass through the hole safely, please indicate which person should pass through which hole.*You won't die in this contest, but if you make a mistake, please solve it as if you were going to die.

Taro is an elementary school student who has just learned how to play kakezan.For some reason, he likes playing cards, so when he sees numbers, he wants to play cards.Recently, he seems to like performing the following processing on integers greater than or equal to 0.(Processing flow) Taro seems to like this process, but I can't predict how many times Step 2 needs to be repeated, and I think it might have to be repeated an infinite number of times.So, I asked you, Taro's older brother and a university student, how many times you have to repeat step 2 for every integer n greater than or equal to 0.Given Q integers N 1 .. N Q greater than or equal to 0, your task is to find how many times step 2 is performed on each integer before the process ends.If you need an infinite number of steps, output -1.It is also necessary to take into account the results of segmentation that result in a 0 at the beginning of the digit.For example, when n=1024, calculating 1*024, 10*24, and 102*4 each yields 24,240,408, so 408 is selected and this becomes the next n.

Full Search Sister is a very talented woman.The older sister can easily count up to several thousand routes on a grid-like road.You and full exploration sister are now in a room covered with hexagonal tiles.The older sister seems very excited to see a hexagon for the first time.Unaccustomed to expressing the arrangement of hexagons in coordinates, the older sister expressed the room using the coordinate system shown in Figure 1.You want to move from point to point on this coordinate system.However, your sister will tell you the direction she wants to move every minute.A regular lady would give you directions to avoid passing through the same coordinates.However, the lady who is not familiar with this coordinate system simply tells you the direction corresponding to the remainder of dividing |x×y×t| (x: x coordinate, y: y coordinate, t: elapsed time [minutes] since the first instruction) by 6 (corresponding to the number shown in the diagram), and she guides you in a completely random direction.You don't want to hurt your sister, so you want to follow her instructions as much as possible to reach your destination.You are allowed to do the following seven actions.Immediately after your sister gives you the instructions, you always do one of these actions.There is furniture in the room, and you cannot move into the tiles on which the furniture is placed.Also, movement such that the absolute value of the y-coordinate exceeds ly or the absolute value of the x-coordinate exceeds lx is not allowed.However, the older sister sometimes instructs such movements.Ignoring the instructions means moving in a different direction than the one indicated by the older sister, or staying where you are.Print the minimum number of times you need to ignore instructions to reach your destination.If it is impossible to reach the destination, output -1.

Taro came to a certain square to look for treasure.There are many treasures buried in this square, but Taro has the latest equipment, so he knows where all the treasures are buried.The plaza is very large, so Taro decides to pick an area and search for treasure, but since there are so many treasures, he can't immediately tell which one is in that area.So Taro decided to count the number of treasures in that area.

Takahashi-kun will be sightseeing in New York with Ringo-chan.Takahashi didn't have any particular hopes for sightseeing, but at Ringo's enthusiastic invitation, he decided to go sightseeing at the ``short distance'' spots shown on the map below.This "long narrow part" is very long and narrow and can be considered a straight line.Additionally, Hosonagai-dokoro has a starting point at one end and a finishing point at the other, and several sightseeing horse-drawn carriages run from the starting point to the finishing point.This carriage operates according to the following rules.In addition, the following constraints are satisfied regarding the ``short and wide place'' and ``slightly wide place'' mentioned earlier.Hearing this, Takahashi realized that by adjusting the departure times of the carriages while adhering to the rules for carriage operation mentioned above, he could reduce the time it took from the first carriage's departure until all the carriages arrived.To find out the results obtained by Mr. Takahashi, you are given the number of horse-drawn carriages n, the parameter S i regarding the speed of each horse-drawn carriage, the total number m of ``slightly wider areas'' on the ``longer distance place'', and the distance D i of each ``slightly wider area'' from the starting point.Your job is to write a program that finds the minimum value of the time it takes from the departure of the first carriage until all the carriages arrive.Although Ringo-chan thoroughly enjoyed sightseeing at Hosonagai-dokoro, Takahashi-kun only said, ``It was Hosonagai...'' after the trip.

The year is 3xxx, and the highly developed civilization has reached a period of stagnation.Historians have decided to learn the wisdom of the past in order to overcome this situation.What we focused on was the materials left behind by geniuses from the early days of computers.This document contains a calculation formula and I would like to know the result, but unfortunately some of the text has faded and is no longer readable.Since I had no choice, I decided to find the largest possible number as a calculation result.The calculation formula is written in binary numbers, and there are three types of operations: addition, subtraction, and multiplication.Parentheses are also used, but numbers in parentheses are never the only characters.To be precise, it must satisfy the grammar defined in the BNF below.Due to the limits of computing power of computers at the time, the numbers must be integers between 0 and 2 and 10, and they do not fall outside of this range during calculations.However, calculations are performed first, and multiplication is performed before addition and subtraction.Otherwise, calculate from left to right.

Taro is an elementary school student who doodles on the backs of flyers.One day, Taro came up with the following game.Taro came up with this game, but it takes Taro a lot of time to complete it.So, I asked you, a college student, for help.As Taro's older brother and a university student, your job is as follows.To consider the exact situation, you derived the cost of writing a circle in a certain square and the cost of erasing a circle in a certain square.Using this cost, consider a procedure that minimizes the cost of operations required to clear this game.Write a program that outputs the minimum cost and the steps to achieve that cost.Regarding the output, any operation and order may be used as long as the procedure achieves the minimum cost.

Time passed and Taro became a high school student.I became interested in computer science due to the influence of my older brother, who was a university student.Taro continued reading his computer science textbook and learned of a famous problem called the ``Longest Increasing Subsequence Problem.''Taro understood this problem, but he was wondering if he could create a similar problem himself.So Taro created the following problem through trial and error.For example, consider the case where the sequence A is {5,-4,10,-3,8}.Prepare a sequence C that represents the position of the break, and set C={2,4}.At this time, sequence A is divided into parts (5,-4), (10,-3), and (8), and when you add these parts together, you get 1, 7, and 8, respectively, and the resulting sequence B becomes {1, 7, 8}.Taro named this problem the "longest increasing sequence problem" and devised an algorithm to solve it.Then, I contacted you to check it out for you now that you are a working adult.Your job is to create a program that solves the problems created by Taro, who has grown up.Since its job is to check, it also outputs the positions of m-1 delimiters.Note that there may be cases where multiple such division methods can be considered for the optimal m, but if this m is output correctly, it is sufficient to output one of the possible ways.

Taro has decided to move.Taro has a lot of luggage, so he decided to hire a moving company to transport it.There are various weights of luggage, so I asked them to place the luggage in order from the lightest to the lowest to make it easier to understand, but the moving company left the luggage in different order.So Taro tried to rearrange his luggage, but the luggage was heavy and he needed physical strength to carry it.Each piece of luggage can be moved from its current location to any location you like, such as between other items or to the edge of the item, but moving a certain item requires as much physical strength as the weight of that item.Taro doesn't have much physical strength, so he decided to think of a way to arrange the items in order from the lightest to the lightest without using as much physical strength as possible.

The University of Aizu Elementary School (Aizu University Elementary School) is famous as one of Japan's leading training schools for competitive programmers.Of course, even when participating in sports day, it is essential to practice algorithms.As the head of the competitive programming department, you of course want to win this competition.This time we will focus on a certain sport.A certain competition is a traditional competition held at Aizu University and Small School.There are V cones placed in the schoolyard.Some pairs of cones are connected by arrows drawn with white lines.The tip of the arrow is on only one side, and an integer is also written on it.The same pair of cones may be connected to multiple arrows.Competitors randomly select a cone and begin moving.To move, move from the cone where the contestant is located on the arrow in that direction and move on to the next cone.You can follow the same cone and the same arrow over and over again.After moving from cone to cone, the contestant may choose to move further or end the move.The objective of this competition is to get a score of K or higher by following the arrows.Each time you follow the arrow, the score is added to the integer value.The contestant who achieves a score of K or higher with fewer arrows wins.If the number of arrows is the same, the contestant with the higher score wins.If the contestant makes the optimal move according to this rule, output how many arrows the contestant should pass through.Also, if the number of arrows traveled is less than 100, output all the cones that should be traveled in order.Although there may be multiple optimal movements, the results of any movement may be output.If there is no move that increases the score to K or higher, output -1.Additionally, all cones are numbered from 0 to V-1, and all are green (meaningful).

A certain country, which has been frequently invaded by unidentified creatures, has decided to protect its important facilities with new defensive weapons.This weapon can damage cryptids by filling a polygonal area with a special gas.If the concentration of the gas changes during the invasion of unidentified creatures, it will deal damage equal to the absolute value of the difference in concentration.No damage occurs when the gas is moving in an area with the same concentration.Current technology can only maintain a constant concentration of gas, so a certain country decided to introduce multiple new defense weapons.The gas concentration is the same for all weapons, and the concentrations included in the areas of multiple weapons are added together.Based on the appearance point of the unidentified creature and the location of the important facility, I would like you to find the minimum amount of damage that the unidentified creature will receive when it invades the important facility.However, the invasion route of unidentified creatures shall not include the vertices of polygons or intersections with other polygons.Also, it does not invade along the sides of a polygon.

Idols --- girls' eternal dreams.However, only a handful make it to the top.You have decided to step into this survival world as an idol producer.And today, you will be taking your idol to an important audition.The deciding factors for the audition are visuals, dance, and vocals.During the audition, there are m appeal times, and each idol can perform a visual appeal, dance appeal, or vocal appeal for each appeal time.If you perform a visual appeal, the idol's visual points will increase by the visual value she has, if you perform a dance appeal, the dance points will increase by the dance value she has, and if you perform a vocal appeal, the idol's vocal points will increase by the vocal value she has.After m appeals are completed, the 3 idols with the highest visual points among the N idols will receive 5 audition points, the 3 idols with the highest dance points will receive 3 audition points, and the 3 idols with the highest vocal points will receive 2 audition points.On the other hand, if the visual point is at the bottom, the audition point will be deducted by 1 point, if the dance point is at the bottom, the audition point will be deducted by 1 point, and if the vocal point is at the bottom, the audition point will be deducted by 1 point.(Even if the audition point is less than 1, 1 point will be deducted from there).At the beginning of the audition, each idol has 0 visual points, 0 dance points, and 0 vocal points.You are the producer of Idol 1 and can instruct which appeals to perform at each appeal time.Idols other than Idol 1 will randomly perform one of the appeals with equal probability at each appeal time (that is, each will be performed with a 1/3 probability).If Idol 1's visual, dance, and vocal points are equal to other idols, Idol 1 will always be ranked lower.Find the expected value when you instruct idol 1 to maximize the expected value of audition points earned.The fate of idols depends on your instructions!By the way, all instructions must be given before the audition begins, and no changes may be made to the instructions during the audition.In other words, it is not possible to look at how other idols appeal after the audition begins and then issue instructions that will maximize the expected value in that case.

Mr. Morishita was in trouble... He had Mr. Anehara write a program, but the program crashed.Mr. Anehara wrote a program that reads an expression in reverse Polish notation and outputs the calculation result.According to the crash log, it seems that the cause was a division by 0.This may be because Mr. Morishita entered the wrong formula, or perhaps there is a bug in the program written by Mr. Anehara.I decided to read the program written by Mr. Anehara, but it seems that his program is written in an unfamiliar language called assembly, and just looking at it makes my head pound.So Mr. Morishita decided to ask you to write a program to check whether the formula is correct.An incorrect formula means that if you calculate according to that formula, there is a possibility that you will end up with division by 0.The code written by Mr. Anehara runs on a very simple computer; addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division are performed using integers, and the results are saved as 8-bit unsigned integers.For example, 255+1 becomes 0, and 3/2 becomes 1.Ah, Anehara-san doesn't have it.

There is now a card with n numbers written on it.Think about making numbers by arranging some or all of these.Find the sum of all the numbers created at this time.For example, if you have the numbers 1 and 2, you will have four numbers: 1, 2, 12, and 21, so they all add up to 36.Even if the numbers are the same after arranging them, if they are arranged in different ways, add them separately.For example, if you have a card named 1 and a card named 11, there are two ways to arrange them side by side to make 111, but each one is added separately.There are no cards with a leading zero, and numbers with a leading zero are not accepted.Divide the answer by 1,000,000,007 and print it.

A gene is a string consisting of A , T , G , and C .Genes in this world are known to strangely follow certain syntactic rules.Syntax rules are given in the form:Symbols are either nonterminal or terminal.Non-terminal symbols are represented by a lowercase string, and terminal symbols are represented by a string of some of the characters A , T , G , C surrounded by " [ " and " ] ".An example syntax rule would be:"Nonterminal symbol i: symbol i_1 symbol i_2 ... symbol i_ni" is called a rule for nonterminal symbol i, and there is exactly one rule for each nonterminal symbol that appears in the syntax rule.A string s "matches" a nonterminal symbol i if there exists a substring {s j } of s such that s = s 1 + s 2 + ... + s ni , and s j ( 1 ≤ j ≤ n i ) matches symbol j in the rule.When a string s "matches" a terminal symbol, it means that the string consists of one character, and that character is included in the string representing the terminal symbol.A string follows a syntax rule if it matches the nonterminal symbol 1.Rule i does not include a nonterminal symbol j ( j ≤ i ) among its symbols.You are given a syntax rule and four integers Na , Nt , Ng , Nc .Following the syntax rules, find the remainder when dividing the total number of genes by 1,000,000,007 such that there are exactly Na A's, Nt T's, Ng G's, and Nc C's.

YAML (YAML Ain't Markup Language) is a format for expressing objects as strings.You will be given an object represented by a subset of YAML and a query that specifies properties, so please answer with the values ​​of the specified properties.A subset of YAML follows syntax rules expressed in extended BNF notation, such as:'
' represents a newline character.mapping(n) represents an object, and mapping-item(n) contained in mapping(n) represents a property contained in the object.mapping-item(n): indent(n) key ':' ' ' string '
' indicates that the value of the property represented by key is a string represented by string.mapping-item(n): indent(n) key ':' '
' mapping(m) indicates that the value of the property represented by key is the object represented by mapping(m).An object cannot contain two or more properties with the same key.A query that specifies a property is given in the form of alternating ' . ' and key , such as , which represents ``(..omitted..) property with key n of the object that is the value of the property with key 1 of the object given in yaml, whose value is the value of the property with key 2.''Furthermore, for a certain i ( 1 ≤ i ≤ n - 1 ), if the value of the property represented by .key_1.key_2.(..omitted..).key_i is not an object, or if it is an object but does not include the property key_i+1, it is assumed that the property represented by .key_1.key_2(..omitted..).key_n does not exist.

Hakone Ekiden is a traditional New Year's event in Japan.In the Hakone Ekiden, 10 runners from each team aim for the goal while linking sashes at each relay station.During the TV broadcast, the passing ranking of each team is displayed at the relay station, as well as the change in ranking from the previous relay station.Then, look at it and answer how many possible orders there are for each team to pass through the previous relay station.The number of possible passing orders can be very large, so divide your answer by dividing by 1,000,000,007.

One room uses an electronic lock system that uses an IC card to open and close the door.This system works as follows.When each user holds their IC card against the door, the ID of that IC card is passed to the system.When the ID is registered, the system will unlock the door if it is locked, lock it if it is not, and output a message for each.If the ID is not registered, a message indicating that it is not registered will be output and the door will not be unlocked or locked.Now, N IDs (U 1 , U 2 , ..., U N ) are currently registered in the system, and it is locked.Assume that an IC card is held up to the door M times, and its IDs are T 1 , T 2 , ..., T M in order.Find what message the system outputs at this time.

FizzBuzz is a game where you say integers greater than or equal to 1 in order according to the following rules.An example of the game progress is shown below.1, 2, Fizz, 4, Buzz, Fizz, 7, 8, Fizz, Buzz, 11, Fizz, 13, 14, FizzBuzz, 16, … The obtained utterances are combined into one string, and the obtained string is called FizzBuzz String.Given the index s, output 20 characters from the sth character of the FizzBuzz String.However, the index shall start from 1, and the length of the obtained character string may be sufficiently large (s+20 or more).

A convex polygon consisting of N vertices is given.The coordinates of each vertex are expressed counterclockwise as (X 1 , Y 1 ), (X 2 ,Y 2 ), ..., (X N , Y N ).Find the coordinates of point P such that no matter what straight line passes through point P to cut the convex polygon, the areas of the two convex polygons obtained after cutting are equal.

A permutation A 1 , A 2 , ..., A N of N numbers from 1 to N is given.The operation reverse(i, j) can be performed on this permutation to reverse the order of the numbers in the interval [i, j] (1 ≤ i ≤ j ≤ N).For example, applying reverse(2, 4) to [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] becomes [1, 4, 3, 2, 5].Calculate the minimum number of operations required to make the permutations sorted in ascending order.

Given a string s=s 1 , s 2 ,…,s n of length n and m queries.Each query q k (1 ≤ k ≤ m) is one of four types: "L++", "L--", "R++", and "R--", and l[k] and r[k] are defined below for the kth query q k.However, l[0]=r[0]=1.In this case, answer how many types of strings are created for m substrings s l[k] , s l[k]+1 , …, s r[k]-1 , s r[k] (1 ≤ k ≤ m).

There is an NxN grid.(Figure for N=3) It costs 1 to move to an adjacent square.However, it cannot be moved diagonally.There are also M squares with obstacles, and you are not allowed to enter those squares.How many ways are there to move from square (1, 1) to square (N, N) with minimum cost?The answer can be large, so take the MOD with 1000000009 (=10 9 +9) and output it.

Given are n integers a 1 , a 2 , …, a n , n integers p 1 , p 2 , …, p n , and an integer m.The operation of selecting the kth integer a k with a probability of p k [%] is performed for each k (1 ≤ k ≤ n), and an integer between 0 and n is selected.Find the expected number of integers between 1 and m that are divisible by at least one of the selected integers.

It was the last day of the summer camp you strayed into the labyrinth on the way to Komaba Campus, the University of Tokyo. The contest has just begun. Your teammates must impatiently wait for you. So you have to escape from this labyrinth as soon as possible. The labyrinth is represented by a grid map. Initially, each grid except for walls and stairs is either on the first floor or on the second floor. Some grids have a switch which can move up or down some of the grids (the grids on the first floor move to the second floor, and the grids on the second floor to the first floor). In each step, you can take one of the following actions: Luckily, you have just found a map of the labyrinth for some unknown reason. Let's calculate the minimum step to escape from the labyrinth, and go to the place your teammates are waiting!

You are given one polygonal line, which is a collection of line segments. Your task is to calculate the sum of areas enclosed by the polygonal line. A point is defined to be "enclosed" if and only if the point is unreachable without crossing at least one line segment from the point at infinity.

You are playing a solitaire puzzle called "Connect", which uses several letter tiles. There are R × C empty cells. For each i (1 ≤ i ≤ R) , you must put a string s i (1 ≤ |s i | ≤ C) in the i -th row of the table, without changing the letter order. In other words, you choose an integer sequence {a j } such that 1 ≤ a 1 < a 2 < ... < a |s i | ≤ C , and put the j -th character of the string s i in the a j -th column (1 ≤ j ≤ |s i |) . For example, when C = 8 and s i = "ICPC", you can put s i like followings. '_' represents an empty cell. For each non-empty cell x , you get a point equal to the number of adjacent cells which have the same character as x . Two cells are adjacent if they share an edge. Calculate the maximum total point you can get.

Given a tree with n (1 ≤ n ≤ 200,000) nodes and a list of q (1 ≤ q ≤ 100,000) queries, process the queries in order and output a value for each output query. The given tree is connected and each node on the tree has a weight w i (-10,000 ≤ w i ≤ 10,000) . Each query consists of a number t i (t i = 1, 2) , which indicates the type of the query , and three numbers a i , b i and c i (1 ≤ a i , b i ≤ n, -10,000 ≤ c i ≤ 10,000) . Depending on the query type, process one of the followings:

There is a cube on a rectangle map with H -rows and W -columns grid. Two special squares a start and a goal are marked on the map. Initially, the cube is on the start square. Let's repeat to roll it and take it to the goal square. Rolling the cube means to select one of four edges which touch the map, and push the cube down without detaching the edge from the map. That is, there are four directions you can move the cube toward. Directions where we can roll the cube are limited depending on each square. An instruction is written in each square and represented by a single character as follows: Regardless of instructions, it is not allowed to roll the cube to outside of the map. On each face of the cube, a string is written. Let's output the string which concatenates strings written on the top face seen during the rollings from the start square to the goal square. Since there may be multiple paths that take the cube to the goal square, choose the minimal string in ascending lexicographic order. Please note that there are cases where no path exists from the start to the goal, or the cases you can make the lexicographically minimal string infinitely longer.

There are twelve types of tiles in Fig. 1. You were asked to fill a table with R × C cells with these tiles. R is the number of rows and C is the number of columns. How many arrangements in the table meet the following constraints?

You are a researcher investigating algorithms on binary trees. Binary tree is a data structure composed of branch nodes and leaf nodes. Every branch nodes have left child and right child, and each child is either a branch node or a leaf node. The root of a binary tree is the branch node which has no parent. You are preparing for your presentation and you have to make a figure of binary trees using a software. You have already made a figure corresponding to a binary tree which is composed of one branch node and two leaf nodes (Figure 1.) However, suddenly the function of your software to create a figure was broken. You are very upset because in five hours you have to make the presentation in front of large audience. You decided to make the figure of the binary trees using only copy function, shrink function and paste function of the presentation tool. That is, you can do only following two types of operations. Moreover, you decided to make the figure using the minimum number of paste operations because paste operation is very time cosuming. Now, your job is to calculate the minimum possible number of paste operations to produce the target figure. For example, the answer for the instance of sample 1(Figure 4) is 3, because you can produce the target figure by following operations and this is minimum.

In the International City of Pipe Construction, it is planned to repair the water pipe at a certain point in the water pipe network. The network consists of water pipe segments, stop valves and source point. A water pipe is represented by a segment on a 2D-plane and intersected pair of water pipe segments are connected at the intersection point. A stop valve, which prevents from water flowing into the repairing point while repairing, is represented by a point on some water pipe segment. In the network, just one source point exists and water is supplied to the network from this point. Of course, while repairing, we have to stop water supply in some areas, but, in order to reduce the risk of riots, the length of water pipes stopping water supply must be minimized. What you have to do is to write a program to minimize the length of water pipes needed to stop water supply when the coordinates of end points of water pipe segments, stop valves, source point and repairing point are given.

In the year 20XX, mankind is hit by an unprecedented crisis. The power balance between the sun and the moon was broken by the total eclipse of the sun, and the end is looming! To save the world, the secret society called "Sun and Moon" decided to perform the ritual to balance the power of the sun and the moon called "Ritual of Sun and Moon". The ritual consists of "Ritual of Sun" and "Ritual of Moon". "Ritual of Moon" is performed after "Ritual of Sun". A member of the society is divided into two groups, "Messengers of Sun" and "Messengers of Moon". Each member has some offerings and magic power. First, the society performs "Ritual of Sun". In the ritual, each member sacrifices an offering. If he can not sacrifice an offering here, he will be killed. After the sacrifice, his magic power is multiplied by the original number of his offerings. Each member must perform the sacrifice just once. Second, the society performs "Ritual of Moon". In the ritual, each member sacrifices all remaining offerings. After the sacrifice, his magic power is multiplied by x p . Here x is the number of days from the eclipse (the eclipse is 0th day) and p is the number of his sacrificed offerings. Each member must perform the sacrifice just once. After two rituals, all "Messengers of Sun" and "Messengers of Moon" give all magic power to the "magical reactor". If the total power of "Messengers of Sun" and the total power of "Messengers of Moon" are equal, the society will succeed in the ritual and save the world. It is very expensive to perform "Ritual of Sun". It may not be able to perform "Ritual of Sun" because the society is in financial trouble. Please write a program to calculate the minimum number of days from the eclipse in which the society can succeed in "Ritual of Sun and Moon" whether "Ritual of Sun" can be performed or not. The society cannot perform the ritual on the eclipse day (0-th day).

Usoperanto is an artificial spoken language designed and regulated by Usoperanto Academy. The academy is now in study to establish Strict Usoperanto, a variation of the language intended for formal documents. In Usoperanto, each word can modify at most one other word, and modifiers are always put before modifiees. For example, with a noun uso ("truth") modified by an adjective makka ("total"), people say makka uso , not uso makka . On the other hand, there have been no rules about the order among multiple words modifying the same word, so in case uso is modified by one more adjective beta ("obvious"), people could say both makka beta uso and beta makka uso . In Strict Usoperanto, the word order will be restricted according to modification costs . Words in a phrase must be arranged so that the total modification cost is minimized. Each pair of a modifier and a modifiee is assigned a cost equal to the number of letters between the two words; the total modification cost is the sum of the costs over all modifier-modifiee pairs in the phrase. For example, the pair of makka and uso in a phrase makka beta uso has the cost of 4 for beta (four letters). As the pair of beta and uso has no words in between and thus the cost of zero, makka beta uso has the total modification cost of 4. Similarly beta makka uso has the total modification cost of 5. Applying the "minimum total modification cost" rule, makka beta uso is preferred to beta makka uso in Strict Usoperanto. Your mission in this problem is to write a program that, given a set of words in a phrase, finds the correct word order in Strict Usoperanto and reports the total modification cost.

One day, during daily web surfing, you encountered a web page which was written in a language you've never seen. The character set of the language was the same as your native language; moreover, the grammar and words seemed almost the same. Excitedly, you started to "decipher" the web page. The first approach you tried was to guess the meaning of each word by selecting a similar word from a dictionary of your native language. The closer two words (although from the different languages) are, the more similar meaning they will have. You decided to adopt edit distance for the measurement of similarity between two words. The edit distance between two character sequences is defined as the minimum number of insertions, deletions and substitutions required to morph one sequence into the other. For example, the pair of "point" and "spoon" has the edit distance of 3: the latter can be obtained from the former by deleting 't', substituting 'i' to 'o', and finally inserting 's' at the beginning. You wanted to assign a word in your language to each word in the web text so that the entire assignment has the minimum edit distance. The edit distance of an assignment is calculated as the total sum of edit distances between each word in the text and its counterpart in your language. Words appearing more than once in the text should be counted by the number of appearances. The translation must be consistent across the entire text; you may not match different words from your dictionary for different occurrences of any word in the text. Similarly, different words in the text should not have the same meaning in your language. Suppose the web page says "qwerty asdf zxcv" and your dictionary contains the words "qwert", "asf", "tyui", "zxcvb" and "ghjk". In this case, you can match the words in the page as follows, and the edit distance of this translation is 3: "qwert" for "qwerty", "asf" for "asdf" and "zxcvb" for "zxcv". Write a program to calculate the minimum possible edit distance among all translations, for given a web page text and a word set in the dictionary.

Mr. Knight is a chief architect of the project to build a new art museum. One day, he was struggling to determine the design of the building. He believed that a brilliant art museum must have an artistic building, so he started to search for a good motif of his building. The art museum has one big theme: "nature and human beings." To reflect the theme, he decided to adopt a combination of a cylinder and a prism, which symbolize nature and human beings respectively (between these figures and the theme, there is a profound relationship that only he knows). Shortly after his decision, he remembered that he has to tell an estimate of the cost required to build to the financial manager. He unwillingly calculated it, and he returned home after he finished his report. However, you, an able secretary of Mr. Knight, have found that one field is missing in his report: the length of the fence required to surround the building. Fortunately you are also a good programmer, so you have decided to write a program that calculates the length of the fence. To be specific, the form of his building is union of a cylinder and a prism. You may consider the twodimensional projection of the form, i.e. the shape of the building is considered to be union of a circle C and a polygon P . The fence surrounds the outside of the building. The shape of the building may have a hole in it, and the fence is not needed inside the building. An example is shown in the figure below.

Let f ( x ) = a 0 + a 1 x + a 2 x 2 + ... + a d x d be the function where each a i (0 ≤ i ≤ d ) is a constant integer (and a d is non-zero) and x is a variable. Your task is to write a program that finds all complex integer solutions of the equation f ( x ) = 0 for a given f ( x ). Here, by complex integers, we mean complex numbers whose real and imaginary parts are both integers.

You are a secret agent from the Intelligence Center of Peacemaking Committee. You've just sneaked into a secret laboratory of an evil company, Automated Crime Machines.
