Your mission is to get a confidential document kept in the laboratory. To reach the document, you need to unlock the door to the safe where it is kept. You have to unlock the door in a correct way and with a great care; otherwise an alarm would ring and you would be caught by the secret police. The lock has a circular dial with N lights around it and a hand pointing to one of them. The lock also has M buttons to control the hand. Each button has a number L i printed on it. Initially, all the lights around the dial are turned off. When the i -th button is pressed, the hand revolves clockwise by L i lights, and the pointed light is turned on. You are allowed to press the buttons exactly N times. The lock opens only when you make all the lights turned on. For example, in the case with N = 6, M = 2, L 1 = 2 and L 2 = 5, you can unlock the door by pressing buttons 2, 2, 2, 5, 2 and 2 in this order. There are a number of doors in the laboratory, and some of them don’t seem to be unlockable. Figure out which lock can be opened, given the values N , M , and L i 's.

Once upon a time, in a fantasy world far, far away, monsters dug caves and dungeons for adventurers. They put some obstacles in their caves so it becomes more difficult and more exciting for the adventurers to reach the goal. One day, Emils, one of the monsters in the caves, had a question about the caves. How many patterns of a cave can they make, by changing the locations of the obstacles in it? Here's the detail of the question. A cave consists of W × H squares. Monsters can put obstacles at some of the squares, so that adventurers can't go into them. The total number of obstacles is fixed, and there can't be two or more obstacles in one square. Adventurers enter the cave from the top-left square, and try to reach the bottom-right square. They can move from one square to any of the four adjacent squares, as long as there are no obstacles in the destination square. There must be at least one path between any two squares that don't have obstacles. There must be no obstacles in the top-left square, nor in right-bottom square. The question is, given the width W and height H of the cave, and the number S of obstacles, how many patterns of the caves the monsters can make. As the obstacles have the same look, they should not be distinguished each other. It was a very interesting mathematical question. Emils couldn't solve this question by himself, so he told it to his colleagues instead. None of them could answer to it, though. After that, the question soon got popular among the monsters working in the caves, and finally, they became unable to sleep well as they always thought about the question. You are requested to write a program that answers to the question.

You happened to get a special bicycle. You can run with it incredibly fast because it has a turbo engine. You can't wait to try it off road to enjoy the power. You planned to go straight. The ground is very rough with ups and downs, and can be seen as a series of slopes (line segments) when seen from a lateral view. The bicycle runs on the ground at a constant speed of V . Since running very fast, the bicycle jumps off the ground every time it comes to the beginning point of a slope slanting more downward than the previous, as illustrated below. It then goes along a parabola until reaching the ground, affected by the gravity with the acceleration of 9.8m/s 2 . It is somewhat scary to ride the bicycle without any preparations - you might crash into rocks or fall into pitfalls. So you decided to perform a computer simulation of the ride. Given a description of the ground, calculate the trace you will run on the ground by the bicycle. For simplicity, it is sufficient to output its length.

You are a magician and have a large farm to grow magical fruits. One day, you saw a horde of monsters, approaching your farm. They would ruin your magical farm once they reached your farm. Unfortunately, you are not strong enough to fight against those monsters. To protect your farm against them, you decided to build magic walls around your land. You have four magic orbs to build the walls, namely, the orbs of Aquamarine (A), Bloodstone (B), Citrine (C) and Diamond (D). When you place them correctly and then cast a spell, there will be magic walls between the orbs A and B, between B and C, between C and D, and between D and A. The walls are built on the line segments connecting two orbs, and form a quadrangle as a whole. As the monsters cannot cross the magic walls, the inside of the magic walls is protected. Nonetheless, you can protect only a part of your land, since there are a couple of restrictions on building the magic walls. There are N hills in your farm, where the orbs can receive rich power of magic. Each orb should be set on the top of one of the hills. Also, to avoid interference between the orbs, you may not place two or more orbs at the same hill. Now, you want to maximize the area protected by the magic walls. Please figure it out.

It's time to arrange the books on your bookshelf. There are n books in the shelf and each book has a unique number; you want to sort the books according to the numbers. You know that the quick sort and the merge sort are fast sorting methods, but it is too hard for you to simulate them by hand - they are efficient for computers, but not for humans. Thus, you decided to sort the books by inserting the book with the number i into the i -th position. How many insertions are required to complete this task?

Trees are sometimes represented in the form of strings. Here is one of the most popular ways to represent unlabeled trees: For example, the tree depicted in the figure below is represented by a string " ((()())()) ". A strange boy Norward is playing with such strings. He has found that a string sometimes remains valid as the representation of a tree even after one successive portion is removed from it. For example, removing the underlined portion from the string " ((() ())( )) " results in " ((())) ", which represents the tree depicted below. However, he has no way to know how many ways of such removal there are. Your task is to write a program for it, so that his curiosity is fulfilled.

One of the questions children often ask is "How many stars are there in the sky?" Under ideal conditions, even with the naked eye, nearly eight thousands are observable in the northern hemisphere. With a decent telescope, you may find many more, but, as the sight field will be limited, you may find much less at a time. Children may ask the same questions to their parents in a spaceship billions of light-years away from the Earth. Their telescopes are similar to ours with circular sight field. It can be rotated freely, that is, the sight vector can take an arbitrary value. Given a set of positions of stars and the spec of a telescope, your task is to determine the maximum number of stars that can be seen through the telescope at a time.

Mr. Haskins is working on tuning a database system. The database is a simple associative storage that contains key-value pairs. In this database, a key is a distinct identification (ID) number and a value is an object of any type. In order to boost the performance, the database system has a cache mechanism. The cache can be accessed much faster than the normal storage, but the number of items it can hold at a time is limited. To implement caching, he selected least recently used (LRU) algorithm: when the cache is full and a new item (not in the cache) is being accessed, the cache discards the least recently accessed entry and adds the new item. You are an assistant of Mr. Haskins. He regards you as a trusted programmer, so he gave you a task. He wants you to investigate the cache entries after a specific sequence of accesses.

Let G be a connected undirected graph where N vertices of G are labeled by numbers from 1 to N . G is simple, i.e. G has no self loops or parallel edges. Let P be a particle walking on vertices of G . At the beginning, P is on the vertex 1. In each step, P moves to one of the adjacent vertices. When there are multiple adjacent vertices, each is selected in the same probability. The cover time is the expected number of steps necessary for P to visit all the vertices. Your task is to calculate the cover time for each given graph G.

Takeshi, a famous craftsman, accepts many offers from all over Japan. However, the tools which he is using now has become already too old. So he is planning to buy new tools and to replace the old ones before next use of the tools. Some offers may incur him monetary cost, if the offer requires the tools to be replaced. Thus, it is not necessarily best to accept all the orders he has received. Now, you are one of his disciples. Your task is to calculate the set of orders to be accepted, that maximizes his earning for a given list of orders and prices of tools. His earning may shift up and down due to sale income and replacement cost. He always purchases tools from his friend's shop. The shop discounts prices for some pairs of items when the pair is purchased at the same time. You have to take the discount into account. The total price to pay may be not equal to the simple sum of individual prices. You may assume that all the tools at the shop are tough enough. Takeshi can complete all orders with replaced tools at this time. Thus you have to buy at most one tool for each kind of tool.

A scientist Arthur C. McDonell is conducting a very complex chemical experiment. This experiment requires a large number of very simple operations to pour water into every column of the vessel at the predetermined ratio. Tired of boring manual work, he was trying to automate the operation. One day, he came upon the idea to use three-pronged tubes to divide the water flow. For example, when you want to pour water into two columns at the ratio of 1 : 1, you can use one three-pronged tube to split one source into two. He wanted to apply this idea to split water from only one faucet to the experiment vessel at an arbitrary ratio, but it has gradually turned out to be impossible to set the configuration in general, due to the limitations coming from the following conditions: Still, Arthur did not want to give up. Although one-to-many division at an arbitrary ratio is impossible, he decided to minimize the number of faucets used. He asked you for a help to write a program to calculate the minimum number of faucets required to pour water into the vessel, for the number of columns and the ratio at which each column is going to be filled.

Major Mickle is commanded to protect a secret base which is being attacked by N tanks. Mickle has an ultimate laser rifle called Mickle's beam . Any object shot by this beam will be immediately destroyed, and there is no way for protection. Furthermore, the beam keeps going through even after destroying objects. The beam is such a powerful and horrible weapon, but there is one drawback: the beam consumes a huge amount of energy. For this reason, the number of shots should be always minimized. Your job is to write a program that calculates the minimum number of shots required to destroy all the enemy tanks. You can assume the following:

Brian Fulk is struggling with a really poor computer for a couple of days. Now he is trying to write a very simple program which receives a positive integer x and returns some of its multiples, say, a 1 x ,..., a N x . However, even such a simple task is not easy on this computer since it has only three arithmetic operations: addition, subtraction and left shift. Let us describe the situation in more details. Initially the computer stores only a given positive integer x . The program Brian is writing will produce a 1 x ,..., a N x , where a 1 ,..., a N are given multipliers, using only the following operations: The program should not generate any value greater than 42 x ; under this constraint he can assume that no overflow occurs. Also, since this computer cannot represent negative values, there should not be subtraction of a greater value from a smaller value. Some of you may wonder where the number 42 comes from. There is a deep reason related to the answer to life, the universe and everything, but we don't have enough space and time to describe it. Your task is to write a program that finds the shortest sequence of operations to produce the multiples a 1 x ,..., a N x and reports the length of the sequence. These numbers may be produced in any order. Here we give an example sequence for the first sample input, in a C++/Java-like language:

You have a deck of 2 N cards (1 ≤ N ≤ 1000000) and want to have them shuffled. The most popular shuffling technique is probably the riffle shuffle, in which you split a deck into two halves, place them in your left and right hands, and then interleave the cards alternatively from those hands. The shuffle is called perfect when the deck is divided exactly in half and the cards are interleaved one-by-one from the bottom half. For example, the perfect riffle shuffle of a deck of eight cards <0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7> will result in a deck <0, 4, 1, 5, 2, 6, 3, 7>. Since you are not so good at shuffling that you can perform the perfect riffle shuffle, you have decided to simulate the shuffle by swapping two cards as many times as needed. How many times you will have to perform swapping at least? As the resultant number will obviously become quite huge, your program should report the number modulo M = 1000003.

You are the owner of a restaurant, and you are serving for N customers seating in a round table. You will distribute M menus to them. Each customer receiving a menu will make the order of plates, and then pass the menu to the customer on the right unless he or she has not make the order. The customer i takes L i unit time for the ordering. Your job is to write a program to calculate the minimum time until all customers to call their orders, so you can improve your business performance.

The currency system in the Kingdom of Yoax-Musty is strange and fairly inefficient. Like other countries, the kingdom has its own currencty unit denoted by K $ (kingdom dollar). However, the Ministry of Finance issues bills for every value between 1 K $ and (2 31 - 1) K $ worth. On the other hand, this system often enables people to make many different values just with a small number of bills. For example, if you have four bills of 1 K $ , 2 K $ , 4 K $ , and 8 K $ worth respectively, you can make any values from 1 K #36; to 15 K $ . In this problem, you are requested to write a program that finds the minimum value that cannot be made with a given set (multiset in a mathematical sense) of bills. For the case with the four bills (1 K $ , 2 K $ , 4 K $ , and 8 K $ ), since you can make any values up to 15 K $ , your program should report 16 K $ .

You are on board a trading ship as a crew. The ship is now going to pass through a strait notorious for many pirates often robbing ships. The Maritime Police has attempted to expel those pirates many times, but failed their attempts as the pirates are fairly strong. For this reason, every ship passing through the strait needs to defend themselves from the pirates. The navigator has obtained a sea map on which the location of every hideout of pirates is shown. The strait is considered to be a rectangle of W × H on an xy-plane, where the two opposite corners have the coordinates of (0, 0) and ( W , H ). The ship is going to enter and exit the strait at arbitrary points on y = 0 and y = H respectively. To minimize the risk of attack, the navigator has decided to take a route as distant from the hideouts as possible. As a talented programmer, you are asked by the navigator to write a program that finds the best route, that is, the route with the maximum possible distance to the closest hideouts. For simplicity, your program just needs to report the distance in this problem.

Find the palindrome number closest to the integer n.Note that a non-negative integer x is a palindrome if the string representing x in decimal notation is equal to the string representing it inverted.For example, numbers such as 0,7,33,10301 are palindromes, and numbers such as 32,90,1010 are not palindromes.

There is a string S.Initially, S is an empty string.Perform the following process n times in order.Add x_i p_i (="("or ")") to the end of S.After performing the processing, determine whether S is a balanced string.``The string is balanced'' is defined as follows.Empty strings are balanced.For balanced strings a and b, a + b (+ represents concatenation of strings) is balanced.For a balanced string a, "(" + a + ")" is balanced.

Sora the cat loves walks.When you walk down the street, you discover something new.The town where Sora lives consists of N squares and N-1 roads.The road exactly connects the two squares, and the roads do not branch or intersect.For a path, the time t it takes to walk, the upper limit m of the number of discoveries that can be made, and the value v of each discovery are specified.When walking along a road from one end to the other, one discovery of value v is obtained on the mth journey, but no discovery is made after the m+1 time.It looks like Sora is going for a walk around town again today.Sora's walk starts from Plaza 1, passes through several paths (maybe 0), and finally returns to Plaza 1.Also, since she feels lonely when the sun goes down, Sora wants to keep her walking time to less than T.Your job as Sora's friend is to find the maximum value of the total value of discoveries made along a walking route where the walking time is less than or equal to T.

You have found the source code of a mysterious program.I immediately tried to compile and run it, but no matter how long I wait, the process does not finish.It seems that the program is in an infinite loop.When I looked at the source code in detail, I found the following.The source code consists of N lines, and each line is either a label statement or a goto statement.Label statements and goto statements each follow the following formats.LABEL: goto LABEL; Here, LABEL is a string representing the label.The program is processed line by line in order starting from the first line.The behavior when processing a label statement and a goto statement is as follows: Nothing happens when a label statement is processed When a goto statement is processed, the next processing starts with the label statement with the label of the same name The program ends when the last line is processed and there are no more lines to process.You want your program to terminate properly by removing some goto statements.On the other hand, I want to minimize the number of parts that need to be modified.What is the minimum number of goto statements that should be deleted in order to terminate the program?

A string S is given.Perform the following process Q times in order.Replace all characters c_i in S with the string p_i at the same time.Finally, output the string S from character A to character B (1-indexed).

For a set S of natural numbers, let f(S) be the number of elements in the set \{ GCD(T) | T ⊆ S, T is not empty \}.Here, GCD(T) is the largest integer that evenly divides all the numbers contained in T.In particular, note that when T consists of only one integer a, GCD(\{a\}) = a.Find f(\{i, i+1, . . ., i+W - 1\}) for i = 1, 2, . . ., N - W+1.

~How to draw a magic circle~ It is said that the power of a magic circle becomes stronger the more blue it contains.Therefore, I would like you to find the area of ​​the blue region included in the magic circle.The following figure is an example of a magic circle that can be drawn using this procedure.

There is a bit string of length n.When the i-th bit from the left is 1, you can get a_i points.If the number of 1's within a distance w around the i-th bit from the left ( =|\{j \in \{1,...,n\}∩\{i-w,...,i+w\} | j-th bit from the left is 1\}| ) is an odd number, you can get b_i points.Find the bit string that will give you the most scores.

A beautiful mountain range can be seen from the train window.The window is a rectangle with the lower left corner coordinates (0, 0) and the upper right corner coordinates (W, H).N mountains are visible from the window, and the i-th mountain has the shape of an upwardly convex parabola y = a_i (x-p_i)^2 + q_i.Find the length of the boundary line between the mountain and the sky.The following three figures correspond to Sample Input.The part indicated by the thick line is the boundary between the mountain and the sky.

It was only recently that the color scheme of each side of the Rubik's Cube sold in Japan was matched to the color scheme used as a standard around the world.To get used to this, let's start by playing with a simple 2x2x2 cube scramble.In the initial state, the Rubik's Cube is arranged so that each color is concentrated on one side, with red visible on the top, yellow on the front, and blue on the right side.Also, the underside is orange, the back is white, and the left side is green.Using a developed diagram, it can be expressed as follows.The operation of rotating each surface 90 degrees clockwise is expressed by the initial letters U, F, R, D, B, and L.A Rubik's cube scramble is represented as a string S, where each character corresponds to one rotation operation.Output the color of each side of the Rubik's cube as a development diagram when scrambled according to the input from the initial state.

Define the suffix array SA for a string S of length N as a permutation of positive integers less than or equal to N obtained by the following procedure.The substring of S from the i-th character to the j-th character is expressed as S[i..j].However, the subscript is 1-indexed.For example, the suffix array SA for S ="mississippi" is as follows.As input, we are given a suffix array SA for a string of length N.Find the Kth (1-indexed) character string in lexicographical order among the character strings that yield SA.However, the original string consists of at most A characters, starting with 'a' in alphabetical order.

Your company has n employees.For a set of m employees (a_i, b_i), a_i is b_i's boss.Employee x is the de facto superior of employee y if at least one of the following holds true:x is y's boss.There exists an employee z who is y's de facto superior, and x is z's superior.There are no employees in your company who are effectively their own bosses.Your company is planning a company trip in which all employees will participate.At the request of all employees, the room allocation at the inn must be ``good room allocation.''A certain room allocation is said to be a ``good room allocation'' if both of the following are satisfied.Each employee is assigned to a room.When employee x and employee y are assigned to the same room, x is not y's effective boss.Since the staff members who were the secretaries were very talented, we allocated the rooms in a way that would give us a ``good room allocation'' and minimize the number of rooms needed.Unfortunately, the budget is insufficient.It seems that the number of rooms needed must be reduced.Therefore, you, who work in the human resources department, decide to reduce the number of rooms required to obtain a ``good room assignment'' by dissolving just one boss-subordinate relationship.So, which relationship should I cancel?

Mr. Mori, a wealthy man, wants to buy n plots of land divided into grids and build four factories, A, B, C, and D.First, determine the width w of the entire land as a value between 1 and n.Let the top left parcel of this land be (0, 0).Then, purchase the (0, 0) plot first and the (1, 0) plot second.In this way, after purchasing the ( x , y ) plot at the i-th location, the plot at ( x + 1, y ) is purchased at the i + 1-th location.If you are trying to buy a plot at ( w , y ), buy a plot at (0, y + 1) instead.Furthermore, before Mr. Mori purchased the land, a fortune teller told him the following.``It's a good idea to build a factory A on the land of the a-th grid you buy, a factory B on the b-th grid you buy, a factory C on the c-th grid, and a factory D on the d-th grid.'' Mr. Mori is very fond of fortune-telling and always wants to follow the advice of fortune-tellers.However, since the two pairs A and B and C and D are related factories, it is better to have them as close as possible.They want to keep related factories as close to each other as possible, while adhering to both their own land purchase method and the fortune teller's advice.Here, when the two lands are located at ( x 1 , y 1 ) and ( x 2 , y 2 ), the distance can be found as | x 1 - x 2 |+ | y ​​1 - y 2 |.| x | represents the absolute value of x.Your task is to write a program that calculates the total distance when purchasing land so that the value of (distance between factories A and B) + (distance between factories C and D) is minimized.Figure 1 shows the combination of all factory locations in the first sample.When you purchase land with a width of 3, the sum of the distances is the minimum.

VOCAL ANDROID is a composition tool that synthesizes voice by inputting melody and lyrics.You went to Vocal Android's live performance held in Wakayama Prefecture on March 9, 2013, and were impressed by the splendor of the music.So, you decide to purchase the new vocal android "Tatsune Repro (CV. Ritsumeitaro)" and start composing music.However, although the melody was completed, I was stuck writing the lyrics.I have prepared several fragments of the phrases I want to use, and then combine them and apply them to each melody.Each melody has a fixed length, and the phrases must be applied to match this length.In addition, each phrase has a score that indicates how good the rhythm is when using that phrase, as well as a lower limit length and an upper limit length.As long as the length of the phrase is an integer, it can be adjusted freely from the lower limit to the upper limit.However, even if you change the length, the points you get will not change.Figure 1 is the first example of the sample.Your job is to combine phrases to match the length of the melody to create a song with the highest total score possible.The same phrase may be used multiple times.

You are an excellent project manager.This time, I was contracted to manage an extremely large-scale project.Based on my intuition and experience, I managed to create an arrow diagram like the one shown in Figure 1.Each node represents a connection point for work.Also, S is the start state of the project, and T is the connection point that represents the end state of the project.For example, at connection point P3, work G cannot be started until work C and work E are completed.The project's plan is perfect, and every join point has no working path out of it and back to it.In addition, all operations have a connection point before and after them, and connection points other than the start state and end state always have operations before and after them.However, this project requires a lot of work.In the arrow diagram, the work on the path that takes the longest time (critical path) has a large impact on the project's time constraints.So you decide to write a program to calculate the number of days required for a critical path on an arrow diagram.By the way, this critical path always exists on the arrow diagram.

``Wow! What a terrible code!'' My senior colleague was shouting as he opened the old source code.It looks like I'll be forced to do another tedious job...There are a dizzying number of if statements in the source code.Apparently the control structure is not that complicated.A single conditional statement includes a variable, the NOT operator "~", and the binary operators AND "&" / OR "|".Variables represent true or false, and variable names are represented by one or more letters of the alphabet.The NOT operator has priority in the order of operations, but you can change the order by using parentheses to calculate it first.There may be one or two variables, binary operations may appear at most once, and other operations may appear as many times as needed.Anyway, the compilation passed.Before modifying the code, you need to write tests.This time I was asked to write a test case for this code.The number of test cases is sufficient as long as all instructions are executed at least once.Estimate the number of test cases and if you can do it with just one, take it.

Many unique idols belong to the idol agency 841 Production.Twin idols Aki and Maki are idols belonging to 841 Pro, and both of them are very fond of games.One day, the two of them decided to have fun by making the following game themselves.This game is a cooperative game between two people, so it is expected that the twins will play in unison.First, before starting the game, two people separately draw the field on the floor as they like.The field consists of several boards with letters of the alphabet written on them and routes that connect the boards.They can move between boards by following this route.However, the route has an arrow, and you can only move in the direction of that arrow.Since there is no route between Aki's field and Maki's field, it is not possible to go back and forth between each other's fields.First, the game begins with Aki and Maki standing on their favorite boards on their own field.Then, the two people act simultaneously according to the following rules.Aki & Maki want to get as high a score as possible in this game.You, a talented new producer at 841 Pro, will brilliantly give you hints on how many points the two of you can get using your programming expertise.

We formed a school idol group called u's, and in order to aim for even greater heights, we watched idols belonging to the idol group 841 Productions that we admired on DVDs in the club room.These idols are now active on the shining stage, but it seems that they used to ride a robot called BYDOL and work on removing meteorites.So, we learned that in order to become a top idol, we needed BYDOL control skills, so we decided to get BYDOL in a hurry and have a mock battle using BYDOL against school idols from other schools.In this mock battle, the troops are divided into the white army and the red army, and they shoot each other using bullets loaded in BYDOL.Members of each army board the BYDOL and fire a bullet ``just once'' when given the signal.The coordinates of all BYDOLs at the time the signal is given are fixed, and BYDOLs can know each other's coordinates using radar.Two or more BYDOLs will never appear at the same coordinates.The field in which BYDOL can move is represented by a two-dimensional plane, and it is assumed that there are no walls that surround the entire field.However, there are some circular walls inside the field.The inside of this wall is plastered with concrete, so BYDOL cannot penetrate.The circles that make up the wall do not touch, intersect, or contain each other, and the wall does not exist at the coordinates where BYDOL is located.Each BYDOL has five parameters set: the coordinates it is located at when the signal is given, its firing range, the number of bullets it can fire at one time, whether it is loaded with bullets that reflect off walls, and the number of points the enemy army receives when defeated.The bullets can be fired in any direction 360 degrees from the BYDOL's position and travel in a straight line.As long as the distance traveled by the bullet after being fired is less than or equal to the range value, and as long as the bullet does not hit other BYDOLs or walls, the bullet continues to travel.All bullets moving on the field do not collide even if their trajectories intersect.Some BYDOLs are loaded with bullets that bounce off walls.All BYDOL bullets in this case will reflect off the wall only once.However, unless the sum of the distance from your BYDOL to the reflected point on the wall and the distance from the reflected point to the opponent's BYDOL is less than the range value, it will not be considered to have hit the opponent.When reflecting off a wall, as shown in Figure 1, draw a tangent to the point where the bullet hit the wall, and use this line as a reference to reflect the bullet so that the angle of incidence θ 1 and the angle of reflection θ 2 are equal.If the trajectory touches the circle that makes up the wall, the bullet will continue straight and will not be reflected by the wall.A certain BYDOL can shoot multiple bullets at once and hit multiple BYDOLs within the range of the number of bullets it can fire.However, the same BYDOL is not allowed to fire more than one bullet in the exact same direction.Each BYDOL has a set number of points that the enemy army will receive when it is defeated.In this shootout, the army with the most points wins.If the same score is obtained, it will be considered a draw.BYDOL will fall if hit by one bullet, so no matter how many shots you shoot at the same BYDOL, you will only get points once.If you shoot an ally BYDOL, you will be disqualified, so never shoot.Self-destruction is not allowed either.Your job as the operational commander in U's is to calculate the points each army gets when they act to get the maximum number of points, and to determine which army can win.If we can do this, U's will be one step closer to becoming a top idol.

One day, the older sister decided to teach the children how to count combinations.Therefore, the older sister decided to try solving the following problem.Figure 1 shows all the paths when N = 2, M = 2.Figure 1 shows a total of 12 ways to travel, and the routes are represented by thick red lines.In this problem, even if N and M become slightly larger, the number of combinations increases explosively, making it very difficult to count them by hand.So, the older sister made a plan to transplant her brain into a robot and spend 250,000 years counting the number of combinations in 10 x 10.``You'll die if you calculate 10 x 10! Stop it!'' Despite the children who stop her, the older sister starts calculating.Even if she goes to such lengths, she wants to teach her children the wonders of combinatorial explosions.As a programmer, you are touched by the older sister's enthusiasm, and you decide to write a program to solve this problem.However, it is difficult to create a program that calculates 10 x 10 straight away, so I decided to start by writing a program for when M is small.

A great king of a certain country suddenly decided to inspect the land of a friendly country.The country is famous for its trains, and the king often visits various stations.There are 52 train stations, each with a single uppercase or lowercase letter name (no names overlap).This train route is in a circular pattern, with station A followed by station B, station B followed by station C, Z station followed by A station, then B station, then Z station followed by A station, and then back.It is a single track, and there are no trains running in the opposite direction.One day, a newspaper reporter obtained a list of the stations in which the king would visit.``dcdkIlkP...'' He will visit d station first, then c station, then d station, and so on.Just when I thought I could track down and interview the king of a great country, something unexpected happened.The list was encrypted for anti-terrorism purposes!A friend of the reporter reportedly obtained the key to cracking the code.This reporter was immediately handed over the keys and began to revise the list.A key is made up of a series of numbers."3 1 4 5 3" What these numbers mean is that the first station you visit is the one three stations before the one on the list.The second station you visit is the station before the second station on the list, and so on, indicating how many stations before the station on the list you actually visit.The reporter started to make corrections, but the number of keys was smaller than the number of stations he had listed.When he asked his friends what he should do, he was told that after using the last key, he could use the first key again.And the reporter was finally able to revise the list.``abZfFijL...'' Just when I thought there was nothing to be scared of anymore, an even more unexpected situation came to light.Great kings stayed for many days, and there was a list and key for each itinerary.The reporter was instructed by his boss to decrypt the entire list, but due to the volume, he could not finish it alone.Your task is to help him and write a program that will automatically decrypt this list.

There is a game called Sim Forest 2013.In this game, players can become the god of the forest and raise forest animals.Animals hatch from eggs and reproduce.When an egg meets certain conditions, it mutates with a certain probability and a new species of animal is born.There is an animal dictionary in this game, and when you obtain a new type of animal, it is recorded in the animal dictionary.Mr. Mori is an avid Sim Forest 2013 player.His animal dictionary will be complete when he fills in one remaining species.Mr. Mori decided to obtain this remaining species through mutation from eggs.The game progresses as follows.This game meets the following specifications.As inputs, s, n, t, days of the week and times when mutation is possible, p, m are given.Find the probability when the start day and time of the first stage are set so that the probability of mutation occurring by the end of all stages is maximized.

Summer vacation has already begun at the school that twins Ami and Mami attend, and a large amount of homework has been assigned this year as well.However, the two playful girls decided to go out and play outside without doing their homework at all.If things continue like this, it's obvious that they'll end up crying on the last day of summer vacation, so you, acting as their guardian, decide to keep them from leaving the house today until they finish their book report homework.You are well prepared and have already borrowed all the assigned books from the library.However, due to library regulations, only one of each book can be borrowed.Now, for educational reasons, I decided to have them each read all the books and write their impressions without cooperating with each other.In addition, since the return deadline for the books was approaching, I decided to ask him to finish reading all the books as soon as possible.Then, you decide to think about how to complete your homework as quickly as possible under those conditions.Here, the time when the twins finished reading the book and the time when they finished their homework are considered as the time when both twins finished their work.Since there is only one copy of each book, two people cannot read the same book at the same time.Additionally, due to adult circumstances, I cannot stop reading a book once I have started reading it, nor am I able to stop writing a review about a book once I have started.Of course, I can't write a review about a book I haven't read.Furthermore, since Ami and Mami are twins, the time it takes to read each book and the time it takes to write a review is the same for them.For example, suppose there are three books, and the time it takes to read each book and the time it takes to write a review are as follows.In this case, if you proceed with your homework as shown in Figure C-1, you will be able to finish reading all the books in time 10 and finish your homework in time 15.In the method shown in Figure C-2, the homework is completed in time 14, but the time to finish reading the book is not the shortest, so it cannot be used this time.Furthermore, it is not possible for two people to read the same book at the same time, as shown in Figure C-3, or to interrupt reading, as shown in Figure C-4, or to write a review of a book that they have not read.While taking into consideration various adult circumstances, let's think of a way to complete homework as quickly as possible for the twins who want to go out to play.

In a certain sea area, there are islands called the Mist Islands, which are blessed with beautiful nature and rich resources.Because the climate is warm, people have lived peacefully there for a long time.However, recently, a serious fact has come to light.It is said that due to tectonic movements, the islands of the Mist Archipelago will soon sink.In order to respond to this situation, a task force consisting of representatives from each island was formed.First, the work of the research team made it clear when each island would sink.It was also discovered that bridges could be built between several islands by combining the technologies of the Mist Islands.As a result, it was decided to build bridges between the islands, allowing residents of each island to evacuate to a specific island.At this stage, it seems that it has not been decided which island will be the evacuation destination.Therefore, in the beginning, bridges are built so that any island can be used as an evacuation destination by crossing several bridges to reach all other islands.When an island sinks over time, a bridge with one island at both ends sunk becomes impassable.At this time, depending on how the first bridge is built, an island may be created that cannot be reached from another island.In this case, by building a new bridge, a travel route can be secured between the islands that have not yet sunk.If a travel route cannot be secured no matter how many bridges are built, no more bridges will be constructed.The task force will have to decide on evacuation destinations and complete the evacuation of residents by then.Furthermore, if it is not possible to build a bridge to secure a travel route at this point, no bridge will be constructed at all.The task force will probably come up with a new plan.By the way, the construction cost of a bridge is determined by the location where it is built.Evacuation costs something, so the task force seems to be trying to build bridges so that the total construction cost is as low as possible.How can we build a bridge to achieve this?

Dr. Akita, who lives in the neighborhood, is a local history researcher and has recently discovered a new old document about Ono Komachi. It is widely known that Ono Komachi made a condition for Major General Fukakusa, who wanted a relationship, to continue visiting her 100 nights without fail, but according to his claim, this discovery newly revealed that the two of them played a certain game every night. The rules are as follows.For a detailed definition of valid formulas, please see below. In this game, the larger the final formula calculation result, the fewer days Rear Admiral Fukakusa had to attend. In other words, Rear Admiral Fukakusa's purpose was to maximize the result, while Ono Komachi's purpose was to minimize it. By the way, in the old document there was a record of the number of edits and the formula determined by the servant,The important results were unreadable due to worms. The doctor was interested in the results and tried to calculate them on his own, but it was too difficult for him who is not good at game theory, so he asked your neighbor for help. So you decided to write a program and cooperate with him. It may seem suspicious that there were modern mathematical formulas in the Heian period, but before the cause of getting along with your neighbors,You shouldn't worry about such trivial matters. A valid mathematical expression in this game is a non-empty string consisting of only 18 types of characters: "(", ")", "*", "+", "-", "&", "^", "|", "0" - "9", and a finite number of terms connected using a binary operator. When written down, it becomes as follows. Terms are:A valid numeric expression enclosed in parentheses, such as "("[valid numeric expression]")", or a positive integer. A positive integer is a string consisting of only digits, without a leading "0". Finally, a binary operator is exactly one character from one of the following: "*", "+", "-", "&", "^", or "|". See below for a detailed definition.From the above rules, etc. are considered invalid mathematical expressions. Note that on the other hand, etc., although redundant, are accepted and considered valid mathematical expressions under the above rules. In this game, there are six types of binary operators: "*", "+", "-", "&", "^", and "|". They are multiplication, addition, subtraction, bitwise conjunction,These are bitwise exclusive OR and bitwise OR. When considering bitwise operations, negative numbers are assumed to be expressed as 2's complement numbers with a sufficiently large number of digits. In other words, you can think of calculating as an ordinary signed integer type. Also, the priority is from highest to lowest. Note that * has higher priority than + in an expression such as *This means that is calculated first, in other words, . Also, assume that all binary operators are left associative. This means that in an expression like , they are calculated from the left. In other words, .

Princess Tetra of the Palace Kingdom is known as an unparalleled lover of puzzles, and has recently become obsessed with the ``Tetra Puzzle'' she devised.This is a puzzle that uses a board covered with equilateral triangular squares and a regular tetrahedral block called a tetrahedral block, which is made up of four equilateral triangular panels.The purpose of the puzzle is to unfold all the tetra blocks on the board so that the following conditions are met.Figure F-1 shows an example of a tetra puzzle problem and its answer.Tetra blocks are placed in the ★ and ● squares, and each block is designated to cover the ☆ and ○ squares.In the example answer, the square with a horizontal line is the square that was covered by the tetra block placed on the ★ square, and the square with the vertical line is the square that was covered by the tetra block placed on the ● square.Figure F-2 is an example of an invalid answer to the example question in the figure above.In the example on the left, the square directly above the ★ square is covered by two panels, and in the example on the right, each panel does not have a shape obtained by expanding tetra blocks, so this is not accepted as an answer.Princess Tetra has created a collection of puzzle problems for the upcoming summer festival in order to let as many people know how fun puzzles are.And since you are trusted by the princess as the second puzzle lover in the kingdom, you are appointed to be in charge of checking the problem set.Now, as you were proceeding with the checking process, you noticed a problem.It seems that the problem set contains puzzles for which there are no solutions.You wanted to respect the puzzles created by the princess as much as possible, so you decided to change the puzzles to solvable ones by deleting one of the tetra blocks along with the information about the squares that the tetra block should cover.There are only three hours left until the check deadline given by the princess.Please review the problem set and make corrections quickly.

You, a proud hero, are finally about to reach the Demon King.However, in order to reach the throne room where the Demon King is, they must clear the final map prepared by the Demon King.In this map, everything changes as if it were a two-dimensional plan view from above.Humans are transformed into mere dots on this map.The map is divided into two areas by a straight line.When there is a person in one area, a mirror image of the person is reflected in the other area, at a position symmetrical to the straight line.There are multiple buildings in each of the two areas.In this map, a building is a polygon formed by connecting several points, and the inside surrounded by the sides and the top of the sides are the inside of the building.Buildings, unlike people, do not create mirror images in other areas.If you enter this map, you will be bound inside one of the buildings by the Demon King's magic.You can only clear the map by moving to the only escape point inside the building you are being held in.Furthermore, you have been cursed by the Demon King and your movements are restricted.The restriction is that your location and the location of your mirror image must be inside some building.As long as you follow these restrictions, you can move freely inside the building.Now, you must clear this map as quickly as possible and defeat the demon king who is trying to destroy the world.Given your initial position, find the distance of the shortest path to the escape point.However, if you cannot move to the escape point, you will not be able to escape from the eternal map and will only rot away.

Mr. Takatsuki, who is planning to participate in the Aizu training camp, comes from a poor family and does not have much money.Therefore, I am trying to save money by using the Seishun 18 Kippu.The 18 Ticket is an excellent ticket that allows you to ride as many trains as you like on slow trains all day long, and to enter and exit the ticket gates as you like (detailed usage rules are omitted).The appeal of the 18 Kippu is that you can use your free time to look around at local souvenirs at the station where you transfer.Since this is a great opportunity, she would like to visit various stations during her trip.However, since I don't want to miss the next train, I decided to only go around the station if there was more than T minutes between the time I arrived at the transfer station and the time I left the transfer station.Mr. Takatsuki will give you a transfer plan using the 18 ticket, so print out the names and times of the stations you can visit.Please note that the first station you depart from and the last station you arrive at are not included in your list of options.

Mr. Takatsuki, who is scheduled to participate in the Aizu training camp, comes from a poor family and always tries to use paper as sparingly as possible.In order to decide on the team for the Aizu training camp, she decided to play a lottery with the other participants.The method of making the Amidakuji for this training camp is as follows.First, draw N vertical bars in parallel on a piece of paper.Then, draw M horizontal bars in order from the top so that they are perpendicular to the vertical bars and the heights of the horizontal bars are all different.For example, the width of Sample Input 1 is as shown in Figure 1.At this point, Mr. Takatsuki looked a little dissatisfied.It's a waste of paper to write an Amidakuji in such a vertical manner.You should be able to compress the height even more.So, I would like you to solve the following Amidakuji height compression problem for her.First of all, the height of an Amidakuji is the value obtained by counting all horizontal bars at the same height as height 1, and counting this up to the lowest horizontal bar.Here, it is assumed that each horizontal bar can be freely moved up and down in order to perform height compression.However, it is not allowed to remove or add horizontal bars.Amidakuji after height compression must meet the following conditions.The end points of horizontal bars do not touch the end points of other horizontal bars.The results of tracing the Amidakuji after compression and the results of tracing the Amidakuji before compression match.Figure 2 is a compressed version of Figure 1.The "horizontal bar that connects vertical bars 1 and 2" moves to the top and becomes the same height as the "horizontal bar that connects vertical bars 4 and 5," and these two have a height of 1.Also, the "horizontal bar connecting vertical bars 3 and 4" and the "horizontal bar connecting vertical bars 2 and 3" have different heights, making the total height 3.Compress the height of the given Amidakuji and output the compressed height.

Mr. Takatsuki, who is planning to participate in the Aizu training camp, comes from a poor family and does not have much money.She is looking for a hotel for a training camp, but is struggling to find a plan that will save her as much money as possible.Find a way to stay at a hotel that will cost you less in total, and let her know.The number of hotel candidates for lodging is N, and each hotel is referred to as Hotel 1, Hotel 2, ..., Hotel N.She is planning to book D nights at these hotels.Since the room rate for all candidate hotels can vary from night to night, the input gives the cost for D nights for each hotel.Output the hotel stay that minimizes the total cost of accommodation for D nights.However, please note that it is not necessary to stay at the same hotel for all nights.If it's cheaper, she's considering changing hotels every night.If there are multiple such accommodation methods, output the accommodation method that minimizes the number of hotel moves.The number of hotel moves is the value obtained by adding up the number of D nights when the hotel for the xth night (1 <= x < D) is different from the hotel for the x+1st night, and this is considered one trip.If you still cannot decide on one method, output the accommodation method that is the smallest in dictionary order.Given two sequences of hotel numbers A = {a1, a2, ..., aD} and B = {b1, b2, ..., bD}, A is lexicographically smaller than B if ai is smaller than bi for the first i such that ai is different from bi.

Are you familiar with the data structure called BDD (Binary Decision Diagram)?ZDD, which has recently become a hot topic in connection with the Combination Explosion Sister video, is a data structure derived from BDD.This problem is a basic implementation of BDD.A BDD is a cycle-free graph (DAG) that represents a logical function.For example, the logical function representing the truth table in Table 1 is BDD in Figure 1.BDD consists of five types of parts: 0 edge (dashed arrow), 1 edge (solid arrow), 0 terminal node (0 square), 1 terminal node (1 square), and variable node (circle with a number written on it).There is one 0-terminal node and one 1-terminal node at the bottom.Each variable node outputs one 0 edge and one 1 edge, which are connected to the next node.Each variable node corresponds to the variable with the number written on the node, and if the value of that variable is 1, it will be traced to the 1 edge side, and if it is 0, it will be traced to the 0 edge side.Then, as a result of tracing from the top node, if you reach the 1 terminal node, the answer will be 1, and if you reach the 0 terminal node, the answer will be 0.For example, if we trace "Variable 1 = 1, Variable 2 = 0, Variable 3 = 1" in the truth table of Table 1 using BDD, we will trace it as shown in the bold line in Figure 1, and we can see that the result is 1.In this problem, variable nodes always appear in the order of variable 1, variable 2, ..., variable N, one step at a time from the top of the BDD.Now, in this problem, we will ask you to create a program that compresses the simple BDD just described using simplification rules.The simplification rules are the two rules shown in FIG.First, the rule in FIG. 2(a) is applied when there is a variable node A and "the point pointed to by the 0 edge of A = the point pointed to by the 1 edge of A".In this case, whether the value of the variable is 0 or 1, there is only one transition destination, so this variable node is found to be unnecessary.Therefore, all variable nodes that meet this condition can be deleted.The rule in Figure 2(b) is applied when there are two variable nodes A and B, and "the variable number of A = the variable number of B, and the destination of the 0 edge of A = the destination of the 0 edge of B, and the destination of the 1 edge of A = the destination of the 1 edge of B."In this case, it can be seen that having the same node twice is wasteful, so the two variable nodes can be shared as one variable node.By repeatedly using the simplification rules until the shape of the BDD does not change, the BDD in Figure 1 changes from Figure 3(a) to (b), and finally transforms into a more compact BDD as shown in Figure 3(c).You can see that the BDD that originally had 7 variable nodes has now become a BDD with 3 variable nodes.Since the truth table representing the logical function is input, output the number of variable nodes in the BDD after applying the simplification rules.

Mr. Takatsuki, who is scheduled to participate in the Aizu training camp, is an avid student and has recently been working hard on studying English.She tries to memorize as many English words as possible by playing the following games on her phone.The cell phone she carries is a touch panel type that allows you to operate the screen with your fingers.A 4 * 4 square is drawn on the screen of a mobile phone, and each square has an uppercase letter of the alphabet written on it.In this game, players try to find as many English words as possible hidden in the grid within a time limit of T seconds, and compete for points based on the types of English words they discover.The steps to find a single word are as follows.First, decide on the starting square that corresponds to the first letter of the word and place your finger there.Then, from the square you are currently placing your finger on, trace it with your finger toward one of the eight adjacent squares, up, down, left, right, or diagonally.However, you cannot pass through a square that has already been traced from the starting square to the current square.When you reach the end of the word, release your finger.At that moment, points are added for each word traced with your finger from the starting square to the ending square.It takes x seconds to trace a word with x letters.The time it takes to move your finger from tracing one word to tracing the next word can be ignored.During input, a dictionary of words to be added points is also input.Each word in this dictionary is assigned a score.When you trace a word written in the dictionary with your finger, points corresponding to that word are added up.However, if a word is traced in exactly the same way from the starting square to the ending square, points will only be added once for the first time.Points will not be added if you trace a word with your finger that is not written in the dictionary.You will be given a dictionary and a game board, so output the maximum score you can get within the time limit.

Kikuchi is a big fan of bicycles.Today, he is about to get on his favorite bicycle and hit the cycling road.There are N towns in the area where he lives.Let's call each town Town 1, Town 2..., Town N.The town he lives in is Town 1.Many people in this area enjoy cycling as a hobby, and recently cycling roads have been built frequently connecting towns.The cycling path allows you to travel in both directions between the two towns.In the input process, which towns have been newly connected by cycling roads are entered in chronological order.Kikuchi will only go cycling when a new cycling road is completed and the following conditions are met.First, it's boring to see the same scenery over and over again while cycling, so the route needs to be such that you don't pass through the same town or the same cycling route more than once.Since we want to return home at the end of the cycle, the starting point needs to be Town 1 and the finishing point needs to be Town 1.And you must ride on one of the latest completed cycling roads.Also, you don't have to go through every town or cycle route.Completed cycling roads will be given in chronological order, so when each cycling road is completed, answer whether Kikuchi can cycle.

Mr. Hagiwara, the magician, has a very negative personality.Whenever she feels depressed, she uses her digging magic to create a hole and buries herself crying.The magic of digging a hole is as follows.She first draws N line segments on the ground.Then, when she casts a spell, a hole is created in the area surrounded by the line.Two holes that intersect or contain are counted as one hole.However, two holes that touch only at the apex are counted as separate holes.Figures 1 and 2 show diagrams of Sample Inputs 1 and 2.First, in Figure 1, the triangles A and B touch each other only at their vertices, so they are counted as separate holes.Triangle B and triangle C count as one hole because their sides overlap.Therefore, Figure 1 shows two holes.In Figure 2, the D rectangle is included in the E rectangle, so these count as one hole.E also intersects the hole in the upper right, so there is only one hole in Figure 2.You are Ms. Hagiwara's caretaker and must fill the countless holes she has created.Write a program to find the number of holes she made to find out how many holes she has to fill.

It is important to use strong passwords to use the Internet more safely.At the same time, it is also very important not to reuse the same password.No matter how strong your password is, if the plaintext is leaked in one place, it will be very easy to break.Of course, if all applications hadhed passwords and used SALT properly, they would not cause such damage even if they were leaked, but there are still applications that store passwords in plain text.Well, the contest is about to start.You have to register a team account...A string representing the password is given.Check whether the string satisfies all of the following conditions, and if so, output "VALID", and if any item does not satisfy, output "INVALID".A string representing the password is given on one line.If the string representing the password satisfies all the conditions in the problem, print "VALID" on one line, and if any conditions are not met, print "INVALID" on one line.It is a password widely used around the world.That's a great password.Widely used as a PIN number.Must be at least 6 characters long.

ICPC World Finals Day 1 In a programming contest, it is important to understand the program execution time.If you make a mistake in estimating the execution time and write code that exceeds the time limit, you will lose that much time.This is especially true in team competitions like ICPC where only one computer can be used.So, just before the ICPC World Finals, Mr. Tee decided to start practicing calculating the execution time of a program by mental calculation.Since "log is like a constant," let's start by working on polynomials.There is a program that calculates the answer to a certain problem, and a computer that executes it.We are given a positive integer \(n\) representing the input of the problem, a polynomial \(f(n)\) representing the number of instructions to be executed in the program depending on the input \(n\), and the execution time of one computer instruction \(T\) [nanoseconds] (=\( 10^{-9} \)[seconds]).Determine whether the total execution time of the program is ``less than'' 1 second when the instructions are executed the number of times equal to the polynomial \(f(n)\), and if it is ``less than'' 1 second, find the total execution time.On the first line, a positive integer \(n\) representing the input of the problem and an integer \(T\) representing the execution time [nanoseconds] of one instruction are given, separated by spaces.A polynomial \(f(n)\) representing the number of instructions to be executed on the second line is given.The polynomial \( f(n) \) is expressed by the following BNF.Here, each monomial n^k represents \(n\) to the \(k\) power.If the total execution time of the program is ``less than'' 1 second, output the total execution time in nanoseconds, and if it ``exceeds'' 1 second, output "TLE" on one line.The total execution time is \( (100^{1} + 100^{2} + 100^{3}) \times 100 \)[nanoseconds].The total execution time is \( 1000^{3} + 1000^{0} = 10^{9}+1 \)[nanoseconds], which exceeds 1[second].The total execution time is \( (10^{8})^{3} \times 10^{8} = 10^{32} \)[nanoseconds].

When Mr. Kay was browsing a certain SNS as usual, a problem appeared on his timeline that said, ``The only person who can solve it has an IQ of 150 or more.''Mr. Kay has an IQ of over 150, so he solved the problem in an instant without even looking.From his point of view, there is no need to use his brain to solve such problems.It is enough to leave it to the computer.The problem contained the following mysterious formula:Considering the above operator \( + \), find the number of integer pairs \( x,y \) such that \( x \geq 0, y \geq 0 \) and \( x + y = a \) for a certain positive integer \( a \).A positive integer \(a\) is given on one line.Print on one line the number of pairs \((x,y)\) that satisfy \( a = x + y, x \geq 0, y \geq 0\).There is one way: \( 5 + 4 \).There are two ways: \( 11 + 11 \) and \( 2 + 0 \).\( 1 + 0 = 11 , 0 + 1 = -11 \).\( 1 \) cannot be generated.Note that \( 1 - 0 \) is not \( 101 \).

ICPC World Finals Day 2 Mr. Tee and his friends arrived at the airport terminal.From now on, we will change planes and board the enemy country R.Since we will be transiting through country D, we will have to exchange the money we have into two different currencies.Mr. K: "How do you exchange money, Mr. Tee?" Mr. Tee: "Huff, huff, huff, huff, huff, huff, huff." Mr. K.: "I see. I'm also going to allocate 20,000 yen to 5,000 yen for country D and 15,000 yen for country R." Mr. Tee: "Huff, huff, huff, huff, huff, huff.”Mr. Kay: "Huh? That's strange. The exchange results are different from mine..." A traveler is thinking of leaving Japan, sightseeing in country D (currency unit D), country R (currency unit R), and returning to Japan.I have \( M \) yen now.I know that \( c_{D} \)[D] and \( c_{R} \)[R] will be consumed in each country, so I want to exchange money so that I don't run out of money.When you exchange \( x \) yen to money from country D, it becomes \( \lfloor \frac{r_{D}x}{100} \rfloor \)[D], and when you exchange \( x \) yen to money from country R, ​​it becomes \( \lfloor \frac{r_{R}x}{100} \rfloor \)[R].For example, when \( r_{D}=11, c_{D}=10 \), if you exchange 150 yen into country D's money, it will become \( \lfloor \frac{11 \times 150}{100} \rfloor = \)16[D], so there will be no shortage of money.However, if you only exchange 50 yen, you will end up with 5 [D], which is less than 10 [D], so you will run out of money.Also, when I return home, I will exchange all the money I have into Japanese yen.If you exchange \( x \)[D] into Japanese yen, it will become \( \lfloor \frac{100x}{r_{D}} \rfloor \) yen, and if you exchange \( x \)[R] into Japanese yen, it will become \( \lfloor \frac{100x}{r_{R}} \rfloor \) yen.In the first example above, 10[D] of the 16[D] obtained is consumed, so 6[D] is left over.If you exchange this into Japanese yen, it becomes \( \lfloor \frac{100 \times 6}{11} \rfloor = 54\) yen.Find the maximum amount of Japanese yen that will be returned to you when you return home if you make the optimal exchange of Japanese yen when you leave the country.If either country D or R is short of money no matter what currency exchange is done, print "-1".*\( \lfloor x \rfloor \) represents the largest integer not exceeding \(x\).On the first line, the amount of Japanese yen currently on hand \(M\), the exchange rate between the Japanese yen and the currency unit of country D \( r_{D} \), the exchange rate between the Japanese yen and the currency unit of country R \( r_{R} \), the amount of money consumed in country D \( c_{D} \), and the amount of money consumed in country R \( c_{R} \) are given, separated by spaces.Print the maximum amount of Japanese yen that will be returned to you on one line.If either country D or R is short of money no matter what currency exchange is done, print "-1".If you convert 667 yen to country D's money and 10,000 yen to country R's money, you will get 20[D] and 100[R] respectively.This traveler has a lot of money.This traveler is broke and has nowhere to go.

ICPC World Finals Day 3 On this day, Mr. Tee was investigating the percentage of language usage within the team.Unusually, our team does not use the same language.The investigation revealed that two people were using C++ and one was using Java.Now, let's turn this into a pie chart.Wow, it looks like Java usage is low.No matter how you think about it, it can't be like this, so let's make some changes.This is a recently popular technique that involves shifting the center coordinates of the pie chart.(Everyone should not imitate me.) Hmm, now I have found a balance.By the way, how has the area changed?A pie chart with radius \(r\) and number of items \(n\) is given.The composition ratio of item \(i\) is \( p_{i} \)[%], and items are assigned clockwise from the coordinate \( (0, r) \) (see the figure below).Find the percentage change in the area occupied by each item when the center coordinates of the pie chart change from \( (0, 0) \) to \( (x, y) \).On the first line, output the rate of change [%] in the area occupied by each item, rounded down to an integer, separated by spaces.The area of ​​item 1 changed from about 21,048 to about 15,153.The rate of change is 15153/21048 ≒ 71.99%.The area of ​​item 2 changed from about 10,367 to about 16,262.The rate of change is 16262/10367 ≒ 156.86%.The approximate changes in area for each item are as follows.

ICPC World Finals Day 4 It was a dark and stormy night.In his dream, Mr. Tea was transported back in time.It looks like the ICPC venue, but there's something strange about it...Could it be...is this the ICPC from N years ago (N is a natural number)? The competition started in confusion.But there's no need to panic.I'm sure the problem is easy.For example, shouldn't this problem be solved just by sorting?Write the code calmly and submit it immediately.However, the result is...Memory Limit Exceeded...?! For C++ language, use the following code. For Java language, use the following code to sort the pseudorandom number sequence \( a[0], a[1], \cdots, a[n-1] \) in ascending order.Answer the \(k\)th number (\( a[k-1] \)).On the first line, the length of the pseudorandom number sequence \(n\), the desired index \(k\), and the initial value \(x0\) in the code are given separated by spaces.Output the \(k\)th smallest number in one line in the pseudorandom number sequence defined in the problem statement.The number sequence generated on this input is as follows.

ICPC World Finals Day 5 Mr. Tea got lost in the city of Country R.The problem is that I have no idea where I am because the cityscapes are similar.Country R is ``frightening'', so they must return to the hotel before they are attacked by the enemy.Fortunately, I only remember how I turned, so I'll just proceed at random.There is a two-dimensional grid map of \( w \times h \) cells.Let the coordinates of the upper left square be \( (1, 1) \) and the coordinates of the lower right square be \( (w, h) \).The map is surrounded by a wall, and \(n\) obstacles are placed at the coordinates \( (x_{i}, y_{i}) (1 \leq i \leq n) \).Furthermore, the instruction sequence \( r_{1}r_{2} \cdots r_{m} \) and the target coordinates \( (g_{x}, g_{y}) \) are given.After setting the starting coordinates and direction of travel, the pedestrian starts walking according to the following movement rules.1. Initialize the counter to \( i=1 \) and set the starting coordinates and direction of movement (up, down, left, right).2. Go straight until you come across a wall/obstacle.3. Do one of the following in front of the wall/obstacle.3. a. \( i>m \), that is, if there is no command, it will end walking.3. b. If the command \( r_{i} \) is L, change direction to the left, if R, change direction to the right.4. Increment \( i \).5. Return to 2.If the destination coordinates \( (g_{x}, g_{y}) \) are included in the coordinates passed from the start of the walk to the end of the walk (including the start coordinates), it is interpreted that the pedestrian has reached the destination.Find how many combinations of starting coordinates and direction of travel there are that can reach the destination.However, it is not possible to set obstacles or coordinates outside the map as the starting coordinates.(You can set the destination coordinates.) On the first line, the map's width \(w\), height \(h\), destination x coordinate \( g_{x} \), destination y coordinate \( g_{y} \), and number of obstacles \(n\) are given, separated by spaces.From the second line to the \( n+1 \) line, the coordinates \( (x_{i}, y_{i}) \) of each obstacle are given separated by spaces.An instruction sequence \( r_{1}r_{2} \cdots r_{m} \) of length \(m\) is given at the \( n+2 \)th line.Output in one line the number of combinations of starting coordinates and direction of travel that will allow you to reach your destination.In addition to the following 5 ways of walking, there are 4 ways of walking that start at the target coordinates and pass through the target coordinates.Walking starting from the following starting coordinates and direction of travel will pass through the destination coordinates.

Mr. B loves Brainf*ck and uses Brainf*ck to submit all of his school assignments.Recently, I was brainwashed by Mr. B and ended up limiting the language in which the teacher solved the assignments to Brainf*ck.If this continues, everyone will lose their credits.You decide to help everyone save their units by writing a program that generates Brainf*ck programs.Of course, programs that generate Brainf*ck programs do not need to be written in Brainf*ck.Generate a Brainf*ck program that outputs the specified string \( s \).Describe the language specifications of Brainf*ck used by judges.Brainf*ck programs are written as strings.The program string must match the square brackets ( [ and ] ), but there are no other restrictions.When a Brainf*ck program runs, it has a byte array and pointers to its elements.Byte arrays have infinite size and can hold 8-bit non-negative integer information.This can be expressed in C language as follows.In Brainf*ck, one command is represented by one character, and there are seven types of commands:Instructions are executed in order from the beginning, and characters that do not represent instructions are ignored as comments.The above Brainf*ck specifications are roughly the same as the commonly used Brainf*ck, so you can refer to http://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brainfuck etc.However, please note that Brainf*ck used for judging cannot use single character input commands.A string \( s \) of up to 1000 characters per line is given.Output the Brainf*ck code within 20000 characters.Any program whose execution result matches \( s \) is accepted.Input never contains spaces.This case is a sequence of ASCII codes from 33 to 126.

Once upon a time, there was a girl whose hobby was Othello.When I have time, I enjoy playing Othello with my family.One day, the girl wanted to play Othello as usual.However, everyone was busy and no one came to play.So I came up with a game that can be played by one person using an Othello board and spinning tops.Othello pieces are placed in all the vertical (R \) and horizontal (C \) squares so that their tops are either black or white.Consider repeating the operation of ``selecting a certain square and flipping over all the pieces diagonally above, below, to the left, to the right,'' as shown in the figure below.Find the number of operations that make the tops of all pieces white.However, the following conditions must be met.On the first line, an integer \( R \) and an integer \( C \) are given, separated by spaces.Subsequently, \( C \) integer values ​​(0 or 1) are given to each \( R \) line.The \( c \)th integer in the \( r \)th row represents the state of the square (r, c).1 means the top side is black, 0 means the top side is white.If you can make the tops of all the pieces on the board white, divide the number of such operations by \( 1000000009 (= 10 ^ 9 + 9) \) and print the remainder on one line.If you cannot make the tops of all the pieces on the board white, print \( 0 \) on one line.There are four ways: \( \{(1,1)\}, \{(1,2)\}, \{(1,3)\}, \{(1,1),(1,2),(1,3)\} \).

ICPC World Finals Day 6 Russian Constructivism is an art movement in the Soviet Union that began in the mid-1910s.Mr. Tee, who had been staying in Country R for a long time, was inspired by such things and decided to create a cool design even while rehearsing for the ICPC World Finals.Mr. Tee says: ``A circle and a line segment are enough symbols. What is important is how beautifully the line segments intersect.'' Assign \(n\) coordinates \( 1, 2, ..., n \) at equal intervals around the circumference.Exactly one line segment comes out from each coordinate, and the line segment at coordinate \( i \) is connected to a different coordinate \( a_{i} \).(Conversely, a line segment with coordinates \( a_{i} \) is connected to coordinates \( a_{a_{i}} = i \).) From this state, at most \( k \) line segments can be reconnected on the circumference (regardless of coordinates and length).What is the maximum size of the set of line segments that intersect with each other?The number of coordinates \(n\) and the number of line segments that can be reconnected \(k\) are given on the first line, separated by spaces.On the second line, \( a_{i} \), which represents the coordinates where the line segments with coordinates \(i\) are connected, is given separated by spaces.After reconnecting at most \(k\) line segments, print the maximum size of the set of line segments that intersect with each other in one line.Line segments that intersect with each other are represented by red line segments in the figure below.If you reconnect the line segments connecting 1 and 7 as shown in the figure below, you will get three line segments that intersect with each other.Since all line segments intersect each other, the maximum number of line segments that intersect is 4.

*This story is fiction and has no relation to any real people or organizations.Social games are very popular these days, and many companies are developing social games.You are infiltrating one of the competing social game development companies as a spy.The company you work for develops social games.The game they are developing is a game where you collect cards and fight, and the main source of revenue is the paid ``gacha-gacha'', which costs 300 yen per play.There are three types of cards in this game: ``Normal,'' ``Rare,'' and ``S Rare.''There are N types of "S rare" cards.With paid "Gacha Gacha", you have a 97% chance of getting one rare card and a 3% chance of getting one S rare card each time you play.You have the authority to manipulate the appearance rate of each S rare card (3% in total) in the paid "Gacha Gacha", and you have decided to "decrease" the company's profits.First, we investigated the usage trends of paid ``Gacha Gacha'' among various players, and found that ``Each player is satisfied after drawing their favorite S rare card, and does not use ``Gacha Gacha'' after that.''From the above information, you have modeled the company's profits under the assumption that each player prefers a specific S-rare card and continues playing Gacha-gacha until he draws that S-rare card.In other words, the company's profit is the sum of the percentage of people who prefer S rare card i x the expected value of the amount of money spent until obtaining S rare card i."Minimize" the company's profits by appropriately manipulating the appearance rate of S rare cards.However, the appearance rate must satisfy the following conditions.Print the minimum value of the company's profit defined in the problem statement.Set card 1 to appear at a rate of \( 0.01% \), and card 2 to appear at a rate of \( 2.99% \).
