You have a permutation p_1, p_2, ... , p_N of integers from 1 to N . You also have vertices numbered 1 through N . Find the number of trees while satisfying the following condition. Here, two trees T and T' are different if and only if there is a pair of vertices where T has an edge between them but T’ does not have an edge between them. For all integer pairs i, j ( 1\leq i < j \leq N ), if there is an edge between vertices i and j , there is an edge between vertices p_i and p_j as well. Since this number can be extremely large, output the number modulo 998244353 .

You are given two trees T, U . Each tree has N vertices that are numbered 1 through N . The i -th edge ( 1 \leq i \leq N-1 ) of tree T connects vertices a_i and b_i . The i -th edge ( 1 \leq i \leq N-1 ) of tree U connects vertices c_i and d_i . The operation described below will be performed N times. On the i -th operation, flip a fair coin. If it lands heads up, remove vertex i and all edges directly connecting to it from tree T . If it lands tails up, remove vertex i and all edges directly connecting to it from tree U . After N operations, each tree will be divided into several connected components. Let X and Y be the number of connected components originated from tree T and U , respectively. Your task is to compute the expected value of X \times Y . More specifically, your program must output an integer R described below. First, you can prove X \times Y is a rational number. Let P and Q be coprime integers that satisfies \frac{P}{Q} = X \times Y . R is the only integer that satisfies R \times Q $\equiv$ P $ \bmod \;$ 998244353, 0 \leq R < 998244353 .

You are given a rooted tree G with N vertices indexed with 1 through N . The root is vertex 1 . There are K kinds of colors indexed with 1 through K . You can paint vertex i with either color c_i or d_i . Note that c_i = d_i may hold, and if so you have to paint vertex i with c_i ( =d_i ). Let the colorfulness of tree T be the number of different colors in T . Your task is to write a program that calculates maximum colorfulness for all rooted subtrees. Note that coloring for each rooted subtree is done independently, so previous coloring does not affect to other coloring.

Mr. T has had an integer sequence of N elements a_1, a_2, ... , a_N and an integer K . Mr. T has created N integer sequences B_1, B_2, ... , B_N such that B_i has i elements. B_{N,j} = a_j ( 1 \leq j \leq N ) B_{i,j} = K \times B_{i+1,j} + B_{i+1,j+1} ( 1\leq i \leq N-1, 1 \leq j \leq i ) Mr. T was so careless that he lost almost all elements of these sequences a and B_i . Fortunately, B_{1,1}, B_{2,1}, ... , B_{N,1} and K are not lost. Your task is to write a program that restores the elements of the initial sequence a for him. Output the modulo 65537 of each element instead because the absolute value of these elements can be extremely large. More specifically, for all integers i ( 1 \leq i \leq N ), output r_i that satisfies r_i $\equiv$ a_i $ \bmod \;$ 65537, 0 \leq r_i < 65537 . Here, we can prove that the original sequence Mr. T had can be uniquely determined under the given constraints.

You are given a positive integer N . Your task is to determine if there exists a square matrix A whose dimension is N that satisfies the following conditions and provide an example of such matrices if it exists. A_{i, j} denotes the element of matrix A at the i -th row and j -th column. For all i, j (1 \leq i, j \leq N) , A_{i, j} is an integer that satisfies 1 \leq A_{i, j} \leq 2N - 1 . For all k = 1, 2, ..., N , a set consists of 2N - 1 elements from the k -th row or k -th column is \{1, 2, ..., 2N - 1\} . If there are more than one possible matrices, output any of them.

Find the angle exactly between the two given angles θ 1 and θ 2 .Here, the angle exactly between them is defined as ``θ 1 + t', where t' is the one with the smallest absolute value among t that satisfies θ 1 + t = θ 2 − t, as shown in the figure below.

GPA is an abbreviation for "Galaxy Point of Aizu" and takes a real number from 0 to 4.GPA Rock, Paper, Scissors is a game played by two people.After each player makes a move of their choice, such as goo, chop, or par, with the signal ``Rock, Paper, Scissors, Pon,'' the person with the highest GPA becomes the winner, and the person with the lowest GPA becomes the loser.In case of a tie, it will be a draw.Given the GPA data of N people, output the points won by each player in a round-robin match.However, the winning points will be 3 for a win, 1 for a draw, and 0 for a loss.A round-robin game is a method in which all participants play against each other exactly once.

Mr. ukuku1333 is a clumsy person, so when he expanded the product of the linear expressions of x, he couldn't understand the original linear expression.Given the n-degree polynomial of x, factorize it into the form of a product of the linear expressions of the original x.The nth degree polynomial of x is given by the following BNF.<Polynomial> := <Term> | <Polynomial>+<Term> | <Polynomial>−<Term> <Term> := x^<Exponent> | <Coefficient>x^<Exponent> | <Coefficient>x | <Constant> <Exponent> := [2-5] <Coefficient> := [1-9][0-9]* <Constant> := [1-9][0-9]* If the exponent and coefficient are omitted, it is assumed to be 1.

Gaccho loses motivation as the final exam approaches, and often starts missing school.There are N days left until the final exam.Gaccho's motivation on day i is X i, and the motivation required to go to school on day i is Y i.Gaccho-kun goes to school only on days when X i ≥ Y i.Worried about Gaccho, Haji decided to encourage Gaccho and let him go to school as much as possible.Haji-kun initially has an encouragement power of P.If Haji-kun spends encouragement power t (t is any real number greater than or equal to 0) to encourage Gaccho-kun on day i, Gaccho-kun's motivation on day i increases by t, and Haji-kun's encouragement power decreases by t.Furthermore, when i +1 ≤ N, Gaccho-kun's motivation X i+1 on day i +1 changes to max(0, X i+1 − t ).Haji-kun cannot encourage beyond his own ability to encourage.Find the maximum number of days Gaccho goes to school when Haji uses the optimal method of encouragement.

A company has $n$ computers on its network and $m$ communication cables connecting them.Computers are distinguished by identifiers from $0$ to $n - 1$, and communication cables also have identifiers from $0$ to $m - 1$.Currently, any two different computers in this company can communicate with each other through several communication cables, but there is no guarantee that there will be only one communication path.The problem with this network is that there are too many communication cables that can become tangled.So you decide to remove some communication cables so that there is only one communication path for any given communication.Communication cable $i$ connects computers $a_i$ and $b_i$ in both directions, and its length is $c_i$.Also, the effort required for you to remove the communication cable $i$ is $d_i$.Before you start work, you determine the ratio of the amount of effort required to complete the task to the sum of the lengths of communication cables to be removed, and estimate the minimum amount of discomfort.However, if none of them can be removed, the discomfort is $0$.

The great devil Sakanya was attacked by his natural enemy, a cat, again today.I can't afford to be hit all the time, so I bought a secret weapon.This secret weapon creates giant rocks that block the cat's path of movement and prevent it from approaching you.Now, Sakanya and a cat are in a closed rectangular interval surrounded by cells (0,0), ( n −1,0), ( n −1, m −1), (0, m −1).There is a cat in square (0,0) and Sakanya in square (n −1, m −1).Cats can move to adjacent squares vertically, horizontally, or horizontally, but cannot go outside the area.Some squares cannot be entered due to holes or obstacles.Sakanya can prevent cats from entering a certain square by creating one rock in that square.However, rocks cannot be generated in squares (0,0) and (n −1, m −1).Find the minimum number of rocks required to block the path from square (0,0) to square (n −1, m −1).

N people, assigned IDs from 1 to N, are trying to sit on N chairs, assigned numbers from 1 to N.Person with ID i wants to sit on chair p i.N people line up in a line in descending order of ID, and the person at the top of the line takes the following actions.When this action is repeated until all people sit on the chairs, output the ID of the person sitting on each chair.

Gaccho is obsessed with the popular game Zombie Hunter.This game has five types of armor for the head, torso, arms, waist, and legs, and the player character (PC) controlled by Gaccho can equip up to one armor for each part of the body.In addition, each piece of armor has a set weight and defense power, and if the total weight of the armor a PC is equipped with is between A and B or less, and the total defense power is also between A and B or less, that PC can activate the skill.Gaccho has N pieces of armor.I would like you to investigate whether the PC can activate skills by choosing the appropriate armor to equip.

You bought a tree-shaped lighting device called a tree light.This luminaire has n contacts, each numbered from 0 to n−1.Each contact consists of a light bulb that can express 10 levels of brightness and a device to switch the light bulb's status.Initially, the brightness of the bulbs at all contacts is 0.There is also a cable line between the contacts.All contacts are connected by n−1 cable lines and are suspended with contact 0 at the top.Here, a set of contacts connected to contact i downward from contact i via zero or more cable lines is called a subtree with contact i as the root.You may take one of the following actions regarding this light fixture:count(r, x, y): Count the number of bulbs whose brightness is greater than or equal to x and less than or equal to y among the bulbs of the contacts included in the subtree rooted at the contact r.change(r, x, y) : Changes the brightness of all bulbs whose brightness is exactly x among the bulbs of the contacts included in the subtree rooted at the contact r to y.Since q actions are given, each time count(r, x, y) is given, print the number of light bulbs at that time.

There are N familiars and M demons.The distance between familiar i and devil j is H i j.I want each demon to have one familiar.Which familiar will visit which demon will be assigned according to the following conditions.Print the maximum difference in the number of visiting demons per familiar on the first line, and the maximum distance between the devil and the familiar on the second line.

Rafi had nothing to do during his vacation and had a sudden urge to eat donuts, so he decided to go to the store and buy some donuts.There is one donut shop in each town, and all donut shops are closed on odd-numbered days.Lahui has his own preferences when it comes to donuts, so the level of satisfaction he gets will vary depending on the store.Therefore, Rahui decided to act optimally to obtain as much satisfaction as possible.In the world Rahui lives in, there are n cities, each assigned a number from 0 to n -1, connected by m roads.Each road is one-way, and Rahui can move from town a i to town b i.Initially, Rahui is in town 0 on even-numbered days.Rahui does not stay in the city, but travels to another city by crossing one street a day.On the day Rahui arrives in town i, if the store is open, he buys one donut and has satisfaction level c i.You can visit the same town as many times as you like, but you can only shop at a store once.Find the maximum total satisfaction when Rahui acts optimally until he can no longer obtain satisfaction.

There are N sets of integers.Each of these sets is assigned a number from 1 to N.The number of elements in the i-th set is K i, and the j-th smallest integer among the elements in the set is a i,j.Taro was to choose any number of sets from among these.Taro thought it would be boring to just choose randomly, so he decided to choose at least three sets.In addition, we decided to choose such that the product of "the number of elements in the union of the selected sets" and "the number of elements in the intersection of the selected sets" is as large as possible.When Taro optimally selects a set, find the maximum value of the product of "the number of elements in the union of the selected sets" and "the number of elements in the intersection of the selected sets".

There are N vacant lots on a two-dimensional plane.Each vacant lot is assigned a number from 1 to N.Every open space is so small that it can be considered a point.The i-th vacant lot exists at (x i , y i ).Taro chooses exactly K out of these N vacant lots and decides to build buildings on those vacant lots.However, Taro didn't think it would be interesting to build multiple buildings too close to each other, so he decided to choose vacant lots so that the Euclidean distance between them was always 2 or more.Create a program that outputs possible combinations of vacant lots that Taro would choose.If there are multiple combinations, output the smallest one in lexicographical order.However, no matter how you choose K vacant lots, if the Euclidean distance of any two vacant lots is smaller than 2, output -1 instead.

Gaccho-kun has a favorite watch.One day, the minute hand of the clock came off and I lost it somewhere.However, Gaccho wants to continue using the watch and wants to be able to read the time using only the hour hand.Output the time (hour h, minute m) for the hour hand information θ.The clock is a so-called analog clock, in which the numbers 1 to 12 are arranged at equal intervals in ascending order clockwise.

NEET lives on the world line for a total of 360 days, ending on the 30th of every month for 1 year and 12 months.In that world, N consecutive holidays with the same dates were applied to people all over the world every year.Holiday i is V i consecutive days starting on D i day of M i month.Since you are a NEET, you have every day off, regardless of holidays.One day, NEET decided to go out for a rare occasion, but he doesn't like crowds, so he doesn't want to go out as much as possible on days when it's crowded due to the holidays.Therefore, NEET calculates the degree of congestion for each day using the following method and tries to find the day with the least congestion.The numerical value representing the degree of influence of a certain date x by consecutive holidays i is S i if date x is included in consecutive holidays i, otherwise max ( 0, S i − min (number of days from x to the first day of consecutive holidays i, number of days from the last day of consecutive holidays i to x) ) The congestion degree of a certain date x is NPlease output the lowest congestion degree in a year, which is the greatest degree of influence among the degrees of influence from individual holidays.However, consecutive holidays i may span multiple years.Also, the dates of consecutive holidays may overlap.

Taro has N character strings, each of length L.Taro loves palindromes, so he would like to create a palindrome as long as possible by selecting some of the N strings and arranging them in any order.Find the longest palindrome that Taro can make.If there are multiple, output the smallest one in lexicographical order.If you cannot create a palindrome no matter how you choose and arrange them, print a blank line.

$N \times There is a grid consisting of N$ squares.Initially all squares are white.Increase the number of black squares by following the steps below.Choose a white square among the even-numbered squares from the top and the even-numbered squares from the left.The selected square will turn black.Furthermore, the adjacent white squares in each of the vertical and horizontal directions of that square also change to black in a chain reaction.This chain continues until there are no white squares in that direction.(Example shown below) □□□□□□□□□ □□□□□□□□□ □□□□□□□□□□□□□□□□□□ □□□□□□□□□ □□□□□□□□□ □□□□□□□□□□□□□□□□□□ □□□□□□□□□ ↓Choose 4th from the top, 6th from the left □□□□□■□□□□□□□□■□□□ □□□□□■□□□ ■■■■■■■■■ □□□□□■□□□ □□□□□■□□□□ □■■■■■■□□□ □■□□□■□□□ ■■■■■■■■■ □□□□□■□□□ □□□□□■□□□□ □■■■■■□□□ □■□□□■□□□ ■■■■■■■■■ □□□□□■□■□ □□□□□■■■■ □□□□□■□■□□□□□□■□■□ □□□□□■□■□ You want to create a grid where the color of the $i$th square from the top and the $j$th square from the left is $A_{i,j}$.Find the minimum number of times to select a square.

Gaccho-kun is trying to play the piano.On a piano, the keys on the right side produce higher notes.Gaccho has a sheet of music and plays according to it.Musical scores include notes that indicate which keys to play at a certain time in chronological order.There are no chords (sounds produced when multiple keys are pressed) in this score, and we will not consider the length of the notes.Gaccho-kun follows the following rules when playing the piano.Play with only one hand Play one key with one finger The first note can be played with any finger When playing a note higher than the previous note, use the finger that is one or more to the right of the finger that played the previous note When playing a note lower than the previous note, use the finger that is one or more to the left of the finger that played the previous note When playing a note that is the same pitch as the previous note, use the same finger that played the previous noteGiven the musical score that Gaccho-kun uses, find the minimum number of fingers required on the player's arm to play the musical score according to the rules.However, note that more than 6 fingers may be required.
Given an integer n, print the smallest m such that nCm (the number of combinations for selecting m from n different items) is an even number.

Tomorrow is the day of the Maiz Elementary School field trip.Gaccho, a student at Maiz Elementary School, was so excited that he realized he had forgotten to buy snacks for tomorrow.Gaccho wants to be particular about sweets in order to enjoy his excursion to the fullest.Gaccho receives $X$ yen in pocket money from his mother and goes to buy some sweets.However, according to the rules of the elementary school, the total amount of sweets that can be brought on a field trip is limited to $Y$ yen, and if it exceeds $Y$ yen, all the sweets will be taken away by the teacher.There are $N$ towns in the school district where Gaccho lives, and each town has one candy store.Each town is assigned a number from 1 to $N$, and Gaccho lives in town 1.Each candy shop sells several types of sweets, and the price of each candy and the level of satisfaction that Gaccho-kun receives with each purchase are determined.However, the number of each candy in stock is limited.Also, since Gaccho uses public transportation when moving between two towns, it costs $d_{i,j}$ yen to go directly from town $i$ to town $j$.At first, Gaccho-kun is in town 1.Gaccho-kun goes to buy sweets so that the sum of the total cost of transportation and the total price of the sweets he bought is within $X$ yen, and the total price of the sweets he bought is within $Y$ yen.You must arrive at Town 1 at the end.Gaccho wants to make sure that the total satisfaction level of the sweets he buys is as high as possible.Find the total satisfaction level when Gaccho takes the optimal action.

There are $N$ arithmetic progressions with number of terms $L$.The first term of the $i$th arithmetic progression is $a_i$ and the common difference is $d_i$.Arrange the first number sequence, the second number sequence, ..., the $N$th number sequence in order from the first number sequence, and let the resulting number sequence be $A$.Then, when creating a new sequence $B$ by selecting an arbitrary interval of length $K$ from $A$, maximize the sum of the sequence $B$.

Today, the exclusive invitation-only restaurant ``Maiz Restaurant'' finally opened.Only members of this restaurant have the right to invite new members, and initially the only member is the restaurant owner, member number 0.Due to its high class quality, Maizu Restaurant will only be open for $N$ days from its opening day.During this period, each member will invite one new friend to become a member.A member number $i$ is assigned to the member invited on the $i$ day, and the member visits the store every day after that, including the day of the invitation.There are only two-person tables in this restaurant, so members eat in pairs whenever possible.The owner also participates in the meal, so other members can pair up with him.However, the members are all shy and only want to pair up with friends who have invited them, or friends who have been invited by them.If each member is unable to pair up with a friend, they eat alone.For each day of $N$, find how many pairs are formed when friends form pairs to maximize the number of pairs.

The cluster of large-scale cluster † type supercomputers (commonly known as "SAKURA") at Maiz University consists of $N$ computers (nodes) and $M$ physical communication channels that can communicate with each other.Additionally, each node is assigned an identification number from 1 to $N$.The use of SAKURA will now be allowed not only to university personnel, but also to companies within the prefecture.Accordingly, it is necessary to inspect each node that makes up SAKURA.However, SAKURA's usage schedule is filled several months into the future, so the entire system cannot be stopped even during inspections.Therefore, we inspect each node one by one every day.The inspection is performed for $N$ days, and node $i$ is inspected on the $i$ day.At this time, disconnect the node to be inspected and the communication path directly connected to that node from the SAKURA cluster.As a result, the entire SAKURA cluster will be divided into one or more clusters, and one of them will be operated in place of SAKURA.Naturally, the node under inspection cannot be operated.If the cluster is divided into multiple clusters, only the cluster with the highest overall performance value will be operated.The "total performance value" of each cluster is the sum of the "single performance values" set for each node composing it.Since you are given the number of nodes that make up SAKURA, the number of communication paths, and the "single performance value" set for each node, output the "total performance value" of the cluster operated during the $i$ day inspection.†: A cluster is a system that combines one or more computers into a unit.Here, it is assumed to be a set of nodes connected by physical communication paths.

Chocolate company Chinor Choco decided to build n new stores.Each store manager has two options for where they would like to build their store, and they build at either location.Tinol Chocolate sells m types of chocolate, each manufactured in a different factory.All stores sell all types of chocolate.Chinor Choco only owns one truck for transporting chocolate, and one truck can only transport enough chocolate for one store.Therefore, trucks have to visit all the factories when moving from one store to another.Find the maximum travel distance when the stores are arranged so that the maximum shortest travel distance when moving from one store to another is the minimum.Multiple stores and factories can exist in the same location.

Two sequences $A$ and $B$ of length $N$ are given.Initially, the $i$ item of the sequence $A$ is $a_i$, and the $i$ item of the sequence $B$ is $b_i$.You will be given a total of $Q$ statements of the following format, so write a program that processes them in the given order.Each statement is represented by three integers $x,y,z$.Set the value of $y$ item of sequence $A$ to $z$.(When $x=1$) Set the value of $y$ item of sequence $B$ to $z$.(When $x=2$) Find and report the minimum value among the $z$ items from the $y$ items of the sequence $A$.(When $x=3$) Find and report the minimum value among the $z$ items from the $y$ items of the sequence $B$.(When $x=4$) Change the sequence $A$ to be exactly the same as the sequence $B$.(When $x=5$) Change the sequence $B$ so that it is exactly the same as the sequence $A$.(When $x=6$)

$N \times There is a grid consisting of N$ squares.Initially all squares are white.Increase the number of black squares by following the steps below.Choose a white square among the even-numbered squares from the top and the even-numbered squares from the left.The selected square will turn black.Furthermore, the adjacent white squares in each of the vertical and horizontal directions of that square also change to black in a chain reaction.This chain continues until there are no white squares in that direction.(Example shown below) □□□□□□□□□ □□□□□□□□□ □□□□□□□□□□□□□□□□□□ □□□□□□□□□ □□□□□□□□□ □□□□□□□□□□□□□□□□□□ □□□□□□□□□ ↓Choose 4th from the top, 6th from the left □□□□□■□□□□□□□□■□□□ □□□□□■□□□ ■■■■■■■■■ □□□□□■□□□ □□□□□■□□□□ □■■■■■■□□□ □■□□□■□□□ ■■■■■■■■■ □□□□□■□□□ □□□□□■□□□□ □■■■■■□□□ □■□□□■□□□ ■■■■■■■■■ □□□□□■□■□ □□□□□■■■■ □□□□□■□■□□□□□□■□■□ □□□□□■□■□ You want to create a grid where the color of the $i$th square from the top and the $j$th square from the left is $A_{i,j}$.Determine whether it is possible to make it.If possible, find the location and order of the squares to be chosen.

In the year 1333, Dr. Ushishi, the greatest scientist in human history, developed an artificial intelligence with the ID ai1333 in order to pass on his wisdom to future generations.For the next 100 years, ai1333 brought great benefits to humanity, but on the 100th anniversary of its birth, ID created a new artificial intelligence for ai13333 as its successor, and permanently stopped its functions.Every 100 years since then, artificial intelligence has left a successor with an ID that starts with 'ai1333' and has a '3' concatenated at the end.Since the number of years $x$ that has passed since 1333 is given as input, output the ID of the artificial intelligence created in that year.However, $x$ is guaranteed to be a non-negative integer multiple of 100.

Let $f(x)$ be the sum of each digit when a non-negative integer $x$ is expressed in binary.Given a positive integer $N$, output the largest one among $f(0)$,$f(1)$,...,$f(N)$.Example of calculating the function $f(5)$: When 5 is expressed as a binary number, it is 101, and the sum of each digit is 1+0+1=2. Therefore, $f(5) = 2$.Note: https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/binary system

There are $N$ balls, and each ball has a fixed color and value.There are $C$ types of ball colors from $1$ to $C$, and an upper limit on the number of balls that can be selected for each color is determined.Maximize the total value obtained when selecting at most $M$ balls in total.

Strings $s$,$t$ are given.First, let the string set $A=${$s$},$B=\phi$.At this time, I would like to perform as many of the following operations as possible.The subsequences of "abac" are { "" , "a" , "b" , "c" , "aa" ,"ab" , "ac" , "ba" , "bc" , "aac" , "aba" , "abc" , "bac" , "abac" }."a" is a substring created by extracting the first or third character of "abac"."ac" is a substring created by extracting the first and fourth characters or the third and fourth characters of "abac".

There are $N$ streetlights on a two-dimensional square of $W \times H$.Gaccho wants to start from $(1,1)$ and go to $(W,H)$.Gaccho is afraid of dark places, so he only wants to walk in spaces that are brightly lit by street lights.Initially, all streetlights only illuminate the square in which they are located.Therefore, Gaccho decided to set a cost $r_i$ for his favorite streetlight $i$.Note that there may be streetlights for which no cost is set.By consuming the cost $r_i$ of a streetlight $i$, it is possible to brighten an area within $r_i$ in Manhattan distance around the streetlight.However, the cost is a positive integer.Gaccho-kun can move to an adjacent square in any direction (up, down, left, or right).Gaccho decided to set the total value of $r_i$ to be the minimum.Find the total value at that time.The Manhattan distance between two points $(a,b)$ and $(c,d)$ is expressed as $|a−c|$+$|b−d|$.

There are $N$ Amidakuji with 3 vertical lines.A good Amidakuji is one that ends on the starting line no matter which line it starts from.You can select one or more Amidakuji and connect them vertically in any order.If you can make a good Amidakuji, output "yes", otherwise output "no".The $i$th Amidakuji has $w_i$ horizontal lines.$a_{i,j}$ represents whether the $j$th horizontal bar from the top of Amidakuji $i$ extends to the left or right from the central vertical line; if $a_{i,j}$ is 0, it is extending to the left, and if it is 1, it is extending to the right.

Given $N$ pairs of non-negative integers $(a_i, b_i)$ and non-negative integers $A$, $B$.I would like to perform one of the following operations as many times as possible.$|a_i - b_i| \leq A$ or $B \leq |a_i - b_i| \leq 2A$ Extract and delete element $i$ that satisfies $|(a_i + a_j) - (b_i + b_j)| \leq A$ or $B \leq |(a_i + a_j) - (b_i + b_j)| \leq 2A$Extract and delete the pair of element $i$ and element $j$ ($i \neq j$) that satisfies

There is a string $S$ of length $N$.All characters in $S$ are numbers between 0 and 9.When you create a sequence of terms $\frac{N\times(N+1)}{2}$ by enumerating all the numbers made from all substrings of $S$, find the $H$th smallest value in the sequence.

Great Demon King Megumin is thinking of defeating the $N$ heroes living on earth.Megumin can cast explosion magic up to $M$ times.Explosion magic is a magic that annihilates heroes within a radius of $r$ centered on arbitrary coordinates.The hero is so thin that there is no need to consider his size.All $M$ explosion spells must be cast in the same radius.I decided to use the minimum radius magic necessary to annihilate the heroes.Minimize the radius of explosion magic.

A sequence $A$ of length $H$ and a sequence $B$ of length $W$ are given.A matrix $C$ consisting of $H$ rows and $W$ columns is defined as follows.$ C_{i,j} = A_i \times B_j (1 \leq i \leq H, 1 \leq j \leq W) $ Process the following four types of queries a total of $Q$ times.Query 1 Add $v$ to $1$ $a$ $b$ $v$ $ A_i ( a \leq i \leq b)b, c \leq j \leq d) Outputs the minimum value of $ and its number Query 4 $4$ $a$ $b$ $c$ $d$ $ C_{i,j} ( a \leq i \leq b, c \leq j \leq d) Outputs the maximum value of $ and its number Refer to sample input/output for details.

Frollo the penguin is on an infinitely large square of ice ($sx$,$sy$).There is a hole in the square ($tx$,$ty$) through which you can enter the water.There are $n$ pieces of ice on the ice, each in square ($x_i$,$y_i$).Frollo can move in four directions: up, down, left, and right.Ice is slippery, so when you move, you keep moving until you hit a block of ice.When it hits a block of ice, it stops one square before the block of ice.You can enter the hole by passing through the holes.Frollo can stop halfway by pushing with all his might just once.It hurts when you hit a block of ice, so you want to minimize the number of times you hit it as much as possible.Find the minimum number of collisions before moving to the square with the hole ($tx$, $ty$).If you cannot reach it, print $-$1.

Ai-chan has a tree $T$ consisting of $N$ vertices and $N-1$ edges.Each vertex has a number from $1$ to $N$ and a weight of a positive integer.Answer the following $Q$ queries in order.Since $1 \le a_i,b_i \le N$($a_i \ne b_i$) is given, output the weight of the connected component with the maximum weight among several connected components created by removing vertices on the $a_i$-$b_i$ path of $T$ and edges connected to them.If there is no such connected component, output 0.However, the weight of a connected component is defined as the sum of the weights of vertices included in the connected component.

There are $n$ bombs on a two-dimensional plane.Each bomb is assigned a number from 1 to $n$, and the $i$th bomb is located at the coordinates $(x_i,y_i)$.It is known that all bombs have the same Manhattan distance from the origin.When the $i$th bomb explodes, bombs located at coordinates within $r_i$ of Manhattan from coordinates $(x_i,y_i)$ also explode in a chain reaction.For each $n$ bomb, find the number of bombs that will remain without exploding if only that bomb is ignited.

The popular video posting site "ZouTube" is currently in the midst of an unprecedented "virtual ZouTuber" boom.Among them, one that has been attracting particular attention recently is the junior virtual ZouTuber "Aizumarim" (commonly known as Azurim).If you're a big fan of Azurim, you're going to send her a "special chat" on Azurim's live stream today.``Special Chat'' is a ``feature that allows viewers to present points to streamers'' provided by ZouTube.Viewers can spend either $500$, $1000$, $5000$, or $10000$ points for each $1 special chat, and present the same amount of points to the broadcaster.Given the total amount of points you currently hold, use those points to find the maximum amount of points you can present to Azurim.You can use the special chat as many times as you like, as long as the amount of points you have is not less than the amount of points you have to spend.

There are two teams, Team UKU and Team Ushi.Initially, Team UKU has $N$ people and Team Ushi has $M$ people.Team UKU and Team Ushi decided to play a game called "U&U."In "U&U", two teams have a common score, and the objective is to cooperate with each other to minimize the common score.In "U&U", everyone's physical strength is initially $2$ and the common score is $0$, and the game progresses as follows.Given the number of people in Team UKU and the number of Team Ushi, find the minimum possible common score at the end of the "U&U" game.

A sequence $A=${$a_{1},a_{2},a_{3},...,a_{n}$} of length $N$ is given.It is assumed that $a_{i}$ ($i=1,2,3,...,n$) is initialized with $i$.Process the following two types of queries a total of $Q$ times.Outputs the value of the $k$th element from the beginning of the sequence $A$.Swap two number sequences using the $k$ and $k+1$th boundaries from the beginning of the number sequence $A$.

There is a grid of $R \times C$ squares with $(0, 0)$ on the top left and $(R-1, C-1)$ on the bottom right.When you are at a certain square ($e$, $f$), you can move from there to $(e+1, f)$, $(e-1, f)$, $(e, f+1)$, $(e, f-1)$, $(e, 0)$, $(e, C-1)$, $(0, f)$, $(R-1, f)$ at a cost of $1$.However, you cannot go outside the grid.When moving from the square $(a_i, a_j)$ to $(b_i, b_j)$, calculate the cost of the shortest path and the remainder when the total number of shortest path combinations is divided by $10^9 + 7$.However, the combinations of routes shall be differentiated depending on the method of movement.For example, when the current location is $(100, 1)$, to get to $(100, 0)$ at the shortest cost, you can go by either $(e, f-1)$ or $(e, 0)$, which counts as $2$ ways.

You brought a rectangular, flat donut with a width of $W$ and a height of $H$, without a hole, for ACPC.Place this donut so that the center of the donut overlaps the coordinate $(0,0)$ on the $2$-dimensional plane, and the side of length H and the $y$ axis are parallel.On day ACPC$1$, you ate a donut located in a range of $w$ horizontally and $h$ vertically centered on the coordinates ($x$,$y$).You want to equalize the amount eaten on day $2$ and day $3$. You would like to divide the donuts by a straight line passing through coordinates $(0,0)$ so that the sum of the areas of donuts on one side of the line is equal to the sum of the areas of donuts on the other side.Find the slope of one such straight line.

An undirected graph with $N$ vertices and $M$ edges is given.Each vertex is assigned a different number from $1$ to $N$.You want to leave vertex $S$ at time $0$ and move to vertex $G$.Parameters $a, b$ are set for each edge, and we know that if we start from a vertex on one side of the edge at time $t$, we will arrive at the vertex on the other side at time $t+ceil\left(\cfrac{b}{t+a}\right)$.Here, $ceil(x)$ represents the smallest integer greater than or equal to $x$.Also, each vertex can consume any non-negative integer extra time.In other words, when you are at a vertex at time $t$, you can choose any non-negative integer $k$ and wait until time $t+k$.If you aim to become the fastest and most powerful algorithm, you want to find the minimum amount of time it takes to travel from vertex $S$ to vertex $G$.

A sequence $X$ of length $N$ is given.In the initial state, all elements of $X$ are $0$.In addition, $M$ integer pairs $(A_i, B_i)$ are given.Perform the following operations for each pair and output the final sequence $X$.

You have been asked by a certain company to create a program.The company has $N$ jobs, and each job is numbered from $1$ to $N$.Work $i$ has $M_i$ prerequisite jobs $X_{i,j}$, and if work $i$ is performed before prerequisite work $X_{i,j}$, work $i$ will suffer a great loss.I would like you to find the number of jobs that would incur losses if all jobs were performed in the order that minimizes the number of jobs that incur losses.

Here is a list of strings.Let's take a break and play shiritori.Shiritori is performed according to the following rules.After this, steps 2 and 3 will be repeated alternately.Now, suppose we continue this process in step 2 until there are no more strings to choose from the list.At this time, I would like to list all possible characters that can be the "last character" of the last selected character string.

The country of Aizu has $N$ islands and $N-1$ bridges, and each island is assigned a number from $1$ to $N$.The $i$th bridge connects island $u_i$ and island $v_i$ in both directions, and islanders can use the bridge to go back and forth between the islands.Also, there is no way to travel between the islands other than by bridge.You can reach any island by crossing several bridges.Currently, there are $X_i$ residents on island $i$.In order to spread the burden on each island's environment, Aizu decided to have some of its citizens move to another island.In order to move a person living on island $a$ to island $b$, it costs the same amount as the distance between island $a$ and island $b$.However, the distance between island $a$ and island $b$ is defined by the minimum number of bridges that need to be crossed to go from island $a$ to island $b$.I would like people to move to any $2$ island so that the absolute value of the difference in the number of residents between those islands is less than $1$.At this time, find the minimum value of the total required cost.

The center coordinates of a convex polygon consisting of $N$ vertices and $M$ circles are given.The radius of every circle is $r$.I would like to find the smallest real number $r$ that satisfies the following conditions.Condition: Every point inside the convex polygon is enclosed in at least one circle.

The day before yesterday, your friend Tosa asked you a favor.She wants to decorate a tree with an arithmetic progression and use it for a birthday party, but the tree is too big and she can't do it alone.Therefore, they would like you, who is an excellent programmer, to write a program to efficiently decorate a tree with an arithmetic progression.You and Tosa are old friends.You decided to accept Tousa's request.We are given a tree of $N$ nodes to decorate.The nodes of the tree are numbered from $0$ to $N-1$.Initially, the points of all nodes in the tree are $0$.The program must support the following two queries.$0$. Finds the sum of the points written in the nodes in the specified section of the tree.$1$. Adds an arithmetic progression to the nodes in the specified interval of the tree.Let's decorate the tree beautifully to celebrate Tosa's birthday.

One day, Kawabayashi-kun is about to eat lunch at the school cafeteria.The school cafeteria has three daily lunch menus: A lunch, B lunch, and C lunch.Kawabayashi-kun is a glutton, so he wants to eat one of each of the three daily lunch menu items.However, Kawabayashi-kun decided to stick to one lunch menu and eat two different lunch menus in order to minimize the total calorie intake due to his health concerns.Given the calories of Lunch A, Lunch B, and Lunch C on a certain day, find the lunch menu that Mr. Kawabayashi will endure.

If you bring exactly $a$ empty milk bottles, you can exchange them for a new $b$ bottle of milk.Mr. Kawabayashi, who initially has $x$ bottles of milk, can drink up to how many bottles of milk?Print the remainder after dividing by $1000000007$.

Den, the phone number for Uknikia Co., Ltd., enters very long phone numbers into his phone every day.One day, Mr. Den was so tired that he came up with a surprising idea.``Wouldn't it be a little easier if we rearranged the buttons on the phone?!'' A phone has squares divided at equal intervals of $3 \times 3$, and each of the nine squares has one button numbered 1 to 9 that can be rearranged.When dialing a phone number, Deng can do two things using only one hand:Move your index finger so that it touches one of the buttons adjacent to the button you are currently touching.Press the button that your index finger is touching.Initially, the index finger can be placed to touch any button from 1 to 9.Mr. Deng considers the most efficient positioning to be the one that minimizes the number of movements of the index finger from the time the first button is pressed until the last button is pressed.Now, here is a customer's phone number of length $N$.Considering only the phone numbers of your customers, what kind of arrangement would be most efficient?Create that arrangement by rearranging the buttons.

There are $n$ rectangles on the $xy$ plane with the horizontal axis $x$ and the vertical axis $y$.The $i$th rectangle has a height of $h_i$ and a width of $1$, and its vertices are point $(i-1, 0)$, point $(i-1, h_i)$, point $(i, h_i)$, and point $(i, 0)$.You choose $n$ positive integers $y_1, y_2, ... y_n$ and perform the following $n$ operations.(1) In the first operation, draw a line segment connecting point $(0, 1)$ and point $(1, y_1)$.(2) In the $i$th operation $(2 \le i \le n)$, draw a line segment connecting the point $(i-1, y_{i-1})$ and the point $(i, y_i)$.For the $i$th operation, $S_i$ is determined as follows.Let A be the endpoint of the line drawn in the $i$th operation with smaller $x$ coordinates, and B be the endpoint of larger $x$ coordinates.Also, let point $(i-1, 0)$ be C, point $(i-1, h_i)$ be D, point $(i, h_i)$ be E, and point $(i, 0)$ be F.Let $S_i$ be the area of ​​the non-common part of trapezoid ABFC and rectangle DEFC.Five examples are shown below.$S_i$ in each example is the total area of ​​the region including the star mark.Find $y_1, y_2, ... y_n$ that minimizes $S = S_1 + S_2 + … + S_n$, and output the minimum value of $S$.

Kotatsugame is a turtle with a kotatsu shell.As soon as Kotatsugame woke up, he was trapped in a stage divided into grid-like sections.This stage has only one exit.A compartment can be a road, a fence, or a bomb, and Kotatsugame can enter the road or bomb compartment by moving up, down, left, or right.It cannot be moved diagonally.Also, you cannot pass through fenced sections.There are endless sections of road outside the stage, but you can't pass through them because there are crocodiles, the turtle's natural enemy.Kotatsugame wanted to go home quickly and sleep (sleep), so he decided to head for the exit of this stage.Kotatsugame, who can freely manipulate the infrared light of a kotatsu, has the ability to turn eight sections around itself into roads at the same time.However, if any of the eight compartments and one of the compartments you are in are bombed, the explosion will cause Kotatsugame to go up in flames, and you will not be able to use Nene or even the famous SNS "Kametta", so you will not be able to activate your ability.Illustrate an example.The section where the illustrations are not placed and the section where the kotatsu turtle is located are the sections of the road.The 8 sections around Kotatsugame refer to the orange sections.In the image on the left, the bomb area is not included in the 8 areas surrounding Kotatsugame and the area where you are, so you can activate the ability.However, in the image on the right, the ability cannot be activated because one of the 8 surrounding areas and the area you are in contains a bomb area.The cost to move is $A$ per block, and the cost to activate the ability is $B$ per time.Note that this is not the cost per wall turned into a road.The starting point and stage exit are roads.Please output the minimum value of the sum of the cost of moving from the starting point to the exit of the stage and the cost of activating the ability.However, if you cannot reach the exit of the stage, print "INF" instead.

A convex polygon consisting of $N$ vertices is given.When considering an equilateral triangle that includes all the vertices of the convex polygon, find the minimum length of one side of the equilateral triangle.

At the all-boys school Bit-kun attends, a Kippo game is held during lunch break.This school's Kippo game is famous for being a bit unusual.First, prepare $N$ worth of Kippo.The length of the $i$th kippo is $a_i$.The first and second players take turns eating Kippo.If you eat a really long piece of kippo all at once, it will stick in your throat, so eat as much as you like, no more than $1$ and less than $D$.Because they are clumsy, they can only eat kippo with integer lengths.When one of the Kippo is eaten first, the person who eats it all loses.When both players act optimally, output "First" if the first player wins, and "Second" if the second player wins.

There are $N$ ghosts lined up in a straight line from left to right.Initially, the $i$th ghost from the left faces to the left if $U_i$ is 'L', and to the right if $U_i$ is 'R'.They are very cowardly and don't want to see scary ghosts if possible.You can instruct each ghost to turn around.It is scary to look back, so when the $i$th ghost looks back once, the fear level of $A_i$ occurs.The ghost looking to the left turns around and looks to the right.The ghost looking to the right turns around and looks to the left.For $ i < j $, if the $i$th ghost is facing right and the $j$th ghost is facing left, we define that the $i$th ghost and $j$th ghost are facing each other.The following $M$ constraints are given.The $S_i$th ghost is afraid of the $T_i$th ghost.In the final state, when the $S_i$th ghost and the $T_i$th ghost are facing each other, a fear level of $B_i$ occurs.Find the minimum value of the total fear level that occurs when the optimal instructions are given.

There is a stack of $n$ cards, and the $i$th card from the bottom has $i$ written on it.This bundle is shuffled $q$ times as defined below.When looking at the stack from below, consider a stack $x$ made by stacking odd-numbered cards on top of each other, and a stack $y$ made by stacking even-numbered cards on top of each other.Then perform any of the $2$ operations below.Operation $0$: Place bundle $y$ on bundle $x$ to make it into $1$ bundles.Operation $1$: Place bundle $x$ on bundle $y$ to make it into $1$ bundles.For example, when considering shuffling $1$ times for a stack of $6$ cards (123456 from the bottom), the stack obtained by performing operation $0$ is 246135 135246 from the bottom, and the stack obtained by performing operation $1$ is 135246 246135 from the bottom.(14:52 correction) After shuffling $q$ times, I want the card with $k$ written on it to be the $d$th card from the bottom of the stack.If possible, output $q$ shuffle operations in order; if not, output $-1$.

A dolphin who lives in an aquarium receives a reward when he jumps through $N$ rings.Iruka-kun flies from the coordinate $(0,0)$ and lands on the water at $(T,0)$.The trajectory of the jump is a parabola.The $i$th ring is determined to have passed through when the jump trajectory intersects with the line segment connecting $(X_i,L_i)$ and $(X_i,H_i)$.Jumping $1$ times requires as much physical strength as the initial speed.Iruka-kun can jump as many times as necessary.Assuming the gravitational acceleration vector as $(0,-1)$, find the minimum total amount of physical strength required for Iruka to pass through all the rings.However, assume that friction and air resistance are negligible.

There is a tree of $N$ vertices.Each vertex is assigned a number from 1 to $N$.Gacho and Kawabayashi decided to play a position game using this tree.The game starts with Gacho and Kawabayashi at different peaks.Gacho takes turns moving the vertices over and over, and the first one to be unable to move is the loser.How to move: When you are at vertex $x$, move to one of the vertices that no one has visited yet that is directly connected to vertex $x$ by an edge.If no such vertex exists, it cannot be moved.Let the vertex where Mr. Gacho is first located be vertex $A$, and the vertex where Mr. Kawabayashi is first located is vertex $B$.If Gacho and Kawabayashi each do their best, find the number of possible combinations of vertices $A$ and $B$ that will result in Gacho winning.

Mr. Aizu decided to play a game using a prime number $P$, a set of natural numbers $G$, and a natural number $A$.First, Aizu writes $1$ on a piece of paper.Then, perform the following series of operations as many times as you like.Select one element from $G$.Let this be $g$.Write the product of the number written on the paper in hand and $g$ on a new piece of paper.Erase the number originally written on the paper.If the number written on the paper in hand is divided by $P$ and $A$ is equal, Aizu wins, otherwise he loses.Determine whether Aizu can win given $P$, $G$, and $A$.

A string $S$ of length $N$ is given.Process the following query $Q$ times.Let query $S[L: R]$ be a string consisting of characters $L$ to $R$ (inclusive) of $S$.Consider expressing $ S[L: R] $ as $AXBXCX(1 \leq |A|,|B|,|C|,|X|)$ using appropriate strings $A,B,C,X$, and output the length of the longest such $X$.However, if no such $X$ exists, 0 is output instead.

Alice and Bob are competing in a 50m dash.However, in this world, the one with the higher AOJ rating is better, so the one with the higher AOJ rating wins.If there is no AOJ rating for either race, there is no comparison, so I have no choice but to compete based on 50m dash time.In this case, the one with the shortest time wins.If the AOJ ratings are the same, it's a draw; if the races have to be competed on 50m times, if the times are the same, it's a draw.

It looks like a shady magician will be showing off his magic.``Now, think of one favorite integer.'' You decide to visualize your number year in your head.The magician has posed the query $N$ times.Each query is one of the following:For each query, process the query against the number you have in mind and visualize the resulting value in your mind.``Now, let me guess the first number you can think of.'' Those magicians are trying to say something.But I seem to have forgotten what to say.A shady magician is staring at you, drenched in sweat.I have no choice, so I'll tell you what to say.It seems that the magician intended to say the following at the end.``If you add $A$ to the integer you are currently thinking of and divide by $B$, it is the first integer you thought of.'' From the constraint, there is only one set of integers $(A,B)$ that satisfies the above condition, no matter how many integers you have in mind.Find the integer pair $(A,B)$.However, $B$ must not be $0$.

Satake hates things that are curved.For example, I don't like pencils, chopsticks, or houses that are curved, and I don't like actions such as turning right or left.Now, Mr. Satake, who lives on the XY plane, was asked to run an errand to do some shopping at $N$ stores $(X_{1},Y_{1}),(X_{2},Y_{2}), \ldots , (X_{N},Y_{N})$.You start from your house at coordinates $(0, 0)$, facing the direction $(1, 0)$, shop at all the stores, and then return home.You can visit the shops in any order.As an errand boy, Satake can perform any of the following actions as many times as he wants.As I said earlier, Satake hates bending.To prepare yourself, find the minimum value of the sum of angles that Satake rotates in action 2.For example, if you rotate $90^{\circ}$ clockwise and then $90^{\circ}$ counterclockwise, the sum of the angles is $180^{\circ}$.

You're trapped in a cave!Perhaps the wall collapsed or something, but there were a lot of rocks blocking the way to the only exit.But then you realize there's a bomb vending machine right next to you.Furthermore, it seems that some "blasters" have also fallen in the cave.If you use these two tools, you will be able to escape to the exit.There are two types of items that can be used inside the cave: bombs and blasters.A bomb is an item that can be used to destroy one square of rock.A blaster is an item that can be used to destroy all rocks in a straight line in front of you.When you destroy the target rock, it will shatter and become a "floor".Bombs and blasters are single-use items, and each item can only be used once.Also, using a blaster will not destroy other blasters.I happened to have a drone on hand and was able to fly it and see what the cave was like.You will be given a field of caves.The fields are given in ASCII art as shown below._### ##B# B##_ The field is a rectangle consisting of $H \times W$ squares with a width of $H$ squares on the top and bottom and $W$ squares on the left and right.The square in $i$ row and $j$ column is expressed as $(i,j)$.Each square has either a floor, a wall, or a blaster.The blaster square shows exactly one blaster on the floor.The outside of the field is surrounded by a wall that cannot be destroyed by bombs or blasters.You cannot go outside the field.You can take the following actions any number of times.Proceed to any floor in an adjacent space.Get any blaster in an adjacent square.After getting the blaster, the square where you got the blaster becomes the floor.Destroy a rock in an adjacent square using one bomb.From the current square, face up, down, left, or right and use one blaster in your possession.Here, cells $(i,j)$ and $(k,l)$ are adjacent if $|i-k|+|j-l|=1$.Blasters are light enough that you can pick up as many as you want while on the move.However, please note that as stated in the item description, you can only use the blaster as many times as you have acquired.Initially, we are at square $(1,1)$.Escape means reaching the square $(H,W)$.You realize that if you buy a bunch of bombs from a bomb vending machine, you can escape without any problems.Fortunately, the bomb vending machine has $10^{100}$ in stock, which seems like enough to escape.However, bombs are very expensive, so you don't want to buy too many.Based on the condition of the cave I obtained, I wanted to know the minimum number of bombs I would need to buy in order to escape.

We are given a permutation $P = \{ P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_N \} $ of length $N$ and an integer $K$.Determine whether the permutation $P$ can be made monotonically increasing by repeating the following operation an arbitrary number of times at least $0$.Choose an integer $x \ (0 \le x \le N-K)$.Cyclically right shift $ P_{x+1}, \ldots, P_{x+K} $ However, cyclic right shift of the subsequence $U=U_1, \ldots, U_M$ means changing $U=U_1, \ldots, U_M$ to $U=U_M, U_1, \ldots, U_{M-1}$.

There are $N$ bus stops numbered from $1$ to $N$ arranged clockwise in a ring.Adjacent bus stops are connected by roads.For each $i \ (1 \le i \le N)$, the length of the road directly connecting bus stop $i$ and bus stop $i+1$ is $d_i$ meters.However, bus stop $N+1$ represents bus stop $1$.There are $M$ buses.The $j \ (1 \le j \le M)$-th bus travels clockwise when $c_j='R'$, and counterclockwise when $c_j='L'$.Also, the bus departs from the bus stop $b_j$ at time $0$ and takes $t_j$ seconds to travel $1$ meters.In this problem, the bus continues to run forever.It takes no time to get on and off the bus.At a bus stop, you can get on and off the bus the moment a bus passes that stop.You cannot get on and off the bus at any other bus stop.You can board as many buses as you want.Process the following query a total of $Q$ times.Find the minimum time required to leave bus stop $x_k$ at time $0$ and travel to bus stop $y_k$ using only the bus.

One day, mo3tthi and tubuann decided to play a game using magic pockets and biscuits.Now we have $K$ pockets numbered $1,2, \ldots ,K$.The $i$th pocket has a capacity of $M_i$ and initially contains $N_i$ biscuits.mo3tthi and tubuann will take turns performing the following sequence, starting with mo3tthi.Choose one pocket.Perform one of the following operations only once.However, if the number of biscuits in the pocket selected as a result of the operation exceeds the capacity of the pocket, the operation cannot be performed.I caressed the pocket I had chosen.Magically increases the number of biscuits in the selected pocket by $1$.Tap the selected pocket.The number of biscuits in the selected pocket will be multiplied by $2$ by magic.The game ends when you can no longer perform any operations, the person who can no longer perform the operations loses, and the person who cannot perform the operations wins.As a friend of mo3tthi, mo3tthi asked you if you could determine whether you can win this game in advance.For mo3tthi, please write a program to determine whether mo3tthi can definitely win this game.

There are $M$ types of characters.Use them to create a string of length $N$.How many character strings are there in which the number of types of characters used is at least $K$?Find the remainder after dividing by $998244353$.Here, two character strings of length $N$ are defined as being different as follows.If two strings are $S = S_1S_2 \ldots S_N$, $T = T_1T_2 \ldots T_N$, there exists $i$ $(1 \leq i \leq N)$ such that $S_i \neq T_i$.

There are coins with heads and tails and dice with numbers from $1$ to $N$.Gacho-kun decided to play the following game using these.The game starts with a score of $0$ and proceeds as follows.When a coin is tossed, there is a probability of $A\%$ that it will come up heads, and a probability of $(100-A)\%$ that it will come up tails.Also, when you roll a die, each eye appears with equal probability.In this case, find the probability that Gacho can clear the game in one game.When the required probability is expressed as $\frac{P}{Q}$ using relatively prime integers $P and Q$, output the integer $R$ between $0$ and $998244352$ such that $R \times Q \equiv P\bmod 998244353$.Under the constraints of this problem, such $R$ always exists uniquely.

$N$ people are standing in a line from left to right.The string $S$ is popular among them.Each person is happy when the following conditions are met; otherwise, they are not happy.Process the following $2$ types of queries a total of $Q$ times, for which $|S|$ characters or more have been passed so far, and the most recent $|S|$ characters, ordered from oldest to newest, match $S$.Query 1 $1$ $l$ $r$ $c$ Send the string $c$ character by character from the left to the people included in the interval $[l, r]$.Query 2 $2$ $l$ $r$ Find the number of happy people included in the interval $[l, r]$.However, the interval $[l, r]$ represents the people from the $l$th to the $r$th from the left.

Moritaoy is a mysterious university student who lives inside a computer.The plot where moritaoy lives is represented as a two-dimensional space $S= \{ (p,q) \in \mathbb{Z}^2 | 0 \leq p \lt 2^H, 0 \leq q \lt 2^W \} $ with dimensions $2^H$ vertically and $2^W$ horizontally.moritaoy has a two-dimensional vector whose elements are some non-negative integers, and moves in this space using addition.When moritaoy is at $(a,b)$ and moves according to the vector $(i,j)$, he specifically indicates the following behavior.Move to $(a+i,b+j)$.However, if such a point does not exist in $S$, it will overflow and move to the point $(k,l) \in S$ in the space where moritaoy lives, which satisfies $a+i \equiv k \bmod 2^H , b+j \equiv l \bmod 2^W$.Below is moritaoy's daily schedule.moritaoy decided to play with various plans.For each $(a,b)$, find the total amount of rest time in a day for all of the schedules in which Moritaoy is at $(a,b)$ at the end of the day.However, two schedules being different means that the number of times the two schedules move in a day is different, or that there is a certain $i$ and the vectors used for the $i$th move are different.

There are $N$ planets in the galaxy and $M$ secret routes called "bridges."The planets are numbered $1,2, \ldots N$, and the bridges are numbered $1,2, \ldots M$.The position of each planet is expressed as a point in real three-dimensional space, and planet $p$ is located at $(x_p,y_p,z_p)$.The $i$th bridge is a secret route from planet $u_i$ to $v_i$.Note that it is not possible to directly travel from planet $v_i$ to $u_i$ using the $i$th bridge.The distance between planets $p$ and $q$ is defined as follows.$\mathrm{d} (p,q) = |x_p - x_q| + |y_p - y_q| + |z_p - z_q|$ Let $S_p$ be the set of planets that can be reached from planet $p$ via $0$ or more bridges.For each $p$, find $\displaystyle \max_{s \in S_p} \mathrm{d} (p,s)$.However, the rebels are constantly targeted by the forces of the vicious Galactic Empire, so they are unable to travel between planets by any route other than bridges.

Define a function $f$ whose argument starts with $1$ and whose argument is a sequence of finite length.$\displaystyle f(\{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n\}) = \sum_{i=1}^n {a_i}^i$ Given the sequence $X = \{ x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N \}$ of length $N$, $f(X')$ for all subsequences $X'$ except the empty sequenceFind the sum, divide it by $998244353$, and print the remainder.However, the subscripts of the subsequences shall be renumbered sequentially starting from $1$ while maintaining the relative order in the original sequence.Furthermore, even if two subsequences are the same as a sequence, if they are taken out at different positions, they are counted separately.

A mysterious plant of $X$ [cm] grows in a certain place. This plant has the following mysterious properties. However, let us assume that this plant does not grow to a negative length. Specifically, when it grows from $C$ [cm] to $D$ [cm] $(C + D \lt 0)$, it stops growing when it reaches $0$ [cm].You say one word from "nobiro", "tidime", or "karero". Find the length [cm] of the plant after $N$ days. The input is given in the following format. $X\ A\ B$ $N $ $S_1$ $S_2$ $\vdots$ $S_N$ Output the length of the plant after $N$ days. Also output a newline at the end.

Cryptography is popular at Xryuseix's school. Xryuseix, who lives in a grid-like town, came up with a new code to determine the meeting place. The ciphertext consists of a string $S$ of $N$ characters, and the $S_i$ characters determine the direction of movement from the current location. The direction of movement is as follows.I wanted to tell yryuseiy the meeting place for the date in ciphertext, but I realized that the ciphertext was redundant. For example, let's say there is a ciphertext called ``ANA.'' This is a ciphertext that moves $1$ squares north, $1$ squares south, and then $1$ squares north. This is equivalent to a ciphertext that moves $1$ squares north, so it can be simply written as ``ANA'' = ``A.''Mr. xryuseix wanted to make the ciphertext concise so that yryuseiy would not have to take a detour. So, you took the place of Mr. xryuseix and wrote a program to make the ciphertext concise. Note that ``make the ciphertext concise'' means ``create the shortest ciphertext that goes to the same destination as the original ciphertext.'' The input is given in the following format. $N$ $S$ $1$Line 1 shows the length of the ciphertext after conciseness, $2$ Output the ciphertext in line 2. If there are multiple possible ciphertexts for the answer, you can output any of them. Also, output a newline at the end of each line. Note that a newline is required after the concise ciphertext (empty string).

There are $N$ propositions, each named $1, 2, \cdots,N$. In addition, $M$ pieces of information about the proposition are given. The $i$th information is given in the form ``$a_i$ $b_i$'', which means that if $a_i$ then $b_i$. (``If'' is a logical inclusion and the transitive law holds) For each proposition $i$Output all propositions that are equivalent to $i$ in ascending order. However, proposition $i$ and proposition $i$ are always equivalent. Proposition $X$ and proposition $Y$ are equivalent if "$X$ then $Y$" and "$Y$ then $X$". The input is given in the following format. $N\ M$ $a_1\ b_1$ $a_2\ b_2$$\vdots$ $a_M\ b_M$ On line $i$, output all propositions that are equivalent to proposition $i$ in ascending order, separated by spaces. Also, output a newline at the end of each line.

There are $N$ islands, numbered from $1$ to $N$. Each island has $N-1$ bridges, and any $2$ island can travel to each other by crossing several bridges. Each bridge has a durability, and the durability of the $i$th bridge at the time the input is given is $w_i$. Each island has $1$ of treasure.Yebi-kun, who is currently on island $S$, wants to take all the treasures to the museum on island $E$. Since Yebi-kun has ✝Magic Power✝, every time he visits island $v$, the durability of all bridges from $v$ decreases by $T$. The durability of the bridge is $0$.When the following happens, the bridge will collapse and you will no longer be able to cross it. Will Yebi-kun be able to deliver all the treasures to the museum?However, since yebi is strong, he can carry as many treasures as he wants at the same time. The input is given in the following format: $N\ T\ S\ E$ $a_1\ b_1\ w_1$ : : $a_{N-1}\ b_{N-1}\ w_{N-1}$ If you can deliver all the treasures to the museum, output "Yes", otherwise output "No". Also, output a new line at the end.Yebi-kun's magic power is too weak to reduce the durability of the bridge.

AOR Squid-chan is a Monster Buster. One day, while walking on the street, he came across a sleeping monster. AOR Squid-chan, who has a strong fighting spirit, decided to strike the monster awake. However, AOR Squid-chan's current attack power is $0$, and he cannot make a proper attack as it is. AOR Squid-chan, who is devoted to being a Monster Buster, was actually entrusted with a special whistle by his master.When a specific song is played with this flute, the attack power increases for a certain period of time. A trained AOR squid can play $N$ songs. The $i$th song takes $R_i$ seconds to play, and after the performance ends, the attack power increases by $A_i$. The effect of this performance ends $T_i$ seconds after the performance ends, and the attack power returns to the one before the performance. Also,AOR Squid-chan can perform overlapping performances. If you finish playing the same song during the performance duration, the attack power will increase by $W_i$ instead of $A_i$. You can perform overlapping as many times as you like, but the effect time will not be extended. Therefore, when the effect applied at the beginning of the currently active $i$th song ends, all the overlapping performance effects will end. Output the maximum value of AOR Squid-chan's attack power. Furthermore, no matter how many times you play, monsters will not wake up,AOR Squid-chan can attack in $0.5$ seconds. The input is given in the following format. $N$ $R_1\ A_1\ W_1\ T_1$ $\vdots$ $R_N\ A_N\ W_N\ T_N$ Output the maximum value of AOR Squid-chan's attack power. Also, output a line feed at the end.

$M$ entry/exit records for a certain room are given to $N$ people numbered from $1$ to $N$.The $i$th entry/exit record consists of $3$ integers $A_i\ B_i\ C_i$, which indicates the following.For the $i$th person ($1 \leq i \leq N$), choose the person with whom you spent the longest time in the room by time $T$.The input is given in the following format. $N\ M\ T$ $A_1\ B_1\ C_1$ … $A_M\ B_M\ C_M$ The $i$ line outputs the person with whom the $i$th person spent the longest time in the room by time $T$.However, if there are multiple people you have spent the most time with other than yourself, output the person with the lowest number.Also, print a newline at the end of each line.The $3$th person spends $0$ with both the $1$th person and the $2$th person, so the smallest value, $1$, is output.

You are given $N$ strings $S_i$. Find the maximum number of "AORs" included in the string obtained by concatenating $S_i$ in any order. Input is given in the following format. $N$ $S_1$ ... $S_N$ Output the maximum number of "AORs" included in the string obtained by concatenating $S_i$ in any order. Also,Also output a newline at the end.
It's a reactive problem.
Write a program which prints $n$-th fibonacci number for a given integer $n$. The $n$-th fibonacci number is defined by the following recursive formula:

For given two sequences $X$ and $Y$, a sequence $Z$ is a common subsequence of $X$ and $Y$ if $Z$ is a subsequence of both $X$ and $Y$. For example, if $X = \{a,b,c,b,d,a,b\}$ and $Y = \{b,d,c,a,b,a\}$, the sequence $\{b,c,a\}$ is a common subsequence of both $X$ and $Y$. On the other hand, the sequence $\{b,c,a\}$ is not a longest common subsequence (LCS) of $X$ and $Y$, since it has length 3 and the sequence $\{b,c,b,a\}$, which is also common to both $X$ and $Y$, has length 4. The sequence $\{b,c,b,a\}$ is an LCS of $X$ and $Y$, since there is no common subsequence of length 5 or greater. Write a program which finds the length of LCS of given two sequences $X$ and $Y$. The sequence consists of alphabetical characters.

Optimal Binary Search Tree is a binary search tree constructed from $n$ keys and $n+1$ dummy keys so as to minimize the expected value of cost for a search operation. We are given a sequence $K = {k_1, k_2, ..., k_n}$ of $n$ distinct keys in sorted order $(k_1 < k_2 < ... < k_n)$, and we wish to construct a binary search tree. For each key $k_i$, we have a probability $p_i$ that a search will be for $k_i$. Some searches may be for values not in $K$, and so we also have $n+1$ dummy keys $d_0, d_1, d_2, ..., d_n$ representing values not in $K$. The dummy keys $d_i (0 \le i \le n)$ are defined as follows: For each dummy key $d_i$, we have a probability $q_i$ that a search will correspond to $d_i$. For $p_i (1 \le i \le n)$ and $q_i (0 \le i \le n)$, we have \[ \sum_{i=1}^n p_i + \sum_{i=0}^n q_i = 1 \] Then the expected cost of a search in a binary search tree $T$ is \[ E_T = \sum_{i=1}^n (depth_T(k_i) + 1) \cdot p_i + \sum_{i=0}^n (depth_T(d_i) + 1) \cdot q_i \] where $depth_T(v)$ is the depth of node $v$ in $T$. For a given set of probabilities, our goal is to construct a binary search tree whose expected search cost is smallest. We call such a tree an optimal binary search tree . Each key $k_i$ is an internal node and each dummy key $d_i$ is a leaf. For example, the following figure shows the optimal binary search tree obtained from Sample Input 1. Write a program which calculates the expected value of a search operation on the optimal binary search tree obtained by given $p_i$ that a search will be for $k_i$ and $q_i$ that a search will correspond to $d_i$.

There are two standard ways to represent a graph $G = (V, E)$, where $V$ is a set of vertices and $E$ is a set of edges; Adjacency list representation and Adjacency matrix representation. An adjacency-list representation consists of an array $Adj[|V|]$ of $|V|$ lists, one for each vertex in $V$. For each $u \in V$, the adjacency list $Adj[u]$ contains all vertices $v$ such that there is an edge $(u, v) \in E$. That is, $Adj[u]$ consists of all vertices adjacent to $u$ in $G$. An adjacency-matrix representation consists of $|V| \times |V|$ matrix $A = a_{ij}$ such that $a_{ij} = 1$ if $(i, j) \in E$, $a_{ij} = 0$ otherwise. Write a program which reads a directed graph $G$ represented by the adjacency list, and prints its adjacency-matrix representation. $G$ consists of $n\; (=|V|)$ vertices identified by their IDs $1, 2,.., n$ respectively.

Depth-first search (DFS) follows the strategy to search ”deeper” in the graph whenever possible. In DFS, edges are recursively explored out of the most recently discovered vertex $v$ that still has unexplored edges leaving it. When all of $v$'s edges have been explored, the search ”backtracks” to explore edges leaving the vertex from which $v$ was discovered. This process continues until all the vertices that are reachable from the original source vertex have been discovered. If any undiscovered vertices remain, then one of them is selected as a new source and the search is repeated from that source. Quick sort is based on the Divide-and-conquer approach. In QuickSort(A, p, r), first, a procedure Partition(A, p, r) divides an array A[p..r] into two subarrays A[p..q-1] and A[q+1..r] such that each element of A[p..q-1] is less than or equal to A[q], which is, inturn, less than or equal to each element of A[q+1..r]. It also computes the index q. In the conquer processes, the two subarrays A[p..q-1] and A[q+1..r] are sorted by recursive calls of QuickSort(A, p, q-1) and QuickSort(A, q+1, r). A nonleaf node is an internal node The number of children of a node x in a rooted tree T is called the degree of x . The length of the path from the root r to a node x is the depth of x in T . Here, the given tree consists of n nodes and evey node has a unique ID from 0 to n -1. Fig. 2 shows an example of rooted trees where ID of each node is indicated by a number in a circle (node). The example corresponds to the first sample input.

Search trees are data structures that support dynamic set operations including insert, search, delete and so on. Thus a search tree can be used both as a dictionary and as a priority queue. Binary search tree is one of fundamental search trees. The keys in a binary search tree are always stored in such a way as to satisfy the following binary search tree property: The following figure shows an example of the binary search tree. For example, keys of nodes which belong to the left sub-tree of the node containing 80 are less than or equal to 80, and keys of nodes which belong to the right sub-tree are more than or equal to 80. The binary search tree property allows us to print out all the keys in the tree in sorted order by an inorder tree walk. A binary search tree should be implemented in such a way that the binary search tree property continues to hold after modifications by insertions and deletions. In addition to a key field and satellite data, each node contains fields left , right , and p that point to the nodes corresponding to its left child, its right child, and its parent, respectively. The insert procedure is passed a node $z$ for which $z.key = v$, $z.left = NIL$, and $z.right = NIL$. The procedure modifies $T$ and some of the fields of $z$ in such a way that $z$ is inserted into an appropriate position in the tree. A binary search tree can be unbalanced depending on features of data. For example, if we insert $n$ elements into a binary search tree in ascending order, the tree become a list, leading to long search times. One of strategies is to randomly shuffle the elements to be inserted. However, we should consider to maintain the balanced binary tree where different operations can be performed one by one depending on requirement. We can maintain the balanced binary search tree by assigning a priority randomly selected to each node and by ordering nodes based on the following properties. Here, we assume that all priorities are distinct and also that all keys are distinct. This combination of properties is why the tree is called Treap (tree + heap). An example of Treap is shown in the following figure. Insert To insert a new element into a Treap, first of all, insert a node which a randomly selected priority value is assigned in the same way for ordinal binary search tree. For example, the following figure shows the Treap after a node with key = 6 and priority = 90 is inserted. It is clear that this Treap violates the heap property, so we need to modify the structure of the tree by rotate operations. The rotate operation is to change parent-child relation while maintaing the binary-search-tree property. The rotate operations can be implemented as follows. The following figure shows processes of the rotate operations after the insert operation to maintain the properties. The insert operation with rotate operations can be implemented as follows. Delete To delete a node from the Treap, first of all, the target node should be moved until it becomes a leaf by rotate operations. Then, you can remove the node (the leaf). These processes can be implemented as follows. Write a program which performs the following operations to a Treap $T$ based on the above described algorithm.

A complete binary tree is a binary tree in which every internal node has two children and all leaves have the same depth. A binary tree in which if last level is not completely filled but all nodes (leaves) are pushed across to the left, is also (nearly) a complete binary tree. A binary heap data structure is an array that can be viewed as a nearly complete binary tree as shown in the following figure. Each node of a nearly complete binary tree corresponds to an element of the array that stores the value in the node. An array $A$ that represents a binary heap has the heap size $H$, the number of elements in the heap, and each element of the binary heap is stored into $A[1...H]$ respectively. The root of the tree is $A[1]$, and given the index $i$ of a node, the indices of its parent $parent(i)$, left child $left(i)$, right child $right(i)$ can be computed simply by $\lfloor i / 2 \rfloor$, $2 \times i$ and $2 \times i + 1$ respectively. Write a program which reads a binary heap represented by a nearly complete binary tree, and prints properties of nodes of the binary heap in the following format: node $id$: key = $k$, parent key = $pk$, left key = $lk$, right key = $rk$, $id$, $k$, $pk$, $lk$ and $rk$ represent id (index) of the node, value of the node, value of its parent, value of its left child and value of its right child respectively. Print these properties in this order. If there are no appropriate nodes, print nothing.

A binary heap which satisfies max-heap property is called max-heap. In a max-heap, for every node $i$ other than the root, $A[i] \leq A[parent(i)]$, that is, the value of a node is at most the value of its parent. The largest element in a max-heap is stored at the root, and the subtree rooted at a node contains values no larger than that contained at the node itself. Here is an example of a max-heap. Write a program which reads an array and constructs a max-heap from the array based on the following pseudo code. $maxHeapify(A, i)$ move the value of $A[i]$ down to leaves to make a sub-tree of node $i$ a max-heap. Here, $H$ is the size of the heap. The following procedure buildMaxHeap(A) makes $A$ a max-heap by performing maxHeapify in a bottom-up manner.

A histogram is made of a number of contiguous bars, which have same width. For a given histogram with $N$ bars which have a width of 1 and a height of $h_i$ = $h_1, h_2, ... , h_N$ respectively, find the area of the largest rectangular area.

You have 4 bags A, B, C and D each of which includes N coins (there are totally 4N coins). Values of the coins in each bag are a i , b i , c i and d i respectively. You have N coins each of which has a value a i . Find the number of combinations that result when you choose K different coins in such a way that the total value of the coins is greater than or equal to L and less than or equal to R .

Note that you must print this count modulo $10^9+7$.
